Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the distinction between a legal estate and a legal interest in land?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
A legal estate gives the holder ownership of the land itself for a defined period. Only two legal estates exist under s.1

1. LPA 1925: the fee simple absolute in possession (freehold) and the term of years absolute (leasehold). A legal interest is a right over another's land (e.g. an easement or mortgage). Legal interests are listed exhaustively in s.1
2. LPA 1925.

Any interest not on that list can only exist in equity.

⚠️ The numerus clausus principle means parties cannot create new legal estates or interests by agreement.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: LEGAL ESTATE · LEGAL INTEREST · S.1 LPA 1925 · NUMERUS CLAUSUS · FEE SIMPLE · TERM OF YEARS

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What are the only two legal estates in land under the LPA 1925?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Under s.1

1. LPA 1925, the only two legal estates are:
1. The fee simple absolute in possession — freehold ownership of indefinite duration, and
2. The term of years absolute — a leasehold for a fixed or periodic term.

All other estates (e.g. life estates, fee tail) were abolished as legal estates by the 1925 legislation and can now only exist as equitable interests behind a trust.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: FEE SIMPLE ABSOLUTE · TERM OF YEARS ABSOLUTE · S.1(1) LPA 1925 · LIFE ESTATE EQUITABLE ONLY · 1925 LEGISLATION

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What legal interests in land are recognised under s.1(2) LPA 1925?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Section 1(2) LPA 1925 lists five categories of legal interest:

1. easements, rights and privileges for an interest equivalent to a fee simple or term of years
2. rentcharges in possession for an equivalent period
3. charges by way of legal mortgage
4. land tax and other similar charges
5. rights of entry annexed to a legal lease or rentcharge.

Any interest not within this list is necessarily equitable only.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: S.1(2) LPA 1925 · EASEMENTS · LEGAL MORTGAGE · RENTCHARGE · RIGHTS OF ENTRY · CLOSED LIST

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the significance of the distinction between legal and equitable interests when a purchaser buys land?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer: Legal interests bind the world — they are enforceable against all persons who acquire the land, including purchasers, regardless of notice. Equitable interests are more vulnerable: in unregistered land, they bind all except a bona fide purchaser of a legal estate for value without notice (the 'equity's darling' doctrine). In registered land, equitable interests must be protected by entry of a notice or restriction on the register, or they will not bind a registered disponee for valuable consideration (s.29 LRA 2002), unless they qualify as an overriding interest.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: LEGAL BINDS WORLD · EQUITABLE VULNERABLE · BONA FIDE PURCHASER · S.29 LRA 2002 · OVERRIDING INTEREST · NOTICE

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What does 'in possession' mean in the definition of the fee simple absolute in possession?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer: 'In possession' under s.205(1)(xix) LPA 1925 means the holder is currently entitled to receive rents and profits or to physical possession. It does not require the owner to be physically present — a landlord who has let the property retains a fee simple in possession because they receive rent. A future interest (e.g. a remainder) does not satisfy this requirement and so cannot exist as a legal estate.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: IN POSSESSION · S.205 LPA 1925 · RENTS AND PROFITS · LANDLORD QUALIFIES · REMAINDER NOT LEGAL

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: Can parties create new types of property rights in land by agreement?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer: No. The numerus clausus principle confines proprietary rights in land to a closed list. Parties can only create rights recognised by law as capable of being proprietary. A right that does not fit within a recognised category (e.g. a novel restriction that goes beyond a freehold covenant) will be treated as a personal contractual right only, binding the original parties but not successors in title. This prevents the fragmentation of land ownership with idiosyncratic burdens: Hill v Tupper [1863].
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: NUMERUS CLAUSUS · CLOSED LIST · NO NEW PROPERTY RIGHTS · PERSONAL NOT PROPRIETARY · HILL V TUPPER

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What formalities are required to create or transfer a legal estate in land?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Under s.52

1. LPA 1925, a legal estate must be created or transferred by deed. A deed requires:
1. writing
2. clear indication it is a deed
3. signature by the grantor
4. attestation by a witness present at signing (s.1 LP(MP)A 1989). Additionally, for registered land, legal title does not pass until registration at HMLR (s.27 LRA 2002) — the disposition takes effect in equity only until registration.

⚠️

**Exception:** a lease of three years or less, taking effect in possession at market rent, may be created without a deed (s.54
2. LPA 1925).
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: DEED · S.52 LPA 1925 · WRITING · SIGNATURE · WITNESS · REGISTRATION · S.54(2) EXCEPTION

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the 'mirror principle' in registered land, and what are its limitations?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer: The mirror principle holds that the register should reflect the complete and accurate picture of title — a purchaser should be able to rely on the register as a full statement of rights affecting the land. In practice the mirror is imperfect: overriding interests bind a registered proprietor without appearing on the register (Sch 1 and Sch 3 LRA 2002). This is the most significant limitation — a purchaser may be bound by interests they could not discover from the register alone.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: MIRROR PRINCIPLE · REGISTER · OVERRIDING INTERESTS · SCH 1 SCH 3 LRA 2002 · IMPERFECT MIRROR

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the difference between an overriding interest under Schedule 1 and Schedule 3 LRA 2002?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Schedule 1 lists overriding interests on first registration — interests that bind the first registered proprietor without entry on the register. Schedule 3 lists overriding interests on registered dispositions — interests that bind a subsequent purchaser without registration. The two lists are similar but not identical.

⚠️

**Key distinction:** legal leases not exceeding 7 years override under both schedules. But an interest of a person in actual occupation overrides under Sch 3 only if the occupation would have been obvious on a reasonably careful inspection, or the disponee had actual knowledge of it (Sch 3, para 2).
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: SCH 1 FIRST REGISTRATION · SCH 3 REGISTERED DISPOSITION · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · INSPECTABILITY · LRA 2002

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: When does an interest of a person in actual occupation override a registered disposition under Schedule 3, paragraph 2 LRA 2002?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Under Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002, an interest of a person in actual occupation overrides a registered disposition if:

1. the person is in actual occupation at the time of the disposition
2. the interest is not excluded by enquiry (i.e. the occupier was not asked and failed to disclose when they reasonably could have); and
3. the occupation would have been obvious on a reasonably careful inspection of the land, OR the disponee had actual knowledge of it.

⚠️ Actual occupation requires a physical presence — a mere entitlement to occupy is insufficient: Williams & Glyn's Bank v Boland [1981].
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: ACTUAL OCCUPATION · SCH 3 PARA 2 · INSPECTABILITY · ENQUIRY · BOLAND · PHYSICAL PRESENCE REQUIRED

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is overreaching, and what conditions must be met for it to operate?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Overreaching is the mechanism by which a purchaser of land takes free of equitable interests under a trust, provided certain conditions are met. The interests are 'overreached' — detached from the land and attached to the purchase money instead. Conditions:

1. the disposition must be by trustees of land (at least two trustees or a trust corporation)
2. capital money must be paid to at least two trustees or a trust corporation (s.2 and s.27 LPA 1925).

⚠️ If capital money is paid to only one trustee, overreaching fails and the beneficiary's interest may bind the purchaser if they are in actual occupation: City of London Building Society v Flegg [1988].
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: OVERREACHING · TWO TRUSTEES · CAPITAL MONEY · S.2 S.27 LPA 1925 · FLEGG · SOLE TRUSTEE FAILS

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: Can overreaching defeat an overriding interest of a person in actual occupation?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Yes — if overreaching operates successfully, it defeats even an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 2, because the beneficiary's interest shifts from the land to the proceeds of sale. The leading case is City of London Building Society v Flegg [1988]: the House of Lords held that where two trustees received the capital money and the conditions of overreaching were met, the beneficiaries' interests (even though they were in actual occupation) were overreached and could not bind the purchaser.

⚠️ Overreaching only works if capital money is paid to two trustees — payment to a sole trustee means overreaching fails.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: OVERREACHING DEFEATS ACTUAL OCCUPATION · FLEGG · TWO TRUSTEES · INTEREST SHIFTS TO PROCEEDS · SCH 3 PARA 2

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What triggers compulsory first registration of title under the LRA 2002?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Section 4 LRA 2002 lists triggers for compulsory first registration, including:

1. transfer of a qualifying estate (freehold or leasehold with more than 7 years to run) for valuable or other consideration, by gift, or pursuant to a court order
2. first legal mortgage of a qualifying estate
3. assignment of a lease with more than 7 years to run.

Voluntary registration is also possible. If first registration is not completed within two months of the triggering event, the legal estate revests in the transferor (s.6 LRA 2002).
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: S.4 LRA 2002 · COMPULSORY REGISTRATION · 7-YEAR LEASE · FIRST MORTGAGE · TWO-MONTH DEADLINE · S.6 REVESTING

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: How are third party rights protected in registered land?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Third party rights in registered land are protected by:

1. Entry of a notice on the charges register — protects most equitable interests (e.g. estate contracts, equitable easements, restrictive covenants). A notice does not guarantee validity but protects priority.
2. Entry of a restriction on the proprietorship register — controls how the registered estate can be dealt with (e.g. requiring two trustees).
3. Overriding interests under Sch 1/Sch 3 — bind without registration.

⚠️ Interests that should be protected by notice but are not will not bind a registered disponee for valuable consideration (s.29 LRA 2002).
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: NOTICE · RESTRICTION · OVERRIDING INTEREST · S.29 LRA 2002 · UNPROTECTED INTEREST · REGISTERED LAND

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the effect of s.29 LRA 2002 on a registered disponee for valuable consideration?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Section 29 LRA 2002 provides that on a registrable disposition for valuable consideration, the disponee takes free of any interest that: (a) is not protected by a notice on the register; and (b) does not qualify as an overriding interest. This is the registered land equivalent of the bona fide purchaser rule. The disponee's title is 'clean' as regards unprotected interests once registration is complete.

⚠️ A disponee who is a volunteer (e.g. a donee) does not benefit from s.29 and takes subject to all prior interests.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: S.29 LRA 2002 · REGISTERED DISPONEE · VALUABLE CONSIDERATION · UNPROTECTED INTERESTS · VOLUNTEER EXCEPTION

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the 'curtain principle' in registered land?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer: The curtain principle means that the details of a trust do not appear on the face of the register — the register shows only the legal proprietors, not the beneficial interests behind the trust. A purchaser dealing with the registered proprietors need not investigate the trust. Beneficial interests are overreached on payment of capital money to two trustees, protecting the purchaser and redirecting the beneficiaries' rights to the proceeds. This principle is implemented through the restriction mechanism — a restriction on the register signals that a disposition requires certain conditions (e.g. two trustees) to overreach.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: CURTAIN PRINCIPLE · TRUST BEHIND REGISTER · OVERREACHING · RESTRICTION · TWO TRUSTEES · BENEFICIAL INTERESTS

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: How are legal interests protected in unregistered land?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
In unregistered land, protection depends on whether the interest is legal or equitable. Legal interests bind the world automatically — no registration needed. Equitable interests must be registered as land charges under the Land Charges Act 1972 to bind a purchaser for money or money's worth of a legal estate. The main registrable classes are: C(i) puisne mortgage; C(iii) general equitable charge; C(iv) estate contract; D(ii) restrictive covenant (post-1925); D(iii) equitable easement; F: spouse's home rights.

⚠️ An unregistered land charge is void against a purchaser for money or money's worth of a legal estate, even if the purchaser had actual notice of it: Midland Bank Trust Co v Green [1981].
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: UNREGISTERED LAND · LCA 1972 · LAND CHARGES · VOID IF UNREGISTERED · ACTUAL NOTICE IRRELEVANT · GREEN

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the doctrine of notice in unregistered land, and when does it still apply?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
In unregistered land, equitable interests not required to be registered as land charges (e.g. beneficial interests under a trust, pre-1926 restrictive covenants) are governed by the doctrine of notice. A bona fide purchaser of a legal estate for value without notice takes free of such interests. Notice may be:

1. actual — subjective knowledge
2. constructive — what the purchaser would have discovered by making reasonable enquiries (Hunt v Luck [1902])
3. imputed — notice acquired by an agent.

⚠️ The doctrine of notice has been largely displaced by the LCA 1972 registration regime for post-1925 equitable interests — it applies only to interests outside the registration system.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: DOCTRINE OF NOTICE · ACTUAL · CONSTRUCTIVE · IMPUTED · BONA FIDE PURCHASER · HUNT V LUCK · LCA 1972 DISPLACES

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the effect of registering a land charge against the wrong name in unregistered land?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
Registration of a land charge must be against the correct full name of the estate owner at the time the charge was created. Registration against an incorrect or abbreviated name is ineffective. In Oak Co-operative Building Society v Blackburn [1968], registration against a common but incorrect version of the name was held not to be valid registration against a purchaser who searched against the correct name. A purchaser who searches against the correct name and finds nothing gets a clear certificate and takes free of the interest, even if it was registered under a variant name.

⚠️ This is a distinction trap — the charge is 'registered' but ineffective.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: WRONG NAME · LAND CHARGE · CLEAR CERTIFICATE · OAK CO-OPERATIVE V BLACKBURN · INEFFECTIVE REGISTRATION

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: What is the 'insurance principle' in registered land, and how does it operate in practice?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
The insurance principle underpins the registered land system: the state guarantees the accuracy of the register and indemnifies those who suffer loss as a result of a mistake. Under Sch 8 LRA 2002, indemnity is available where a person suffers loss by reason of a rectification of the register, or where rectification would be available but is not ordered. The registrar may also pay indemnity for loss suffered due to an error or omission in the register that is not corrected.

⚠️ Indemnity is not available where the loss results from the claimant's own fraud or lack of proper care (Sch 8, para 5).
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: INSURANCE PRINCIPLE · SCH 8 LRA 2002 · STATE INDEMNITY · REGISTER MISTAKE · FRAUD EXCLUDES · RECTIFICATION

Legal and Equitable Interests.front: When can the register be altered or rectified under the LRA 2002, and what is the key distinction between alteration and rectification?
Legal and Equitable Interests.answer:
The register may be altered under Sch 4 LRA 2002 to correct a mistake, bring the register up to date, or give effect to an order of the court or registrar. 'Rectification' is a subset of alteration — it involves correcting a mistake that prejudicially affects a registered proprietor's title.

⚠️

**Key distinction:** rectification against a proprietor in possession requires either the proprietor's consent or proof that it would be unjust not to rectify — there is a presumption in favour of the registered proprietor in possession (Sch 4, para 3(3)). A registered proprietor who is not in possession enjoys no such presumption. Indemnity may be payable where alteration causes loss.
Legal and Equitable Interests.summary_line: ALTERATION · RECTIFICATION · SCH 4 LRA 2002 · PROPRIETOR IN POSSESSION · PRESUMPTION AGAINST RECTIFICATION · INDEMNITY

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: What is a freehold covenant, and why does the question of passing the burden and benefit matter?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer: A freehold covenant is a promise made by a landowner in a deed, either to do something (positive covenant) or to refrain from doing something (restrictive covenant). The original parties are always bound by contract. The question of passing arises when the land changes hands — can a successor in title to the covenantor be bound by the burden? Can a successor in title to the covenantee enforce the benefit? Different rules apply at law and in equity, and the distinction between positive and restrictive covenants is critical.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: FREEHOLD COVENANT · POSITIVE · RESTRICTIVE · BURDEN · BENEFIT · SUCCESSOR IN TITLE · PASSING
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: Does the burden of a freehold covenant pass at common law?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
No. The burden of a freehold covenant does not pass at common law. A successor in title to the covenantor is not bound by the covenant at law, even if they had notice of it: Austerberry v Corporation of Oldham (1885), confirmed by the House of Lords in Rhone v Stephens [1994]. This applies to both positive and restrictive covenants.

⚠️ This is the single most important rule in freehold covenants — the burden never passes at law. Equity provides a partial solution for restrictive covenants only (Tulk v Moxhay).
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: BURDEN NEVER PASSES AT LAW · AUSTERBERRY · RHONE V STEPHENS · POSITIVE AND RESTRICTIVE · TULK V MOXHAY EQUITY ONLY
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: Under what conditions does the burden of a restrictive covenant pass in equity?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
Following Tulk v Moxhay [1848], the burden of a restrictive covenant passes in equity to a successor in title to the covenantor if:

1. the covenant is restrictive (negative) in nature
2. it was made for the benefit of dominant land retained by the covenantee at the time of the covenant
3. it touches and concerns the dominant land
4. the parties intended the burden to run (now presumed under s.79 LPA 1925); and
5. in registered land, it is protected by a notice on the charges register; in unregistered land, it is registered as a D(ii) land charge (post-1925).

⚠️ Positive covenants cannot pass in equity under Tulk v Moxhay.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: TULK V MOXHAY · RESTRICTIVE ONLY · DOMINANT LAND · TOUCHES AND CONCERNS · S.79 LPA · D(II) LAND CHARGE · NOTICE
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: How does the benefit of a freehold covenant pass at common law?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
The benefit of a freehold covenant passes at common law to a successor in title to the covenantee if:

1. the covenant touches and concerns the dominant land (it must affect the land as land, not merely as a personal advantage)
2. the covenantee had a legal estate in the dominant land at the time of the covenant
3. the successor in title has a legal estate in the dominant land; and
4. the benefit was intended to pass (presumed under s.78 LPA 1925).

Smith and Snipes Hall Farm v River Douglas Catchment Board [1949] confirmed s.78 annexes the benefit automatically to each part of the dominant land.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: BENEFIT AT COMMON LAW · TOUCHES AND CONCERNS · S.78 LPA 1925 · LEGAL ESTATE · SMITH AND SNIPES · AUTOMATIC ANNEXATION
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: How does the benefit of a restrictive covenant pass in equity?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
The benefit of a restrictive covenant passes in equity by one of three methods:

1. Annexation — the benefit is permanently attached to the dominant land, either expressly, by s.78 LPA 1925 (Federated Homes v Mill Lodge Properties [1980]), or by building scheme
2. Assignment — the benefit is expressly assigned with each transfer of the dominant land
3. Building scheme — where a developer sells plots on the basis of a common scheme of restrictions, each plot owner can enforce the covenants against all others.

⚠️ Section 78 annexes the benefit automatically to 'each and every part' of the dominant land — a successor to part can enforce.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: BENEFIT IN EQUITY · ANNEXATION · S.78 LPA · FEDERATED HOMES · ASSIGNMENT · BUILDING SCHEME · EACH PART
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: What practical methods exist to make a positive covenant enforceable against a successor in title?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
Since the burden of a positive covenant does not pass at law or equity, practitioners use indirect methods:

1. Chain of indemnity covenants — each buyer covenants with the seller to observe the covenant; enforceable only between immediate parties.
2. Leasehold structure — impose the obligation as a leasehold covenant (burden passes under privity of estate).
3. Reservation of a right of re-entry — triggered on breach.
4. Commonhold — positive obligations are enforceable between unit holders under the Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Act 2002.
5. Halsall v Brizell [1957] — the benefit and burden principle: a person who takes the benefit of a deed must also accept its burdens.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: POSITIVE COVENANT · INDEMNITY CHAIN · LEASEHOLD · COMMONHOLD · HALSALL V BRIZELL · BENEFIT AND BURDEN · INDIRECT METHODS
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: What are the requirements for a building scheme in freehold covenants?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
A building scheme (or scheme of development) allows mutual enforcement of restrictive covenants between all plot owners. Requirements established in Elliston v Reacher [1908] and refined subsequently:

1. a common vendor sold the plots
2. before sale, the vendor laid out the estate in defined plots
3. the covenants were intended to be for the benefit of all plots
4. each buyer knew of this mutual framework when purchasing.

Effect: each plot owner can enforce the covenants against every other plot owner — creating a 'local law' for the estate. Registration of individual covenants is still required for enforcement against successors.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: BUILDING SCHEME · ELLISTON V REACHER · COMMON VENDOR · DEFINED PLOTS · MUTUAL ENFORCEABILITY · LOCAL LAW
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: How may a restrictive covenant be discharged or modified?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
A restrictive covenant may be discharged or modified by:

1. Agreement between the parties with the benefit and burden
2. Unity of seisin — if the same person becomes owner of both dominant and servient land, the covenant is extinguished (though it may revive if the land is later separated)
3. Application to the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) under s.84 LPA 1925, which may discharge or modify a covenant on grounds including: (a) obsolescence; (b) the covenant impedes reasonable use and no practical benefit of substantial value remains; (c) the proposed discharge will not injure those entitled to enforce it; or (d) the parties entitled to the benefit have agreed.

⚠️ The Upper Tribunal has a wide discretion and may award compensation.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: DISCHARGE · MODIFICATION · S.84 LPA 1925 · UPPER TRIBUNAL · OBSOLESCENCE · UNITY OF SEISIN · COMPENSATION
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: What is the 'benefit and burden' principle, and what are its limits?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
The benefit and burden principle (Halsall v Brizell [1957]) holds that a person who takes the benefit of a deed must accept the burdens in that same deed. It has been used to make positive covenants indirectly enforceable: if a successor takes the benefit (e.g. use of communal roads or facilities), they must also accept the corresponding burden (e.g. contribute to maintenance costs).

⚠️

**Limits:** the burden must be directly linked to the benefit taken — a general obligation cannot be imposed through a tenuous connection to a benefit. Confirmed and refined in Thamesmead Town Ltd v Allotey [1998]: the correlation must be direct and the successor must have a genuine choice to take or reject the benefit.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: BENEFIT AND BURDEN · HALSALL V BRIZELL · DIRECT CORRELATION · GENUINE CHOICE · THAMESMEAD · POSITIVE COVENANT
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.front: What is the difference between a restrictive covenant and an equitable easement in terms of creation and enforcement?
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
Both are equitable interests, but they differ in nature and enforcement. A restrictive covenant is a promise not to do something on the covenantor's land — it is contractual in origin and runs in equity under Tulk v Moxhay. An equitable easement is a right to use another's land (e.g. a right of way) that arises where the formalities for a legal easement are not met (e.g. granted by deed but not substantively registered). In unregistered land: equitable easements must be registered as D(iii) land charges; restrictive covenants (post-1925) as D(ii). In registered land: both are protected by notice.

⚠️ Pre-1926 equitable easements are governed by the doctrine of notice, not land charges registration.
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: RESTRICTIVE COVENANT · EQUITABLE EASEMENT · D(II) D(III) LAND CHARGES · NOTICE REGISTERED LAND · PRE-1926 NOTICE
Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden.subtopic: Rules on Passing Benefit and Burden

Burden of Restrictive Covenants in Equit.issue: Whether the burden of a restrictive covenant could bind a successor in title to the covenantor who had notice of it.
Burden of Restrictive Covenants in Equit.rule: The burden of a restrictive covenant runs in equity to bind successors in title to the covenantor, provided the covenant is negative in nature, was taken for the benefit of dominant land retained by the covenantee, and the successor had notice of it (now: is properly registered).
Burden of Restrictive Covenants in Equit.application: Tulk sold Leicester Square to Elms, who covenanted to keep it as an open garden. The land passed through several hands to Moxhay, who had notice of the covenant but proposed to build on the square. Tulk sought an injunction. The court held the covenant bound Moxhay in equity.
Burden of Restrictive Covenants in Equit.conclusion: The injunction was granted. Tulk v Moxhay is the foundational authority for the running of restrictive covenant burdens in equity. It established that a person who takes land with notice of a restriction cannot use it inconsistently with that restriction — now codified and refined by the land registration and land charges systems.
Burden of Restrictive Covenants in Equit.summary_line: RESTRICTIVE COVENANT · BURDEN IN EQUITY · NOTICE · DOMINANT LAND · NEGATIVE COVENANT · TULK V MOXHAY
Burden of Restrictive Covenants in Equit.subtopic: Burden of Restrictive Covenants in Equity

Positive Covenants.issue: Whether the burden of a positive covenant to maintain a roof could pass to a successor in title to the covenantor at common law or in equity.
Positive Covenants.rule: The burden of a positive covenant does not run with the land at common law or in equity — a successor in title to the covenantor is not bound by a positive obligation to do something, regardless of notice.
Positive Covenants.application: A house and cottage shared a roof. The owner of the house covenanted to maintain the roof for the benefit of the cottage. On sale of the house, the new owner (Rhone) refused to repair the roof. The House of Lords confirmed that the burden of the positive covenant could not pass to Rhone — Austerberry v Oldham was correct and should not be departed from.
Positive Covenants.conclusion: The burden of the positive covenant did not bind Rhone. The case confirmed that the rule against passing positive covenant burdens at law is absolute — only indirect methods (leasehold structure, benefit and burden, indemnity chains) can achieve a similar result. Law Commission reform has been recommended but not implemented.
Positive Covenants.summary_line: POSITIVE COVENANT · BURDEN DOES NOT PASS · COMMON LAW · EQUITY · AUSTERBERRY CONFIRMED · RHONE V STEPHENS

Positive Covenants.issue: Whether a successor in title could be bound by a positive covenant to contribute to road maintenance where they also took the benefit of the right to use those roads.
Positive Covenants.rule: A person who takes the benefit of a deed must accept the corresponding burdens in that deed — the benefit and burden principle makes a positive covenant indirectly enforceable where the covenantor's successor takes a directly related benefit.
Positive Covenants.application: Purchasers of plots on an estate were granted the right to use roads and sewers, in return for covenanting to contribute to their maintenance. A successor in title sought to use the roads without contributing. The court held they could not take the benefit without accepting the burden.
Positive Covenants.conclusion: The successor was bound by the positive covenant to contribute. Halsall v Brizell is the primary authority for the benefit and burden principle as a mechanism for enforcing positive covenants against successors. The connection between benefit and burden must be direct — confirmed in Thamesmead Town Ltd v Allotey [1998].
Positive Covenants.summary_line: BENEFIT AND BURDEN · POSITIVE COVENANT · SUCCESSOR BOUND · ROADS AND SEWERS · INDIRECT ENFORCEMENT · HALSALL V BRIZELL

Positive Covenants.issue: Whether the benefit and burden principle applied where the benefit taken by the successor was not directly correlated with the burden sought to be imposed.
Positive Covenants.rule: The benefit and burden principle (Halsall v Brizell) applies only where the benefit and burden are directly and clearly correlated — a successor must have a genuine and real choice whether to take the benefit, and the burden must be the direct counterpart of that benefit.
Positive Covenants.application: Allotey, a successor in title, was required to contribute to the maintenance of an estate's communal facilities. The court held the benefit he received (use of the facilities) and the burden (maintenance contributions) were not sufficiently directly linked — the benefit was too general and the burden too specific.
Positive Covenants.conclusion: The positive covenant was not enforceable against Allotey. Thamesmead confirms that the benefit and burden principle has limits — it cannot be used to impose general maintenance obligations through a tenuous link to some incidental benefit. The correlation must be direct and the choice genuine.
Positive Covenants.summary_line: BENEFIT AND BURDEN · DIRECT CORRELATION REQUIRED · POSITIVE COVENANT · LIMITS OF HALSALL · SUCCESSOR · THAMESMEAD

Positive Covenants.issue: Whether the burden of a positive covenant (to maintain a road) passed to a successor in title to the covenantor at common law.
Positive Covenants.rule: The burden of a positive covenant does not run with the land at common law — a successor in title to the covenantor is not bound by a positive obligation to do something, even if they had notice of it.
Positive Covenants.application: A company covenanted to maintain a road for the benefit of adjacent land. The company's successor refused to maintain the road. The Court of Appeal held the burden could not pass at law — the covenant was positive in nature and personal to the original covenantor.
Positive Covenants.conclusion: The successor was not bound. Austerberry is the foundational common law authority for the rule that positive covenant burdens do not run — confirmed by the House of Lords in Rhone v Stephens [1994]. It remains unreformed despite repeated Law Commission recommendations.
Positive Covenants.summary_line: POSITIVE COVENANT · BURDEN DOES NOT PASS · COMMON LAW · AUSTERBERRY · CONFIRMED IN RHONE V STEPHENS

Benefit of Covenants.front: Federated Homes v Mill Lodge Properties [1980]
Benefit of Covenants.issue: Whether s.78 LPA 1925 automatically annexed the benefit of a restrictive covenant to each and every part of the dominant land, so that a successor to part could enforce it.
Benefit of Covenants.rule: Section 78 LPA 1925 annexes the benefit of a restrictive covenant to the land of the covenantee and their successors in title automatically — express annexation words are not required, and the benefit passes to successors of each and every part of the dominant land.
Benefit of Covenants.application: Mill Lodge covenanted to restrict the number of houses built on its land, for the benefit of Federated Homes' retained land. The benefit was not expressly assigned on later transfers of parts of Federated Homes' land. The Court of Appeal held s.78 annexed the benefit automatically to each part — a successor to part could enforce.
Benefit of Covenants.conclusion: The benefit of the covenant passed to the successor. Federated Homes dramatically expanded the operation of s.78, making express annexation words largely redundant. It has been criticised for effectively imposing annexation regardless of the parties' intentions, but remains good law.
Benefit of Covenants.summary_line: BENEFIT OF COVENANT · S.78 LPA 1925 · AUTOMATIC ANNEXATION · EACH PART · RESTRICTIVE COVENANT · FEDERATED HOMES

Benefit of Covenants.front: Smith and Snipes Hall Farm v River Douglas Catchment Board [1949]
Benefit of Covenants.issue: Whether the benefit of a covenant to maintain river banks passed automatically to a successor in title of the covenantee under s.78 LPA 1925.
Benefit of Covenants.rule: Section 78 LPA 1925 annexes the benefit of a covenant touching and concerning the land to the covenantee's land automatically — successors in title to the dominant land take the benefit without the need for express assignment.
Benefit of Covenants.application: The River Douglas Catchment Board covenanted with landowners to maintain the banks of a river. The land was later conveyed to Smith and Snipes Hall Farm. The Court of Appeal held the benefit of the covenant passed to the successors under s.78 — no express assignment was needed.
Benefit of Covenants.conclusion: The successors could enforce the covenant. Smith and Snipes Hall Farm is an early authority for the automatic annexation effect of s.78, later expanded in Federated Homes — it confirmed that s.78 passes the benefit to successors without the need for assignment words in each conveyance.
Benefit of Covenants.summary_line: S.78 LPA 1925 · AUTOMATIC ANNEXATION · BENEFIT OF COVENANT · SUCCESSOR IN TITLE · TOUCHES AND CONCERNS · SMITH AND SNIPES

Building Schemes.issue: What conditions must be satisfied to establish a building scheme giving rise to mutual enforceability of restrictive covenants between plot owners.
Building Schemes.rule:
A building scheme requires:

1. a common vendor
2. the vendor divided the land into defined plots before sale
3. the restrictions were intended to benefit all plots in the scheme
4. each purchaser bought on the basis of this mutual framework.
Building Schemes.application: A development was sold in plots with similar restrictive covenants. The court considered whether the covenants were mutually enforceable between plot owners inter se. Parker J set out the four conditions, all of which were satisfied on the facts — a building scheme was established.
Building Schemes.conclusion: The building scheme was upheld, allowing mutual enforcement between plot owners. Elliston v Reacher remains the leading statement of the requirements for a building scheme, though later cases (e.g. Re Dolphin's Conveyance [1970]) have relaxed the strict requirements — particularly the need for a defined layout before sale.
Building Schemes.summary_line: BUILDING SCHEME · MUTUAL ENFORCEABILITY · COMMON VENDOR · DEFINED PLOTS · RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS · ELLISTON V REACHER

Passing Benefit and Burden.front: In 1998, Fiona sold part of her garden to George. George covenanted in the transfer deed to maintain the boundary fence between the two properties. George has now sold his plot to Hannah, who refuses to maintain the fence. Fiona seeks to enforce the covenant against Hannah. Advise Fiona.
Passing Benefit and Burden.answer: The covenant to maintain the fence is a positive covenant — it requires expenditure. The burden of a positive covenant does not pass at common law to a successor in title: Rhone v Stephens [1994], confirming Austerberry v Oldham (1885). The burden cannot pass in equity under Tulk v Moxhay either — that doctrine applies only to restrictive (negative) covenants. Hannah, as George's successor, is not bound by the positive covenant to maintain the fence. Fiona's only recourse is against George under the original contractual obligation (privity of contract) — but George has no ongoing obligation once he has sold the property unless an indemnity covenant chain was created. Fiona cannot enforce the covenant directly against Hannah.
Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: POSITIVE COVENANT · BURDEN NEVER PASSES · RHONE V STEPHENS · NOTICE IRRELEVANT · PERSONAL LIABILITY ONLY

Passing Benefit and Burden.front: In 2005, Ian sold part of his land to Jane. Jane covenanted not to use the land for any purpose other than residential use. Ian retained the neighbouring plot. Ian has since sold his plot to Karl. Jane has now sold her plot to Laura, who wants to open a coffee shop. Karl seeks to enforce the covenant against Laura. Advise Karl.
Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
Karl must establish both the benefit and the burden have passed. Burden (against Laura): the covenant is restrictive and negative in nature. Under Tulk v Moxhay [1848], the burden passes in equity if:

1. the covenant is restrictive
2. made for the benefit of dominant land retained by Ian
3. touches and concerns that land
4. intended to run (presumed under s.79 LPA 1925)
5. protected on the register (a notice on the charges register for registered land).

All conditions appear satisfied — the residential use restriction is negative, benefits the neighbouring plot, and touches and concerns it. Assuming a notice was entered, the burden binds Laura. Benefit (in favour of Karl): under s.78 LPA 1925 (Federated Homes), the benefit is automatically annexed to Ian's land and passes to Karl as successor. Karl can enforce the covenant against Laura provided it is protected on the register.
Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: RESTRICTIVE COVENANT · BURDEN PASSES · TULK V MOXHAY · BENEFIT S.78 · FEDERATED HOMES · NOTICE ON REGISTER

Passing Benefit and Burden.front: Max owns a house on an estate developed in 1995. All plots were sold by the same developer with identical restrictive covenants preventing commercial use. Max's neighbour Nina has begun running a business from her garage. Max seeks to enforce the covenant against Nina. Nina argues Max has no right to enforce as he is not the original covenantee. Advise Max.
Passing Benefit and Burden.answer:
Max may be able to enforce through a building scheme. The conditions for a building scheme (Elliston v Reacher [1908]) are:

1. a common vendor — yes, the same developer sold all plots
2. the estate was divided into defined plots before sale — likely given a 1995 development
3. the covenants were intended to benefit all plots in the scheme
4. each purchaser bought knowing of the mutual framework.

If these conditions are met, a building scheme exists and every plot owner can enforce the covenants against every other plot owner — the original covenantee need not be involved. Max can enforce the no-commercial-use covenant against Nina directly.

⚠️ Max should check whether the covenants are protected by notice on the register for each plot — individual registration is still required for enforcement against successors in registered land.
Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: BUILDING SCHEME · ELLISTON V REACHER · MUTUAL ENFORCEABILITY · PLOT OWNER ENFORCES · NOTICE ON REGISTER

Passing Benefit and Burden.front: Olivia owns a house on a private road shared with three neighbours. When Olivia bought her house, the transfer deed granted her the right to use the road and required her to contribute one quarter of the maintenance costs. Olivia has since sold the house to Peter, who refuses to contribute to road repairs, arguing the positive covenant does not bind him. The road is in disrepair. Advise the neighbours.
Passing Benefit and Burden.answer: Peter is correct that the positive covenant to contribute to maintenance does not pass at common law under Rhone v Stephens. However, the benefit and burden principle from Halsall v Brizell [1957] may apply: a person who takes the benefit of a deed must accept the corresponding burdens. Peter is taking the benefit of the right to use the road — a benefit conferred by the same deed that imposed the maintenance obligation. The burden and benefit are directly correlated (Thamesmead Town v Allotey [1998] requires direct correlation). Peter must choose: either use the road and contribute to maintenance, or relinquish the right to use it. He cannot take the benefit without the burden. The neighbours can therefore enforce the maintenance obligation against Peter through the benefit and burden principle, provided Peter continues to use the road.
Passing Benefit and Burden.summary_line: BENEFIT AND BURDEN · HALSALL V BRIZELL · POSITIVE COVENANT · THAMESMEAD · DIRECT CORRELATION · USE ROAD OR CONTRIBUTE

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: Did Federated Homes v Mill Lodge Properties correctly interpret s.78 LPA 1925, and what are the academic objections?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
In Federated Homes v Mill Lodge Properties [1980], Brightman LJ held that s.78 LPA 1925 automatically annexes the benefit of a restrictive covenant to 'each and every part' of the dominant land, making express annexation words redundant and passing the benefit to successors of any part without assignment. The decision was controversial and has attracted sustained academic criticism.

The principal objection, advanced by G.H. Newsom ('Universal Annexation?' (1981) 97 LQR 32) and supported by Roger Smith, is that Brightman LJ misread s.78. The section provides that a covenant relating to land shall be deemed made with the covenantee and their successors in title — it was intended to remove the need for the covenantee to covenant on behalf of successors, not to effect automatic annexation. Pre-Federated Homes authority (Re Ballard's Conveyance [1937]) held that s.78 did not produce automatic annexation, and that view was widely accepted.

A further objection is practical: Federated Homes may annex benefits to land without the parties' awareness, potentially creating enforcement rights that were never intended. The counter-argument, advanced by supporters of the decision, is that s.78 produces a sensible and commercially convenient result — most parties intend the benefit of a covenant to pass with the land.

The Court of Appeal declined to depart from Federated Homes in Roake v Chadha [1984], though it held the annexation effect could be excluded by clear contrary intention. The decision stands, but its doctrinal basis remains contested. The Law Commission's 2011 report, while not directly addressing Federated Homes, proposed a fresh statutory scheme that would make the question moot for future covenants.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: FEDERATED HOMES · S.78 LPA 1925 · AUTOMATIC ANNEXATION · NEWSOM · ROGER SMITH · RE BALLARD · ROAKE V CHADHA · ACADEMIC CRITICISM

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: What is the academic and political debate on the leasehold system in England and Wales, and how far do recent reforms address the underlying concerns?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
The leasehold system — under which approximately 4-5 million residential properties in England and Wales are held on long leases rather than freehold — has been the subject of sustained criticism and recent legislative reform.

The principal objections are:

1. ground rents: developers have inserted escalating ground rent clauses into long leases, generating income streams that render properties unmortgageable and effectively trap leaseholders
2. service charges: leaseholders have limited ability to challenge unreasonable service charges, and management companies face insufficient accountability
3. forfeiture: the threat of forfeiture for breach of leasehold obligations gives landlords disproportionate power over leaseholders who have paid substantial premiums for their leases
4. leasehold house sales: selling new houses on long leasehold terms (rather than freehold) has been widely condemned as unnecessary and exploitative.

Recent reform: the Leasehold Reform (Ground Rent) Act 2022 prohibited ground rents on new residential leases (reducing them to a 'peppercorn'). The Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024 further extended leaseholders' rights to extend their leases and acquire the freehold, and introduced new restrictions on service charges and forfeiture.

Academic commentary: Nick Hopkins and others at the Law Commission ('Leasehold Home Ownership: Buying Your Freehold or Extending Your Lease' (Law Com No 392, 2020)) have recommended abolishing the leasehold system for new houses entirely and making commonhold the default for flats. The 2024 Act falls short of this — it reforms rather than replaces the existing system.

The unresolved tension: leasehold reform has been politically contentious because of the financial interests of large freeholders and management companies. Whether the 2024 reforms are sufficient, or whether commonhold will ultimately replace leasehold for residential property, remains the central open question in English land law policy.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: LEASEHOLD REFORM · GROUND RENTS · SERVICE CHARGES · FORFEITURE · NICK HOPKINS · LAW COM 392 · LEASEHOLD AND FREEHOLD REFORM ACT 2024

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

In 2010, Gary sold part of his garden to Harriet. The transfer deed contained a covenant by Harriet to contribute equally to the cost of maintaining the shared boundary wall. The deed was properly executed and Harriet signed it. Harriet has since sold her plot to Ivan, who had full knowledge of the covenant before purchasing. Gary now seeks to enforce the maintenance covenant against Ivan. Ivan argues he is not the original covenantor.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: Ivan had full knowledge of the covenant before purchasing. The scenario is engineered to make the doctrine of notice feel relevant — students conclude that because Ivan knew about the covenant, he is bound by it.
Trap Cards.rule: The Reality: The burden of a positive covenant never passes to a successor in title at common law or in equity, regardless of notice: Rhone v Stephens [1994], confirming Austerberry v Corporation of Oldham (1885). The covenant to maintain the wall requires expenditure — it is positive. Ivan is not bound. Notice is entirely irrelevant to this analysis.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is Ivan's actual knowledge. In unregistered land, notice is the key concept for equitable interests — students apply it by reflex here. But the rule against passing positive covenant burdens is absolute and has nothing to do with notice. Even if Ivan had signed a document acknowledging the covenant, he would not be bound as a matter of property law (though he might be contractually liable if he gave a personal covenant). The notice doctrine applies to equitable interests, not to the question of whether the burden of a positive covenant runs.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Notice is irrelevant to whether a positive covenant burden binds a successor. The burden of a positive covenant never runs with freehold land at law or in equity — the only exceptions are indirect methods (benefit and burden, indemnity chains, leasehold structure).
Trap Cards.summary_line: POSITIVE COVENANT · BURDEN NEVER PASSES · NOTICE IRRELEVANT · RHONE V STEPHENS · FREEHOLD LAND

Creation and Characteristics.front: What are the four characteristics required for a right to be capable of existing as an easement?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
The four characteristics from Re Ellenborough Park [1956] are:

1. there must be a dominant and a servient tenement — easements cannot exist 'in gross' without dominant land
2. the easement must accommodate and serve the dominant tenement — it must benefit the land itself, not merely the owner personally
3. the dominant and servient tenements must be owned or occupied by different persons
4. the easement must be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant — it must be sufficiently definite, and there must be a capable grantor and grantee.

⚠️ A right that benefits an owner personally but not the land as land cannot be an easement: Hill v Tupper [1863].
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: RE ELLENBOROUGH PARK · FOUR CHARACTERISTICS · DOMINANT SERVIENT · ACCOMMODATION · SUBJECT MATTER OF GRANT · HILL V TUPPER

Creation and Characteristics.front: What is the distinction between an easement and a licence in relation to land?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
An easement is a proprietary right — it is capable of binding successors in title and runs with the land. A licence is a personal permission — it creates no proprietary right and (generally) does not bind third parties. The distinction matters on transfer: an easement will pass with the dominant land; a licence will not.

⚠️ A contractual licence may bind a successor if it is coupled with a grant or equity intervenes (Errington v Errington [1952]), but this is exceptional. A licence that is dressed up as an easement will not be treated as one if the Re Ellenborough Park characteristics are not met.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: EASEMENT PROPRIETARY · LICENCE PERSONAL · BINDS SUCCESSORS · ERRINGTON EXCEPTIONAL · RE ELLENBOROUGH PARK

Creation and Characteristics.front: How may an easement be expressly created?
Creation and Characteristics.answer: An easement may be expressly granted (by the servient owner in favour of the dominant owner) or expressly reserved (by the grantor when selling the dominant land, retaining an easement over it). Express grant or reservation must be by deed to create a legal easement (s.52 LPA 1925). In registered land, a legal easement created by express grant must also be registered as a registrable disposition (s.27 LRA 2002) — until registration, the easement takes effect in equity only. An expressly granted equitable easement arises if: (a) the deed requirement is not met but there is a valid written contract (s.2 LP(MP)A 1989); or (b) the right is not registered in registered land.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: EXPRESS GRANT · EXPRESS RESERVATION · DEED · S.27 LRA 2002 · REGISTRATION · EQUITABLE EASEMENT · S.2 LP(MP)A

Creation and Characteristics.front: What is the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows, and when does it apply?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
The rule in Wheeldon v Burrows [1879] implies an easement in favour of a grantee of part of a seller's land where:

1. the right was used by the seller over the retained land prior to the grant (a 'quasi-easement')
2. the right was continuous and apparent — i.e. there was some physical sign of it on inspection; and
3. the right was necessary for the reasonable enjoyment of the land granted.

The rule operates on a grant (not a reservation) and requires that the grantor previously enjoyed the quasi-easement.

⚠️ It does not require necessity — reasonable enjoyment suffices. Contrast with s.62 LPA 1925 which does not require prior quasi-easement use.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: WHEELDON V BURROWS · QUASI-EASEMENT · CONTINUOUS AND APPARENT · REASONABLE ENJOYMENT · GRANT NOT RESERVATION

Creation and Characteristics.front: How does s.62 LPA 1925 operate to create or upgrade easements?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
Section 62 LPA 1925 provides that a conveyance of land passes with it all 'liberties, privileges, easements, rights and advantages' appertaining to or reputed to appertain to the land. It can:

1. pass existing easements to a purchaser automatically
2. upgrade a licence or permission into a full easement on conveyance — if a permission was enjoyed before the conveyance, s.62 may convert it into a legal easement: Wright v Macadam [1949].

⚠️ Section 62 requires a conveyance (i.e. a disposition by deed). It does not apply where the grantor and grantee were previously the same person occupying the same land (no prior diversity of occupation needed for Wheeldon but needed for s.62 to upgrade permissions: P&S Platt v Crouch [2003]).
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: S.62 LPA 1925 · CONVEYANCE · UPGRADE LICENCE · WRIGHT V MACADAM · DIVERSITY OF OCCUPATION · PRIOR PERMISSION

Creation and Characteristics.front: When will an easement of necessity be implied?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
An easement of necessity is implied where, without it, the granted land would be completely inaccessible — i.e. landlocked with no other means of access. The easement is strictly limited to what is necessary, not what is convenient: Manjang v Drammeh [1991]. It arises on grant (or exceptionally on reservation) and is implied into the deed.

⚠️ Easements of necessity are narrowly construed. The test is absolute necessity — not reasonable enjoyment. If there is any other access, however inconvenient, an easement of necessity will not be implied. Contrast with the Wheeldon v Burrows test (reasonable enjoyment) and the common intention test (parties' shared intention).
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: EASEMENT OF NECESSITY · ABSOLUTE NECESSITY · LANDLOCKED · MANJANG V DRAMMEH · NARROW CONSTRUCTION · NOT CONVENIENCE

Creation and Characteristics.front: How may an easement be acquired by prescription?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
Prescription is the acquisition of an easement by long use. Three methods exist:

1. Common law prescription — use since 'time immemorial' (1189); rarely successful in practice.
2. Lost modern grant — the court presumes a grant was made but lost, where 20 years' use is shown; the fiction can be rebutted by evidence the grant could not have been made.
3. Prescription Act 1832 — use for 20 years (easements generally) or 40 years (light) immediately before action creates a presumptive right that cannot be defeated by proof the use began after 1189.

All methods require use that is: as of right (nec vi, nec clam, nec precario — without force, secrecy, or permission), continuous, and by or on behalf of the fee simple owner against the fee simple owner.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: PRESCRIPTION · AS OF RIGHT · NEC VI NEC CLAM NEC PRECARIO · PRESCRIPTION ACT 1832 · LOST MODERN GRANT · 20 YEARS

Creation and Characteristics.front: What is the 'nec vi, nec clam, nec precario' requirement in prescriptive easements?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
For prescription to operate, use must be 'as of right' — nec vi, nec clam, nec precario (without force, without secrecy, without permission). Without force: the use must not be contentious or exercised against active objection. Without secrecy: the use must be open and visible so the servient owner could have known of and objected to it. Without permission: any express or implied licence defeats prescription — a permissive use cannot ripen into an easement because it is not enjoyed 'as of right'.

⚠️ If the servient owner grants permission, the prescription period is interrupted and must restart.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: NEC VI NEC CLAM NEC PRECARIO · WITHOUT FORCE · WITHOUT SECRECY · WITHOUT PERMISSION · PERMISSION DEFEATS CLAIM

Creation and Characteristics.front: Can an easement grant the dominant owner the right to exclusive possession of the servient land?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
No. An easement cannot amount to exclusive possession or joint use of the servient land — that would be inconsistent with the servient owner's ownership. The right must leave the servient owner with some reasonable use of their land: Copeland v Greenhalf [1952]. A right to store items on a large area of another's land where the servient owner is effectively excluded was held not to be capable of being an easement.

⚠️ However, the courts have since taken a more flexible approach: a right to use a defined area for parking may be an easement if the servient owner retains some use — it depends on degree: Moncrieff v Jamieson [2007] (SC). The absolute exclusion test from Copeland is the starting point.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: EASEMENT · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION · SERVIENT OWNER MUST RETAIN USE · COPELAND V GREENHALF · MONCRIEFF V JAMIESON · PARKING

Creation and Characteristics.front: How may an easement be extinguished?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
An easement may be extinguished by:

1. Release — express release by deed, or implied release by abandonment. Abandonment requires clear evidence of an intention to abandon the right permanently — mere non-use is insufficient: Benn v Hardinge (1992)
2. Unity of ownership and possession — if the dominant and servient land come into common ownership and possession, the easement merges and is extinguished
3. Statutory extinguishment — e.g. compulsory purchase.

⚠️ Non-use alone does not extinguish an easement, however long the period of non-use. The servient owner must show a positive intention to abandon — raising the bar considerably.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: EXTINGUISHMENT · ABANDONMENT · BENN V HARDINGE · NON-USE INSUFFICIENT · UNITY OF OWNERSHIP · POSITIVE INTENTION

Creation and Characteristics.front: How are legal and equitable easements protected in registered and unregistered land?
Creation and Characteristics.answer: In registered land: A legal easement expressly granted must be registered as a registrable disposition (s.27 LRA 2002) to be legal — until then, it is equitable. Implied and prescriptive legal easements may override under Sch 3, para 3 LRA 2002 if: (a) the easement is known to the disponee; (b) it would have been obvious on a reasonably careful inspection; or (c) it has been exercised in the year before the disposition. Equitable easements in registered land should be protected by notice. In unregistered land: Legal easements bind the world. Equitable easements created post-1925 must be registered as D(iii) land charges — if not, void against a purchaser for money or money's worth of a legal estate. Pre-1926 equitable easements are governed by the doctrine of notice.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: LEGAL EASEMENT BINDS WORLD · SCH 3 PARA 3 · D(III) LAND CHARGE · EQUITABLE EASEMENT NOTICE · PRE-1926 DOCTRINE OF NOTICE

Creation and Characteristics.front: What is the 'accommodation' requirement for an easement, and how do the courts apply it?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
The accommodation requirement (Re Ellenborough Park [1956]) means the easement must benefit the dominant land as land — it must be connected to normal use of that land. Two key distinctions:

1. A right that merely benefits the owner personally (not the land) cannot be an easement: Hill v Tupper [1863] — a right to put pleasure boats on a canal benefited the business, not the land.
2. A right that genuinely enhances the amenity of the dominant land qualifies: Re Ellenborough Park — the right to use a communal garden accommodated the surrounding residential properties.

⚠️ Proximity matters but is not sufficient — the right must functionally relate to the use of the dominant land, not merely be geographically near it.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: ACCOMMODATION · RE ELLENBOROUGH PARK · BENEFIT LAND NOT OWNER · HILL V TUPPER · FUNCTIONAL RELATION · PROXIMITY INSUFFICIENT

Creation and Characteristics.front: What is the difference between Wheeldon v Burrows and s.62 LPA 1925 in terms of requirements and effect?
Creation and Characteristics.answer:
Both imply easements on a conveyance of part, but differ significantly: Wheeldon v Burrows requires:

1. a quasi-easement used before the grant
2. that it was continuous and apparent
3. necessary for reasonable enjoyment. It applies only on a grant (not a reservation). Section 62 LPA 1925 requires:
1. a conveyance by deed
2. a right or advantage enjoyed at the time of the conveyance — which may be a mere licence rather than a quasi-easement.

It can upgrade a permission into an easement.

⚠️

**Key distinction:** s.62 does not require the prior quasi-easement to have been used by the grantor — it can operate where the same person owned both plots but a tenant or licensee exercised a right. Wheeldon requires prior use by the grantor over the retained land.
Creation and Characteristics.summary_line: WHEELDON V BURROWS VS S.62 · QUASI-EASEMENT · CONTINUOUS APPARENT · UPGRADE LICENCE · GRANTOR USE · CONVEYANCE

Accommodation Requirement.issue: Whether an exclusive right to put pleasure boats on a canal could exist as an easement benefiting adjacent land.
Accommodation Requirement.rule: A right cannot exist as an easement unless it accommodates and serves the dominant tenement as land — a right that merely benefits the owner personally or their business does not qualify.
Accommodation Requirement.application: Hill was granted an exclusive right to put pleasure boats on a canal adjacent to his land. A rival operator (Tupper) also put boats on the canal. Hill claimed his right was an easement enforceable against Tupper. The court held the right was not an easement — it benefited Hill's business, not his land as land.
Accommodation Requirement.conclusion: The claim failed. The right was a contractual licence personal to Hill, not a proprietary easement. Hill v Tupper remains the leading authority for the accommodation requirement — a right that benefits the owner's commercial activity without enhancing the land itself cannot be an easement.
Accommodation Requirement.summary_line: EASEMENT · ACCOMMODATION REQUIREMENT · PERSONAL BENEFIT NOT EASEMENT · NUMERUS CLAUSUS · HILL V TUPPER

Characteristics of Easements.issue: Whether a right to use a communal garden could exist as an easement benefiting the surrounding residential properties.
Characteristics of Easements.rule:
For a right to be capable of existing as an easement, four characteristics must be present:

1. dominant and servient tenements
2. the right must accommodate and serve the dominant tenement
3. different owners or occupiers
4. the right must be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant.
Characteristics of Easements.application: Owners of houses surrounding Ellenborough Park had been granted the right to use the park as a garden. The Court of Appeal considered whether this right was capable of being an easement. All four characteristics were satisfied — the right to use the garden genuinely enhanced the enjoyment of the surrounding residential properties as land.
Characteristics of Easements.conclusion: The right was capable of existing as an easement. Re Ellenborough Park is the foundational authority for the four characteristics of an easement and the accommodation requirement. It established that recreational rights can qualify as easements if they genuinely benefit the dominant land.
Characteristics of Easements.summary_line: EASEMENT · FOUR CHARACTERISTICS · ACCOMMODATION · DOMINANT TENEMENT · RECREATIONAL RIGHT · RE ELLENBOROUGH PARK

Implied Easements — Wheeldon v Burrows.issue: Whether a right used by a vendor over their retained land prior to a sale of part could be implied as an easement in favour of the purchaser.
Implied Easements — Wheeldon v Burrows.rule:
On a grant of part of land, the grantee acquires by implication all quasi-easements that were:

1. continuous and apparent
2. necessary for the reasonable enjoyment of the land granted; and
3. used by the grantor over the retained land prior to the grant.
Implied Easements — Wheeldon v Burrows.application: Wheeldon sold part of his land to Burrows. Before the sale, Wheeldon had used windows in the workshop on the sold land to receive light from the retained land. No express grant of a right to light was made. The court implied an easement of light in favour of Burrows based on the prior quasi-easement use.
Implied Easements — Wheeldon v Burrows.conclusion: The easement of light was implied. Wheeldon v Burrows remains the primary route for implying easements on a sale of part where the grantor previously exercised a quasi-easement. The rule applies on grant only — not reservation — and requires continuous and apparent use plus reasonable necessity.
Implied Easements — Wheeldon v Burrows.summary_line: IMPLIED EASEMENT · QUASI-EASEMENT · CONTINUOUS AND APPARENT · REASONABLE ENJOYMENT · GRANT OF PART · WHEELDON V BURROWS

Implied Easements — Section 62.issue: Whether a licence to store coal in a shed, enjoyed by a tenant before the grant of a new lease, was converted into an easement by s.62 LPA 1925 on execution of the new lease.
Implied Easements — Section 62.rule: Section 62 LPA 1925 operates on a conveyance to pass all rights and advantages appertaining to the land — it can upgrade a licence or permission enjoyed before the conveyance into a full legal easement.
Implied Easements — Section 62.application: A tenant had been permitted to store coal in a garden shed. On the grant of a new lease, no express reference was made to this right. The Court of Appeal held s.62 operated to convert the permission into an easement passing with the new lease.
Implied Easements — Section 62.conclusion: The tenant acquired an easement to store coal. Wright v Macadam is the leading authority for s.62's ability to upgrade a licence into an easement on a conveyance — even where the right was merely permissive before the grant. It requires a conveyance (by deed) and a right enjoyed at the time of the conveyance.
Implied Easements — Section 62.summary_line: S.62 LPA 1925 · LICENCE UPGRADED TO EASEMENT · CONVEYANCE · PRIOR PERMISSION · STORAGE RIGHT · WRIGHT V MACADAM

Exclusive Possession and Scope.issue: Whether a right to store vehicles on a strip of land belonging to another could exist as an easement.
Exclusive Possession and Scope.rule: A right cannot exist as an easement if it amounts to a claim to exclusive or joint possession of the servient land — an easement must leave the servient owner with some reasonable use of their land.
Exclusive Possession and Scope.application: Greenhalf had for many years parked vehicles on a narrow strip of Copeland's land adjoining his workshop. He claimed an easement by prescription. Upjohn J held the right claimed was too extensive — it would effectively deprive Copeland of any reasonable use of the strip, amounting to virtual possession.
Exclusive Possession and Scope.conclusion: The claim failed. Copeland v Greenhalf establishes that an easement cannot amount to exclusive or joint use of the servient land. It remains the starting point for analysing parking and storage rights as easements — though subsequent cases (Moncrieff v Jamieson) have taken a more flexible approach for defined parking spaces.
Exclusive Possession and Scope.summary_line: EASEMENT · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION · STORAGE · SERVIENT OWNER MUST RETAIN USE · PARKING · COPELAND V GREENHALF

Exclusive Possession and Scope.issue: Whether a right to park vehicles on the servient land could exist as an easement where the servient owner retained other uses of the land.
Exclusive Possession and Scope.rule: A right to park may exist as an easement provided it does not amount to exclusive possession — the test is whether the servient owner retains reasonable use of the land, not whether they retain possession in any technical sense.
Exclusive Possession and Scope.application: Jamieson claimed an easement to park on Moncrieff's land, which was the only practicable place to park given the layout. The House of Lords held that a right to park could in principle be an easement — Copeland v Greenhalf's absolute exclusion test was too strict. The question was whether the servient owner retained any reasonable use.
Exclusive Possession and Scope.conclusion: The easement of parking was recognised in principle. Moncrieff v Jamieson confirmed that parking rights can be easements and relaxed the strict approach in Copeland — the test is practical exclusion of the servient owner from all reasonable use, not any exclusion. The case is significant for modern property practice.
Exclusive Possession and Scope.summary_line: EASEMENT · PARKING · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION TEST · SERVIENT OWNER RETAINS USE · MONCRIEFF RELAXES COPELAND

Extinguishment of Easements.issue: Whether long non-use of a right of way was sufficient evidence of an intention to abandon the easement.
Extinguishment of Easements.rule: Non-use of an easement alone, however prolonged, does not extinguish it — abandonment requires clear evidence of a positive intention to permanently relinquish the right.
Extinguishment of Easements.application: A right of way had not been used for approximately 175 years. The Court of Appeal held that non-use, even for such a long period, was not sufficient to establish abandonment — there was no positive evidence of an intention to give up the right permanently.
Extinguishment of Easements.conclusion: The easement had not been abandoned. Benn v Hardinge sets a high bar for abandonment — mere non-use is insufficient and the court will not readily infer an intention to abandon. The dominant owner must have done something positively inconsistent with an intention to retain the right.
Extinguishment of Easements.summary_line: EASEMENT · ABANDONMENT · NON-USE INSUFFICIENT · POSITIVE INTENTION REQUIRED · EXTINGUISHMENT · BENN V HARDINGE

Implied Easements — Common Intention.issue: Whether an easement could be implied by common intention where the intended use of the granted land required the right, even without strict necessity.
Implied Easements — Common Intention.rule: An easement may be implied by common intention where both parties to the grant knew and intended that the land would be used in a particular way, and that use requires the easement — strict necessity is not required.
Implied Easements — Common Intention.application: A colliery company granted rights to work coal, intending the grantee to use the surface for tipping spoil. No express easement to tip was granted. The House of Lords held an easement could be implied by common intention — both parties knew the intended use of the land required the right to tip.
Implied Easements — Common Intention.conclusion: The implied easement by common intention was recognised in principle, though on the facts the claim failed on other grounds. Pwllbach Colliery establishes the common intention route to implied easements as distinct from and broader than necessity — the parties' shared purpose at the time of the grant is the key.
Implied Easements — Common Intention.summary_line: IMPLIED EASEMENT · COMMON INTENTION · BROADER THAN NECESSITY · INTENDED USE · GRANT OF PART · PWLLBACH

Creation and Prescription.front: Quinn owns a large garden. In 2010 she sold the front portion to Raj, granting him no express easements. Quinn retained the rear portion, which is accessible only through a gate in Raj's garden. Quinn now seeks to use the gate to access her retained land. Raj refuses. Advise Quinn.
Creation and Prescription.answer:
Quinn may claim an easement of necessity over Raj's land. An easement of necessity is implied where the granted or retained land would be completely inaccessible without it. Here, Quinn's retained land has no other access — it is landlocked. The easement is implied on the reservation (Quinn retaining a right over the sold land).

⚠️ Easements of necessity are strictly construed — they arise only where there is absolute necessity, not mere inconvenience: Manjang v Drammeh [1991]. If there is any other access, however inconvenient, the easement will not be implied. Assuming no other access exists, Quinn has an implied easement of necessity over Raj's land to pass through the gate. She may also argue a common intention easement — if both parties knew the rear plot would need access through the gate, an easement is implied to give effect to that intention: Pwllbach Colliery Co v Woodman [1915]. Quinn should seek to register the easement once established.
Creation and Prescription.summary_line: EASEMENT OF NECESSITY · LANDLOCKED · RESERVATION · ABSOLUTE NECESSITY · PWLLBACH ALTERNATIVE · REGISTER EASEMENT

Creation and Prescription.front: Before selling the eastern part of his land to Sara, Tom regularly used a path across the eastern part to reach his greenhouse on the western part he retained. The path was clearly visible — worn into the lawn. No express easement was granted in the transfer. Sara now blocks the path. Can Tom claim an easement?
Creation and Prescription.answer:
Tom may claim an implied easement under Wheeldon v Burrows [1879]. The requirements are:

1. a quasi-easement — Tom used the path over the sold land before the grant; yes, he regularly used it
2. continuous and apparent — the path was worn into the lawn and clearly visible on inspection; yes
3. necessary for the reasonable enjoyment of the retained land — access to the greenhouse on the retained land satisfies this.

All three conditions are met. An easement is implied in Tom's favour over Sara's land (the sold portion) by virtue of Wheeldon v Burrows.

⚠️ The rule operates on a grant of part — Tom as grantor retains an implied easement over the sold land. Tom should register the easement at HMLR to protect it going forward.
Creation and Prescription.summary_line: WHEELDON V BURROWS · QUASI-EASEMENT · CONTINUOUS APPARENT · REASONABLE ENJOYMENT · GRANTOR RESERVES · REGISTER

Creation and Prescription.front: Una rented a flat from Victor. Victor permitted Una to store her bicycle in the communal shed during the tenancy — this was a personal permission, not included in the lease. Victor later sold the building to Wendy, who granted Una a new lease of the flat by deed. The new lease made no mention of the shed. Wendy now says Una has no right to use the shed. Advise Una.
Creation and Prescription.answer: Una may have acquired an easement to use the shed through s.62 LPA 1925. Section 62 provides that a conveyance of land passes all rights and advantages appertaining to or enjoyed with the land. The grant of the new lease by deed is a conveyance. Before the conveyance, Una enjoyed a permission to use the shed — even though it was merely a licence, s.62 can upgrade a permission enjoyed before the conveyance into a legal easement on the grant of a new lease: Wright v Macadam [1949]. The permission was enjoyed with the flat at the time of the new lease. Section 62 therefore converts Una's permission into a legal easement to use the shed, passing with the new lease. Una has an easement and Wendy cannot revoke it.
Creation and Prescription.summary_line: S.62 LPA 1925 · LICENCE UPGRADED · WRIGHT V MACADAM · NEW LEASE BY DEED · CONVEYANCE · EASEMENT ACQUIRED

Creation and Prescription.front: Xavier has used a footpath across his neighbour Yasmin's field to reach his land for 22 years. He used it openly, without force, and without any permission from Yasmin. Yasmin now erects a fence blocking the path and argues Xavier has no legal right. Advise Xavier.
Creation and Prescription.answer:
Xavier may have acquired an easement by prescription under the Prescription Act 1832. For most easements, 20 years' use immediately before action creates a presumptive right. Xavier has used the path for 22 years — satisfying the 20-year period. The use must be as of right: nec vi (without force — satisfied), nec clam (without secrecy — open use, satisfied), nec precario (without permission — no permission was given, satisfied). Xavier's use meets all requirements. He has a prescriptive easement over Yasmin's field.

⚠️ Xavier should act promptly — the 20-year period must be immediately before the action, and Yasmin's fence constitutes an interruption. If the interruption is not challenged within one year, the period may be broken. Xavier should apply to register the easement at HMLR and seek removal of the fence.
Creation and Prescription.summary_line: PRESCRIPTION ACT 1832 · 20 YEARS · NEC PRECARIO · NO PERMISSION · AS OF RIGHT · REGISTER EASEMENT
