Creation and Prescription.front: Zoe claims an easement to park her car on a strip of Adam's land adjoining her property. She has parked there openly and without permission for 21 years. Adam argues that parking cannot be an easement as it amounts to exclusive use of the strip. Advise Zoe.
Creation and Prescription.answer: Zoe must establish that parking can exist as an easement and that her prescriptive claim succeeds. Can parking be an easement? The leading cases are Copeland v Greenhalf [1952] — which held that a right amounting to exclusive or joint possession of the servient land cannot be an easement — and Moncrieff v Jamieson [2007] — which relaxed this to ask whether the servient owner retains reasonable use of the strip, not whether there is any exclusion. If Zoe's parking on the strip leaves Adam with no reasonable use of it, the right cannot be an easement. If Adam retains some reasonable use, it can. On the prescriptive claim: Zoe has used the strip for 21 years (exceeding the 20-year Prescription Act 1832 threshold), openly, without force, and without permission — all requirements are met. If the right is capable of being an easement (Moncrieff test satisfied), Zoe has acquired it by prescription. Adam should be advised the outcome depends on how much use of the strip he retains.
Creation and Prescription.summary_line: PARKING EASEMENT · PRESCRIPTION · COPELAND V GREENHALF · MONCRIEFF · SERVIENT OWNER RETAINS USE · 21 YEARS

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: Does the overriding interest of a person in actual occupation fundamentally undermine the mirror principle of registered land, and how has the LRA 2002 attempted to strike a balance?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
The mirror principle — that the register should reflect the complete picture of title — is structurally compromised by Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002, which allows the interest of a person in actual occupation to bind a registered disponee without appearing on the register. Williams & Glyn's Bank v Boland [1981] dramatically illustrated this: a bank took a mortgage over a matrimonial home without discovering the wife's beneficial interest, which overrode the mortgage by reason of her actual occupation.

The LRA 2002 attempted to narrow the overriding interest while preserving protection for genuine occupiers. Key modifications from the Land Registration Act 1925 position:

1. the occupation must be obvious on a reasonably careful inspection, or the disponee must have actual knowledge of it — mere presence is not automatically sufficient
2. an occupier who fails to disclose their interest when asked loses overriding status
3. the requirement that the interest subsist at the time of the disposition (not merely at the time of registration) was clarified.

Academic opinion is divided. Martin Dixon ('Proprietary Rights and the "Mirror Principle"' (2005)) argues that the compromise in the 2002 Act is defensible — genuine occupation that would be apparent to a careful purchaser is a legitimate exception to the mirror principle, and the alternative (requiring all beneficial interests to be registered) would be impractical for domestic co-ownership. Others, including Peter Sparkes, argue the exception remains too broad and creates unacceptable uncertainty for purchasers and mortgagees.

The unresolved tension: any system that protects unregistered interests must choose between the certainty of the register and the protection of informal rights. The LRA 2002 leans toward protection of occupiers but has not fully resolved the underlying conflict.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: OVERRIDING INTERESTS · MIRROR PRINCIPLE · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · BOLAND · LRA 2002 · MARTIN DIXON · PETER SPARKES · UNRESOLVED

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Jack and Karen own a house as beneficial joint tenants. Jack executes a will leaving 'my share of the house to my daughter Lucy.' Jack dies shortly afterwards. Lucy claims a 50% share of the house under the will. Karen argues the right of survivorship means she takes the whole beneficial interest.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: Jack has executed a valid will expressly dealing with his share of the house. The scenario makes it feel like the will should be effective — Jack clearly intended Lucy to inherit, and the will is valid.
Trap Cards.rule: The Reality: A joint tenant cannot sever a joint tenancy by will. Severance must occur during the joint tenant's lifetime. A will only takes effect on death — but at the moment of death, the right of survivorship (ius accrescendi) operates instantaneously, passing the deceased joint tenant's interest to the surviving joint tenants before the will can take effect. There is nothing left for the will to operate on. Karen takes the full beneficial interest by survivorship.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the valid, specific will. Students see a formal legal document dealing expressly with the share and conclude it must be effective. The trap is the timing — survivorship operates at the moment of death, prior to the will taking effect. Jack needed to sever the joint tenancy during his lifetime (by written notice under s.196(4) LPA 1925, by alienation, or by mutual agreement) to enable his share to pass under the will.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: A will cannot sever a joint tenancy — it takes effect on death, but survivorship operates at death before the will can act. To pass a share under a will, the joint tenant must sever the equitable joint tenancy during their lifetime.
Trap Cards.summary_line: JOINT TENANCY · SEVERANCE BY WILL INEFFECTIVE · SURVIVORSHIP OPERATES AT DEATH · SEVER DURING LIFETIME

Creation and Covenants.front: What are the essential characteristics of a lease?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
A lease (term of years absolute) requires three essential characteristics established in Street v Mountford [1985]:

1. Exclusive possession — the tenant has the right to exclude all others, including the landlord (except with right of entry for specified purposes)
2. A definite term — the maximum duration must be certain at the time of grant (Lace v Chantler [1944])
3. Payment of rent is not strictly essential but is typical.

⚠️ The label attached by the parties is irrelevant — if the agreement grants exclusive possession for a term, it is a lease in law regardless of whether it is called a 'licence': Street v Mountford [1985]. A licence does not grant exclusive possession.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: LEASE · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION · DEFINITE TERM · STREET V MOUNTFORD · LABEL IRRELEVANT · LACE V CHANTLER

Creation and Covenants.front: What formalities are required to create a legal lease?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
The formalities depend on the length of the lease:

1. Leases of more than 3 years: must be created by deed (s.52 LPA 1925). In registered land, a lease of more than 7 years must also be substantively registered (s.27 LRA 2002) to operate as a legal lease.
2. Leases of 3 years or less: exempt from the deed requirement under s.54
2. LPA 1925 if they take effect in possession, at the best rent reasonably obtainable, and without taking a fine.

Oral leases of up to 3 years are therefore valid.

⚠️ A lease granted without the required formalities may take effect as an equitable lease under Walsh v Lonsdale [1882] if there is a valid written contract (s.2 LP(MP)A 1989) and specific performance would be granted.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: LEASE FORMALITIES · DEED OVER 3 YEARS · S.54(2) EXCEPTION · 7-YEAR REGISTRATION · WALSH V LONSDALE · EQUITABLE LEASE

Creation and Covenants.front: What is the rule in Walsh v Lonsdale, and what are its limitations?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
Walsh v Lonsdale [1882] holds that where there is a specifically enforceable agreement for a lease (a contract in writing satisfying s.2 LP(MP)A 1989) but the formal lease has not been granted, equity treats the position as if the lease had been granted — 'equity looks on that as done which ought to be done.' The equitable lease has the same terms as the contractual agreement. Limitations:

1. it binds only the original parties and successors with notice (no automatic binding of third parties as a legal lease would)
2. specific performance is a discretionary remedy — it may be refused
3. the landlord cannot forfeit without a formal lease
4. it does not confer the same third-party enforceability as a legal lease.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: WALSH V LONSDALE · EQUITABLE LEASE · SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE · DISCRETIONARY · THIRD PARTY LIMITS · EQUITY ACTS AS DONE

Creation and Covenants.front: What is the distinction between privity of contract and privity of estate in leasehold covenants?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
Privity of contract exists between the original landlord and original tenant — they remain liable on the lease covenants throughout the lease term under the old rules (pre-1996 leases). Privity of estate exists between the current landlord and current tenant — it arises from the landlord-tenant relationship itself and allows enforcement of leasehold covenants that 'touch and concern' the land.

⚠️ Under the old (pre-LTCA 1995) rules, an original tenant who assigns the lease remains liable under privity of contract for the entire term. This is one of the most heavily tested SQE distinctions in leasehold covenants.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: PRIVITY OF CONTRACT · PRIVITY OF ESTATE · ORIGINAL TENANT LIABILITY · PRE-1996 · TOUCH AND CONCERN · LTCA 1995

Creation and Covenants.front: How do leasehold covenants pass under the Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 (new leases)?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
The LTCA 1995 applies to leases granted on or after 1 January 1996 ('new tenancies'). Under the Act:

1. On assignment of the lease, the outgoing tenant is automatically released from future liability on tenant covenants (s.5 LTCA 1995) — a radical change from the old rules.
2. The landlord is not automatically released on assignment of the reversion — they must serve a s.8 notice and obtain release or tribunal consent.
3. All covenants (not just those touching and concerning) pass with the lease or reversion unless they are personal in nature (s.3 LTCA 1995).

⚠️ Landlords under new tenancies often require an Authorised Guarantee Agreement (AGA) from the outgoing tenant as a condition of consent to assign — the tenant guarantees the incoming assignee's performance.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: LTCA 1995 · NEW TENANCY · AUTOMATIC RELEASE · S.5 · AGA · ALL COVENANTS PASS · S.8 LANDLORD RELEASE

Creation and Covenants.front: How do leasehold covenants pass under the old rules (pre-LTCA 1995, leases granted before 1 January 1996)?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
For 'old tenancies' (pre-1 January 1996):

1. The benefit and burden of covenants that 'touch and concern' the land pass automatically with the lease (Spencer's Case [1583]) and with the reversion (s.141-142 LPA 1925).
2. The original tenant remains liable under privity of contract for the full lease term, even after assignment.
3. An original landlord who assigns the reversion also retains contractual liability.

⚠️ The 'touch and concern' test: a covenant touches and concerns the land if it affects the landlord-tenant relationship as such, and is not merely a personal obligation. Payment of rent and repair obligations touch and concern; an obligation to pay a personal debt does not.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: OLD TENANCY PRE-1996 · TOUCH AND CONCERN · SPENCER'S CASE · ORIGINAL TENANT LIABILITY · S.141-142 LPA 1925

Creation and Covenants.front: What is an Authorised Guarantee Agreement (AGA) and when is it required?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
An AGA is an agreement under which an outgoing tenant (assignor) under a new tenancy (LTCA 1995) guarantees the performance of the covenants by the immediate assignee. It is the only form of continuing liability a landlord may require from an outgoing tenant under a new tenancy.

**Requirements:**

1. the landlord's consent to assignment was conditional on provision of an AGA
2. it is 'reasonable' in the circumstances to require it (s.16 LTCA 1995).

The AGA only covers the immediate assignee — if the lease is further assigned, the original AGA provider is released.

⚠️ An AGA cannot require the outgoing tenant to guarantee performance beyond the immediate assignee — any such provision is void (s.25 LTCA 1995).
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: AGA · AUTHORISED GUARANTEE AGREEMENT · IMMEDIATE ASSIGNEE ONLY · S.16 LTCA 1995 · S.25 VOID BEYOND ASSIGNEE

Creation and Covenants.front: What is the landlord's remedy of forfeiture, and what procedure must be followed?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
Forfeiture is the landlord's right to terminate the lease and recover possession on breach of covenant by the tenant.

**Procedure:**

1. There must be a forfeiture clause in the lease.
2. For non-payment of rent: formal demand required (waived by common law if the lease so provides or 6 months' rent is owing — s.210 Common Law Procedure Act 1852); landlord must make a physical re-entry or issue proceedings.
3. For other breaches: landlord must first serve a s.146 LPA 1925 notice specifying the breach, requiring remedy (if remediable) and requiring compensation.
4. The tenant (or any mortgagee or underlessee) may apply for relief from forfeiture, which the court grants on terms (usually payment of arrears and costs).

⚠️ Waiver: if the landlord accepts rent with knowledge of the breach, they waive the right to forfeit for that breach.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: FORFEITURE · S.146 LPA 1925 · NON-PAYMENT RENT · RELIEF FROM FORFEITURE · WAIVER · REMEDIABLE BREACH

Creation and Covenants.front: What acts constitute waiver of the right to forfeit?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
Waiver occurs when the landlord, with knowledge of the breach giving rise to the right to forfeit, does an unequivocal act recognising the continued existence of the lease. The most common act of waiver is accepting or demanding rent after the breach. Waiver is automatic — it does not require the landlord to intend to waive: Central Estates (Belgravia) Ltd v Woolgar (No 2) [1972].

⚠️

**Key distinction:** for continuing breaches (e.g. ongoing disrepair), waiver of a past breach does not prevent the landlord from forfeiting for the continuation of the breach — a fresh right arises each day. For once-and-for-all breaches (e.g. unlawful subletting), waiver permanently destroys the right to forfeit for that breach.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: WAIVER · ACCEPTANCE OF RENT · AUTOMATIC · WOOLGAR · CONTINUING BREACH · ONCE-AND-FOR-ALL · FRESH RIGHT

Creation and Covenants.front: What are the main methods by which a lease may be terminated?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
A lease may be terminated by:

1. Expiry — a fixed term lease ends on the contractual term date without notice
2. Notice to quit — periodic tenancies may be terminated by either party giving a valid notice (at least one period's notice, ending on the last day of a period)
3. Forfeiture — by the landlord on breach
4. Surrender — by agreement between landlord and tenant
5. Merger — the tenant acquires the landlord's reversion
6. Frustration — rare but possible (National Carriers Ltd v Panalpina [1981])
7. Disclaimer — by a liquidator or trustee in bankruptcy.

⚠️ Statutory protection may prevent termination in some cases — e.g. residential tenancies under the Housing Acts or business tenancies under the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: LEASE TERMINATION · EXPIRY · NOTICE · FORFEITURE · SURRENDER · MERGER · FRUSTRATION · DISCLAIMER

Creation and Covenants.front: What is the difference between a lease and a licence, and why does it matter?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
A lease grants exclusive possession — the tenant has the right to exclude everyone, including the landlord (except for agreed entry rights). A licence grants personal permission to use land without exclusive possession. The distinction matters because:

1. A lease is a proprietary right — it binds successors in title
2. A lease confers security of tenure and statutory protections
3. A licence is personal and generally does not bind third parties.

The courts look at the substance rather than the label: Street v Mountford [1985]. Devices such as 'non-exclusive occupation agreements' or agreements with 'lodgers' will be treated as leases if exclusive possession is in fact granted.

⚠️ Sham agreements are disregarded: Antoniades v Villiers [1990].
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: LEASE VS LICENCE · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION · SUBSTANCE OVER LABEL · STREET V MOUNTFORD · ANTONIADES · SHAM

Creation and Covenants.front: What is a periodic tenancy and how is it created?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
A periodic tenancy is a tenancy that runs from period to period (weekly, monthly, yearly) until terminated by notice. It may be created:

1. expressly — by agreement
2. impliedly — where a person occupies land with the landlord's consent and pays rent on a periodic basis, the law implies a periodic tenancy on those terms
3. under s.54
2. LPA 1925 — an oral periodic tenancy of up to 3 years' duration (in aggregate) may be created without a deed if it takes effect in possession at the best rent.

⚠️ Where a fixed term lease expires and the tenant holds over paying rent, a periodic tenancy is usually implied — the period is determined by how rent is calculated (not how often it is paid).
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: PERIODIC TENANCY · EXPRESS · IMPLIED · HOLDING OVER · S.54(2) LPA 1925 · PERIOD BY CALCULATION

Creation and Covenants.front: What is the 'touch and concern' test for leasehold covenants under the old rules, and how is it applied?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
The 'touch and concern' test determines which covenants pass with the lease or reversion under the old rules (pre-LTCA 1995). A covenant touches and concerns the land if it:

1. benefits the landlord or tenant in their capacity as landlord or tenant (not merely personally)
2. affects the nature, quality, mode of use, or value of the land
3. is not expressed to be personal.

Applied: covenants to pay rent, repair, insure, not to assign without consent — all touch and concern. A covenant for the landlord to repair a third party's premises — does not touch and concern the leased land.

⚠️ Difficult cases often turn on whether the covenant is so personal that it only benefits a specific individual rather than whoever holds the tenancy.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: TOUCH AND CONCERN · OLD RULES · CAPACITY AS LANDLORD TENANT · RENT REPAIR INSURANCE · PERSONAL COVENANT EXCLUDED

Creation and Covenants.front: What are the landlord's remedies for non-payment of rent, and in what order should they be considered?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
For non-payment of rent, the landlord has several remedies:

1. Action for the debt — a contractual claim for arrears
2. Commercial Rent Arrears Recovery (CRAR) — statutory procedure replacing distress for commercial properties, allowing the landlord to take control of the tenant's goods (Commercial Rent Arrears Recovery (Taking Control of Goods) Regulations 2014)
3. Forfeiture — right to re-enter and terminate the lease on breach (subject to procedure)
4. Drawing on a rent deposit (if held)
5. Pursuing a guarantor or former tenant under an AGA or privity of contract.

⚠️ Forfeiture for non-payment: the landlord must make a formal demand (or the lease must waive this) and may then re-enter physically or by court proceedings. The tenant may apply for relief from forfeiture on payment of all arrears and costs.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: NON-PAYMENT RENT · DEBT ACTION · CRAR · FORFEITURE · RENT DEPOSIT · AGA · RELIEF FROM FORFEITURE

Creation and Covenants.front: What are the requirements for a valid s.146 notice before forfeiture for breach of covenant (other than non-payment of rent)?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
Before forfeiting for a breach other than non-payment of rent, the landlord must serve a valid s.146 LPA 1925 notice that:

1. specifies the breach complained of
2. requires the tenant to remedy the breach (if it is capable of remedy)
3. requires the tenant to make compensation in money (if the landlord requires it).

The tenant must be given a reasonable time to comply before the landlord forfeits.

⚠️ Whether a breach is 'remediable' matters: breach of a covenant not to assign without consent was once held irremediable (Scala House and District Property Co v Forbes [1974]), but the current trend is to treat most breaches as remediable (Expert Clothing Service & Sales Ltd v Hillgate House Ltd [1986]). A stigma breach (e.g. use for immoral purposes) may be irremediable.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: S.146 NOTICE · SPECIFY BREACH · REQUIRE REMEDY · REASONABLE TIME · REMEDIABLE · STIGMA BREACH · EXPERT CLOTHING

Creation and Covenants.front: What covenants are implied into a lease by statute or common law on the part of the landlord?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
Implied landlord covenants include:

1. Quiet enjoyment (common law and s.76 LPA 1925) — the tenant's lawful possession will not be substantially interfered with by the landlord or anyone claiming under the landlord
2. Non-derogation from grant — the landlord must not act inconsistently with the purpose for which the land was let
3. Fitness for habitation at commencement (s.8 Landlord and Tenant Act 1985 — low rent residential tenancies)
4. Repair of structure and exterior and installations in short residential tenancies (s.11 Landlord and Tenant Act 1985 — leases under 7 years).

⚠️ The covenant of quiet enjoyment protects against physical or legal interference — it does not guarantee freedom from noise from neighbouring properties.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: IMPLIED LANDLORD COVENANTS · QUIET ENJOYMENT · NON-DEROGATION · S.11 LTA 1985 · FITNESS FOR HABITATION

Creation and Covenants.front: Ben signs a written agreement to rent a studio flat from Carol for two years at £800 per month. Before a formal lease is executed, Ben moves in and pays rent. Carol then refuses to grant the formal lease and seeks possession. Ben argues he has a valid lease. Advise Ben.
Creation and Covenants.answer:
Ben does not have a legal lease — the agreement was not made by deed (required for leases over three years under s.52(1) LPA 1925), and no deed has been executed. However, Ben may have an equitable lease under Walsh v Lonsdale [1882]. The written agreement satisfies s.2 LP(MP)A 1989 (in writing, signed by both parties, incorporating all terms). Equity treats a specifically enforceable contract for a lease as equivalent to the lease itself. Ben has an equitable lease for two years on the agreed terms.

⚠️ The equitable lease is subject to equity's discretion — specific performance may be refused if Ben is in breach. Additionally, the equitable lease does not automatically bind third parties as a legal lease would. Ben should press for execution of the formal deed.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: WALSH V LONSDALE · EQUITABLE LEASE · WRITTEN AGREEMENT · S.2 LP(MP)A · SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE · FORMAL DEED NEEDED

Creation and Covenants.front: Diana grants Edward a five-year lease of a commercial unit. The lease contains a covenant by Edward not to assign without Diana's consent, and a covenant to keep the premises in repair. Edward assigns the lease to Freya with Diana's consent. Freya fails to repair the premises. Diana serves a s.146 notice on Freya and seeks to forfeit. Edward argues he has no further liability. Is Edward still liable, and can Diana forfeit?
Creation and Covenants.answer: Edward's liability: This is a lease granted after 1 January 1996 — a new tenancy under the LTCA 1995. On assignment, Edward is automatically released from future liability on tenant covenants (s.5 LTCA 1995). Unless Diana required an AGA from Edward as a condition of consent to the assignment (s.16 LTCA 1995), Edward has no continuing liability for Freya's breach. If an AGA was required, Edward guarantees Freya's performance — but only while Freya remains tenant. Forfeiture against Freya: Diana must first serve a valid s.146 LPA 1925 notice specifying the breach (disrepair), requiring remedy (disrepair is a remediable breach: Expert Clothing v Hillgate House [1986]), and requiring compensation if desired. Freya must be given reasonable time to comply. If Freya fails to remedy, Diana may forfeit. Freya may apply for relief from forfeiture on terms.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: LTCA 1995 · NEW TENANCY · AUTOMATIC RELEASE · AGA · S.146 NOTICE · DISREPAIR REMEDIABLE · EXPERT CLOTHING

Creation and Covenants.front: George is the landlord of a commercial lease granted in 1993. His tenant Hannah sublets part of the premises without George's consent, in breach of a covenant against subletting without consent. George discovers the breach on 1 March and on 15 March sends a rent demand for the quarter due on 25 March. George now seeks to forfeit for the breach of covenant on 1 April. Can George forfeit?
Creation and Covenants.answer:
George has waived his right to forfeit. Waiver occurs when a landlord, with knowledge of a breach, does any unequivocal act recognising the continued existence of the lease — including demanding rent: Central Estates v Woolgar (No 2) [1972]. George knew of the breach on 1 March and sent a rent demand on 15 March — after acquiring knowledge. This is an unequivocal act of waiver. Waiver is automatic regardless of George's intention.

⚠️ The unauthorised subletting is a once-and-for-all breach — not a continuing breach. Waiver of a once-and-for-all breach permanently extinguishes the right to forfeit for that breach. George cannot forfeit for the subletting. His only remedy is an action for breach of covenant (damages) or to seek an injunction against the subtenant's continued occupation. George should act immediately on discovering a breach — never send a rent demand before deciding whether to forfeit.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: WAIVER · ONCE-AND-FOR-ALL BREACH · RENT DEMAND AFTER KNOWLEDGE · PERMANENT WAIVER · CANNOT FORFEIT

Creation and Covenants.front: Isla granted a 10-year commercial lease to James in 2018. James assigned the lease to Karen in 2020, with Isla's consent. Isla required James to enter into an AGA as a condition of consent. Karen has now failed to pay rent for four months. Isla seeks to recover from James under the AGA. James argues the AGA is invalid as it requires him to guarantee Karen's obligations indefinitely. Advise Isla.
Creation and Covenants.answer:
The AGA is valid, but its scope is limited by s.16 LTCA 1995. An AGA is the only permissible form of continuing liability for an outgoing tenant under a new tenancy (post-1 January 1996). It is valid where the landlord's consent to assignment was conditional on its provision and it was reasonable to require it. Under s.25 LTCA 1995, an AGA cannot require the outgoing tenant to guarantee performance beyond the immediate assignee — any provision purporting to guarantee a future assignee's obligations is void. James's AGA covers Karen's obligations only. Isla can recover the rent arrears from James under the AGA — the AGA is valid and enforceable for Karen's defaults while Karen remains the tenant.

⚠️ If Karen assigns the lease to a further assignee, James's AGA liability ceases — he only guarantees the immediate assignee.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: AGA · LTCA 1995 · S.25 IMMEDIATE ASSIGNEE ONLY · S.16 · VALID AND ENFORCEABLE · AGA CEASES ON FURTHER ASSIGNMENT

Creation and Covenants.front: Leon rented residential premises from Maya under a periodic tenancy. Maya discovered that Leon had been using one room of the flat for prostitution, in breach of a covenant against illegal or immoral use. Maya served a s.146 notice, gave Leon two weeks to remedy the breach, and then forfeited when Leon failed to do so. Leon seeks relief from forfeiture. Advise Maya on the validity of the forfeiture and Leon's prospects of relief.
Creation and Covenants.answer: Validity of forfeiture: Maya must have served a valid s.146 notice specifying the breach and requiring remedy if remediable. The key issue is whether use for immoral purposes is a remediable breach. Courts have held that a 'stigma' breach — use for immoral purposes — may be irremediable: the taint to the property cannot be undone simply by ceasing the activity. If the breach is irremediable, the s.146 notice need not require remedy — but it must still specify the breach. Two weeks' notice may be insufficient even for a remediable breach — reasonable time is required. The forfeiture is likely valid if the notice was properly served and sufficient time given. Relief from forfeiture: the court has discretion to grant relief. For a stigma breach that is irremediable, the court is much less likely to grant relief — the landlord's legitimate interest in not having the premises associated with immoral use weighs heavily. Leon's prospects of relief are poor.
Creation and Covenants.summary_line: S.146 NOTICE · STIGMA BREACH · IRREMEDIABLE · FORFEITURE VALID · RELIEF UNLIKELY · IMMORAL USE

Lease vs Licence.issue: Whether an agreement described as a 'licence' was in law a lease, where it granted exclusive possession of residential premises for a term at a rent.
Lease vs Licence.rule: Where an occupier is granted exclusive possession of residential premises for a fixed or periodic term at a rent, the agreement is a lease in law regardless of the label used by the parties.
Lease vs Licence.application: Street granted Mountford the right to occupy two rooms under an agreement called a 'personal licence'. The agreement expressly stated it was not a tenancy. The House of Lords held the substance of the agreement — exclusive possession, a term, a rent — created a lease. The label was irrelevant.
Lease vs Licence.conclusion: The agreement was a tenancy. Street v Mountford is the foundational authority on the distinction between leases and licences — substance prevails over label. It remains essential for any analysis of whether an occupier has a lease (with statutory protection) or merely a licence.
Lease vs Licence.summary_line: LEASE · LICENCE DISTINCTION · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION · SUBSTANCE OVER LABEL · STREET V MOUNTFORD

Lease vs Licence.issue: Whether two agreements described as licences granted to a couple jointly amounted to a lease, where together they gave exclusive possession of a flat.
Lease vs Licence.rule: Where two or more agreements are designed as a sham to avoid the Rent Acts by avoiding the grant of a lease, the court will look at the true substance — if together they grant exclusive possession, they create a joint tenancy lease.
Lease vs Licence.application: A landlord granted two separate 'licences' to an unmarried couple for a small flat, each licence containing a term allowing the landlord to introduce other occupiers. The House of Lords held the agreements were a sham — the couple had exclusive possession in substance, and the non-exclusive occupancy clauses were unrealistic and never intended to be operated.
Lease vs Licence.conclusion: The agreements together constituted a joint tenancy lease. Antoniades v Villiers confirmed that sham devices designed to evade the Rent Acts will be disregarded — the court examines the true nature of the arrangement, not its artificial structure.
Lease vs Licence.summary_line: SHAM LICENCE · JOINT TENANCY · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION · RENT ACTS · SUBSTANCE OVER FORM · ANTONIADES V VILLIERS

Lease vs Licence.front: Lena owns a house with her partner Marco as beneficial joint tenants. Marco is made bankrupt. His trustee in bankruptcy seeks to sell the house to pay creditors. Lena argues the house cannot be sold as their young child lives there. The bankruptcy occurred 14 months ago. Advise the trustee.
Lease vs Licence.answer: Marco's bankruptcy severed the joint tenancy — his beneficial share vested in the trustee in bankruptcy, converting the equitable joint tenancy into a tenancy in common. The trustee applies under s.335A Insolvency Act 1986 for a sale. After one year from vesting in the trustee, the court must order sale unless the circumstances are exceptional: Re Citro [1991]. Fourteen months have passed — the one-year period has expired. The presence of a young child is a relevant circumstance, but Re Citro established that ordinary human hardship (including the needs of children) does not constitute exceptional circumstances. The courts have consistently held that after one year, the creditors' interests prevail unless something truly unusual is present — beyond ordinary family hardship. The trustee is likely to obtain an order for sale. The court may allow a short postponement to enable Lena to make alternative housing arrangements, but sale will be ordered.
Lease vs Licence.summary_line: BANKRUPTCY · SEVERS JOINT TENANCY · S.335A · 14 MONTHS · AFTER ONE YEAR · EXCEPTIONAL CIRCUMSTANCES · SALE ORDERED

Lease vs Licence.front: Nina owns land in unregistered title. She grants her neighbour Owen an option to purchase the land, which Owen registers as a Class C(iv) land charge against Nina's name. Nina later sells the land to Paul, who does not search the land charges register before completion. Paul now seeks to develop the land and Owen seeks to exercise the option. Advise Paul.
Lease vs Licence.answer: Owen's option was properly registered as a Class C(iv) land charge under the Land Charges Act 1972. A registered land charge binds all purchasers — registration constitutes deemed notice to the world. Paul's failure to search the register is irrelevant: he is bound by Owen's registered option regardless of whether he had actual knowledge of it. In Midland Bank Trust Co v Green [1981], the House of Lords confirmed the strict operation of the land charges system — but there the charge was unregistered. Here the charge is registered, which gives Owen full protection. Paul takes the land subject to Owen's option to purchase. Owen may exercise the option and purchase the land on the agreed terms. Paul cannot develop the land inconsistently with Owen's rights.
Lease vs Licence.summary_line: LAND CHARGES REGISTERED · BINDS PURCHASER · FAILURE TO SEARCH IRRELEVANT · ESTATE CONTRACT · MIDLAND BANK CONTRASTED

Lease vs Licence.front: Rosa and Sam own a house in joint names. Rosa contributed 60% of the purchase price. Sam managed the household and cared for their children throughout. They kept separate bank accounts. They have now separated and dispute their beneficial shares. Rosa claims 60%; Sam claims 50%. The transfer deed contains no express declaration of beneficial interests. Advise.
Lease vs Licence.answer: The starting point under Stack v Dowden [2007] is equal beneficial shares — joint names registration raises a presumption of 50/50. Rosa must rebut this presumption by showing a different common intention inferred from the whole course of conduct. Relevant factors supporting Rosa's 60% claim: her larger financial contribution to the purchase price; separate bank accounts (indicating financial independence — a factor Stack identified as relevant). Factors supporting equal shares: Sam's substantial non-financial contributions (childcare, household management); no evidence of discussions about unequal shares; the parties' overall relationship and lifestyle. The court in Stack indicated that departing from equal shares requires 'unusual' circumstances — in most domestic cases the presumption holds. Rosa's 60% contribution alone is unlikely to be sufficient. The court may well award equal shares unless Rosa can point to additional evidence of a different intention. Under Jones v Kernott [2011], if no actual common intention can be inferred, the court may impute a fair result — which on these facts may still be close to equal.
Lease vs Licence.summary_line: STACK V DOWDEN · EQUAL SHARES · REBUTTAL · SEPARATE FINANCES · NON-FINANCIAL CONTRIBUTIONS · JONES V KERNOTT IMPUTATION

Lease vs Licence.front: Tom has been in adverse possession of registered land owned by Uma for 10 years. He applies under Schedule 6 LRA 2002 to be registered. Uma is notified and objects. Tom argues he qualifies under para 5(c) because he reasonably believed the land was his own throughout — it is a strip adjacent to his garden and the boundary was unclear. Uma argues Tom knew from year six that the land was registered to her. Advise Tom.
Lease vs Licence.answer: Tom's claim under Sch 6, para 5(c) requires that for at least 10 years immediately preceding the application, he reasonably believed the land was his own. The critical issue is his knowledge from year six. If Tom knew from year six that the land belonged to Uma, his reasonable belief cannot have subsisted for the full 10 years — it was extinguished at year six. The para 5(c) condition requires the reasonable belief to have persisted throughout the 10-year period. Knowledge that the land belongs to another is fatal to the reasonable belief requirement: it cannot be 'reasonable' to believe the land is yours when you know it is not. Tom's application under para 5(c) will fail if Uma can establish that Tom's knowledge at year six was actual or constructive. Tom's only remaining option is to wait the further two years after rejection and re-apply under the general Sch 6 procedure — but Uma can recover possession in that period to prevent this.
Lease vs Licence.summary_line: SCH 6 PARA 5(C) · REASONABLE BELIEF FAILS · KNOWLEDGE FROM YEAR SIX · FULL PERIOD REQUIRED · RECOVERY PROCEEDINGS

Lease vs Licence.front: Victor grants Wendy a right of way across his land by a properly executed deed. The deed is completed and the right of way is registered at HMLR as an appurtenant easement. Victor later sells his land to Xavier. Xavier erects a gate across the right of way and argues Wendy's easement does not bind him as it was a personal right only. Advise Wendy.
Lease vs Licence.answer: Wendy has a legal easement — granted by deed and registered at HMLR as a substantive disposition under s.27 LRA 2002. A registered legal easement is a legal interest binding the world — it is not personal but proprietary. Xavier, as purchaser of the servient land, takes subject to all registered interests. The registration of the easement on the register means Xavier had constructive (and likely actual) notice of it. Xavier's argument that it was a personal right fails — an easement granted by deed and registered is a proprietary right running with both the dominant and servient land. Wendy may seek an injunction to require Xavier to remove the gate and an order for damages for any loss caused by the obstruction. Xavier cannot interfere with a registered legal easement.
Lease vs Licence.summary_line: REGISTERED LEGAL EASEMENT · S.27 LRA 2002 · PROPRIETARY RIGHT · BINDS PURCHASER · INJUNCTION · OBSTRUCTION

Lease vs Licence.front: Yara owned a farm. She told her nephew Zac repeatedly that she intended to leave him the farmhouse and 10 acres when she died. Zac, in reliance on this, gave up a career in finance and moved to the farm, working it for 12 years without pay. Yara executed a will leaving everything to charity. She then sold the farmhouse to a developer, Atlas Ltd, who had no notice of Zac's claim. Zac was living in a cottage on the farm (not the farmhouse) at the time of sale. Advise Zac.
Lease vs Licence.answer: Zac's proprietary estoppel claim is strong: assurance (repeated statements about inheriting), reliance (giving up a career, moving to the farm), and detriment (12 years' unpaid work) are clearly established. The equity arises. The question is whether it binds Atlas Ltd as purchaser of the farmhouse. In registered land, Zac's equity should have been protected by entry of a notice on the register — it was not. Zac was not in actual occupation of the farmhouse at the time of sale (he lived in the cottage) — so he cannot rely on Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002 overriding interest for the farmhouse specifically. Atlas, as a registered disponee for valuable consideration without notice, takes the farmhouse free of Zac's unprotected equity under s.29 LRA 2002. Zac's remedy lies against Yara's estate — the equity is enforceable against Yara personally and her estate. The will leaving everything to charity does not defeat the equity arising before the will was made. Zac should claim against the estate for compensation representing the value of the farmhouse and land he was promised.
Lease vs Licence.summary_line: PROPRIETARY ESTOPPEL · EQUITY ESTABLISHED · FARMHOUSE NOT OCCUPIED · S.29 PURCHASER FREE · REMEDY AGAINST ESTATE

Equitable Leases.issue: Whether a specifically enforceable agreement for a lease conferred on the tenant the same rights as if a formal lease had been granted.
Equitable Leases.rule: Equity treats a specifically enforceable agreement for a lease as equivalent to a lease — 'equity looks on that as done which ought to be done.' The tenant holds under an equitable lease on the agreed terms.
Equitable Leases.application: Lonsdale agreed to grant Walsh a 7-year lease of a mill at a rent payable in advance. No formal lease was executed. Walsh paid rent in arrear. When Lonsdale distrained for rent in advance, Walsh argued there was no formal lease authorising this. The Court of Appeal held the equitable lease entitled Lonsdale to distrain on the agreed terms.
Equitable Leases.conclusion: Lonsdale could distrain for rent in advance. Walsh v Lonsdale established that an equitable lease (arising from a specifically enforceable contract) carries the same terms as the formal lease would have done. Its limitations — discretionary remedy, no automatic binding of third parties — distinguish it from a legal lease.
Equitable Leases.summary_line: EQUITABLE LEASE · SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE · AGREEMENT FOR LEASE · EQUITY LOOKS ON AS DONE · WALSH V LONSDALE

Forfeiture and Waiver.issue: Whether a landlord who demanded and accepted rent with knowledge of a breach of covenant had waived the right to forfeit for that breach.
Forfeiture and Waiver.rule: Waiver of the right to forfeit occurs automatically where the landlord, with knowledge of the breach, does any unequivocal act recognising the continued existence of the lease — including demanding or accepting rent. Waiver does not require the landlord to intend to waive.
Forfeiture and Waiver.application: The landlord discovered that the tenant had used the premises for immoral purposes (a breach of covenant). The landlord's agents, aware of the breach, sent a rent demand and accepted rent. The Court of Appeal held waiver had occurred automatically — the landlord's intention was irrelevant.
Forfeiture and Waiver.conclusion: The right to forfeit for that breach was waived. The case establishes that waiver is automatic on acceptance of rent with knowledge — landlords must act promptly on discovering a breach and must not accept rent in the interim.
Forfeiture and Waiver.summary_line: WAIVER · FORFEITURE · ACCEPTANCE OF RENT · KNOWLEDGE OF BREACH · AUTOMATIC · LANDLORD AND TENANT · WOOLGAR

Leasehold Covenants — Old Rules.issue: Whether the benefit and burden of covenants in a lease passed to assignees of the original parties.
Leasehold Covenants — Old Rules.rule: The benefit and burden of leasehold covenants that 'touch and concern' the land pass automatically to assignees of both the lease and the reversion — by virtue of privity of estate.
Leasehold Covenants — Old Rules.application: A lessee covenanted to build a wall on the demised land. The lessee assigned the lease. The question was whether the assignee was bound by the covenant. The court held that covenants touching and concerning the land ran with the lease and could be enforced against and by assignees.
Leasehold Covenants — Old Rules.conclusion: The assignee was bound. Spencer's Case established the foundational principle that leasehold covenants touching and concerning the land run with the lease under the old rules — the principle of privity of estate. It remains relevant for pre-1996 leases and as the doctrinal foundation for the LTCA 1995 reforms.
Leasehold Covenants — Old Rules.summary_line: LEASEHOLD COVENANTS · TOUCH AND CONCERN · PRIVITY OF ESTATE · OLD RULES · SPENCER'S CASE · ASSIGNEES

Essential Characteristics of a Lease.issue: Whether a tenancy 'for the duration of the war' satisfied the requirement that a lease must have a certain maximum duration.
Essential Characteristics of a Lease.rule: A valid lease requires certainty of maximum duration — the term must be certain or capable of being rendered certain at the outset. A term whose maximum duration cannot be known in advance is not a valid lease.
Essential Characteristics of a Lease.application: A landlord granted a tenancy 'for the duration of the war.' The Court of Appeal held this was not a valid lease — the maximum duration of the war was uncertain at the time of the grant, and therefore the term was not certain.
Essential Characteristics of a Lease.conclusion: The purported lease was not valid as a term of years. Lace v Chantler established the certainty of term requirement for leases. It has since been applied and confirmed — a tenancy 'until the land is required for development' similarly fails: Prudential Assurance Co v London Residuary Body [1992].
Essential Characteristics of a Lease.summary_line: LEASE · CERTAINTY OF TERM · MAXIMUM DURATION · UNCERTAIN TERM INVALID · LACE V CHANTLER

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: What is the academic debate on the inference vs imputation distinction in Stack v Dowden and Jones v Kernott, and has the Supreme Court drawn the line in the right place?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
Stack v Dowden [2007] and Jones v Kernott [2011] established that beneficial shares in domestic property disputes are determined by the common intention of the parties, inferred from the whole course of conduct. Jones v Kernott added that where actual intention cannot be inferred, the court may impute an intention — attribute to the parties the intention they would reasonably be taken to have had.

The inference/imputation distinction has attracted significant academic attention. Simon Gardner and Katherine Davidson ('The Supreme Court on Family Homes' (2012) 128 LQR 178) argued that the distinction is illusory in practice — 'imputation' is simply inference dressed up as fairness, and the Supreme Court has introduced a covert discretion that lacks doctrinal coherence. They argue the law has moved from a property-based analysis (what did the parties intend?) to a quasi-redistributive exercise (what is fair?) without acknowledging the shift.

Martin Dixon has argued the opposite — that imputation is necessary and honest. Given the informality of domestic arrangements, requiring strict inference of intention is unrealistic and produces harsh outcomes. Imputation allows the court to achieve a result that reflects the parties' overall relationship rather than fixating on financial contributions.

A further criticism, advanced by Anne Barlow and others, is that Stack and Kernott still privilege financial contributions over non-financial ones (childcare, domestic labour) despite stating otherwise — in practice, courts still return to money as the primary metric.

The unresolved tension: the Supreme Court has created a two-stage test (infer then impute) but the boundary between the stages is unclear. Whether English law has struck the right balance between certainty and fairness in domestic property disputes remains genuinely contested.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: INFERENCE VS IMPUTATION · STACK V DOWDEN · JONES V KERNOTT · GARDNER AND DAVIDSON · MARTIN DIXON · ANNE BARLOW · COVERT DISCRETION

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Mia has a beneficial interest in a property that is being registered for the first time following a transfer to Nathan. Mia is in actual occupation but her occupation would not have been obvious on a reasonably careful inspection — she had been staying temporarily and left most of her belongings elsewhere. Nathan argues Mia's interest does not override because the inspectability condition in Schedule 3, paragraph 2 LRA 2002 is not satisfied.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: The scenario mentions Sch 3, para 2 and the inspectability condition — students apply it and conclude Mia's interest does not override Nathan's title.
Trap Cards.rule: The Reality: This is a first registration, not a registered disposition. Schedule 3 applies only on a registered disposition (a transfer between registered proprietors). Schedule 1 LRA 2002 governs overriding interests on first registration. Under Sch 1, para 2, the interest of a person in actual occupation overrides first registration without any inspectability condition — the Sch 3 refinements (inspectability, enquiry and failure to disclose) do not apply to Sch 1.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the reference to Sch 3, para 2 and inspectability — which is the most familiar formulation of the actual occupation overriding interest rule. Students apply Sch 3 by reflex without asking which schedule applies. The distinction between first registration (Sch 1) and registered dispositions (Sch 3) is fundamental and specifically tested. Mia's actual occupation alone is sufficient under Sch 1 — the inspectability condition is irrelevant.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Schedule 1 (first registration) and Schedule 3 (registered dispositions) are not identical. The inspectability and enquiry conditions in Sch 3, para 2 do not apply to Sch 1, para 2. Always identify whether the transaction is a first registration or a registered disposition before applying the overriding interest rules.
Trap Cards.summary_line: SCH 1 VS SCH 3 · FIRST REGISTRATION · NO INSPECTABILITY CONDITION · ACTUAL OCCUPATION SUFFICIENT · LRA 2002

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Oliver owns a farm and sells the western half to Priya. Before the sale, Oliver regularly used a track across the western half to reach the eastern half he retained. The track was clearly visible. No express easement was granted in the transfer. Oliver now claims an implied easement over the western half (Priya's land) under the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: There is a visible track, prior use by Oliver, and the track is necessary for reasonable enjoyment of the retained eastern half. All the Wheeldon v Burrows ingredients appear to be present — students conclude Oliver has an implied easement.
Trap Cards.rule: The Reality: Wheeldon v Burrows [1879] operates on a grant only — it implies easements in favour of the grantee of part of the seller's land, over the part retained by the seller. It does not operate on a reservation — it does not imply easements in favour of the grantor (seller) over the land they have sold. Oliver is the grantor seeking to reserve an easement over the land he sold. Wheeldon v Burrows does not assist him.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the presence of all the Wheeldon v Burrows factual ingredients — continuous and apparent use, prior quasi-easement, reasonable necessity. Students apply the rule without checking the direction of the implied easement. Wheeldon runs from the seller to the buyer (grantor → grantee). Oliver needs a reservation, not a grant. Easements of necessity or common intention may be implied on reservation, but Wheeldon v Burrows is not available to him.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Wheeldon v Burrows implies easements on grant only — in favour of the grantee over the grantor's retained land. It never implies easements on reservation in favour of the grantor over the land sold. Always check the direction of the claimed easement.
Trap Cards.summary_line: WHEELDON V BURROWS · GRANT ONLY · NOT RESERVATION · DIRECTION OF EASEMENT · GRANTOR CANNOT RELY

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: How is a legal mortgage of registered land created?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
Under the LRA 2002, the only method of creating a legal mortgage of registered land is by a charge by deed expressed to be by way of legal mortgage (s.23(1)(a) LRA 2002). The mortgage must be:

1. created by deed
2. registered at HMLR as a substantive registration (s.27(2)(f)) — it does not take effect as a legal mortgage until registration.

⚠️ The old method of creating a mortgage by demise (granting a long lease to the mortgagee) is no longer available for registered land. Until registration, the charge operates in equity only.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: LEGAL MORTGAGE · REGISTERED LAND · CHARGE BY DEED · S.27(2)(F) LRA 2002 · REGISTRATION ESSENTIAL · EQUITABLE UNTIL REGISTERED

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What is the mortgagee's right to take possession, and what limits apply?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
A mortgagee has a right to take possession of the mortgaged property from the moment the mortgage is created (Four-Maids Ltd v Dudley Marshall Properties Ltd [1957]) — even without any default by the mortgagor. However, the courts exercise a discretion to adjourn possession proceedings under s.36 Administration of Justice Act 1970 for residential mortgages where: the mortgagor is likely to be able to repay the arrears (or remedy any other default) within a reasonable period.

⚠️ Section 36 applies only to dwelling houses — not commercial mortgages. The reasonable period is typically assessed against the remaining mortgage term.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: MORTGAGEE POSSESSION · FROM CREATION · FOUR-MAIDS · S.36 AJA 1970 · DWELLING HOUSE · REASONABLE PERIOD · ADJOURN

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: When does the mortgagee's power of sale arise and become exercisable?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
The power of sale arises (exists) under s.101(1)(i) LPA 1925 when: the mortgage is by deed and the mortgage money is due (i.e. the legal date of redemption has passed). The power becomes exercisable (may lawfully be used) under s.103 LPA 1925 when one of three conditions is met:

1. notice requiring payment has been served and the mortgagor has defaulted for 3 months; OR
2. interest is 2 months in arrear; OR
3. there has been a breach of a mortgage covenant other than the promise to repay.

⚠️

**Key distinction:** a mortgagee who exercises the power of sale before it is exercisable (even though it has arisen) is liable in damages to the mortgagor — but the sale is still valid and the purchaser takes good title (s.104 LPA 1925).
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: POWER OF SALE · ARISING S.101 · EXERCISABLE S.103 · THREE CONDITIONS · S.104 PURCHASER PROTECTED · LIABILITY TO MORTGAGOR

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What duty does a mortgagee owe when exercising the power of sale?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
A mortgagee exercising the power of sale owes a duty to the mortgagor (and anyone with a subsequent interest) to take reasonable care to obtain the true market value of the property at the time of sale: Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971]. The mortgagee need not wait for the best market conditions, but must not sell at a gross undervalue through negligence or wilful disregard.

⚠️ The duty is not a fiduciary duty — it is tortious in nature. The mortgagee may sell to a connected party but must still obtain the best price: Standard Chartered Bank v Walker [1982]. The mortgagee cannot sell to itself.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: MORTGAGEE DUTY · TRUE MARKET VALUE · CUCKMERE BRICK · TORTIOUS NOT FIDUCIARY · NOT TO ITSELF · REASONABLE CARE

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: In what order are proceeds of a mortgagee's sale distributed?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
Under s.105 LPA 1925, proceeds of sale are applied in the following order:

1. Discharge of any prior mortgages — any mortgages with higher priority than the selling mortgagee must be discharged first
2. Costs and expenses of the sale
3. Discharge of the mortgage debt (principal, interest, and costs) of the selling mortgagee
4. Subsequent mortgagees in order of priority
5. Any surplus is paid to the mortgagor.

⚠️ If the sale proceeds are insufficient to discharge the mortgage (negative equity), the mortgagor remains personally liable for the shortfall under their personal covenant to repay.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: S.105 LPA 1925 · PROCEEDS ORDER · PRIOR MORTGAGES · COSTS · SELLING MORTGAGEE · SUBSEQUENT · SURPLUS TO MORTGAGOR

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What is the mortgagor's equity of redemption, and what does the court protect?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
The equity of redemption is the mortgagor's equitable right to redeem the mortgage and recover the property free of the charge on repayment of the debt. Equity protects against 'clogs and fetters' — provisions that seek to prevent, restrict, or postpone redemption in an unconscionable way. The court will not allow:

1. an irredeemable mortgage (or redemption only at the mortgagee's option)
2. a collateral advantage that continues after redemption (subject to exceptions for bona fide commercial bargains): Noakes v Rice [1902]
3. a postponement of the right to redeem for an unreasonably long period.

⚠️ In commercial mortgages between sophisticated parties, courts are more willing to uphold postponement clauses as a matter of freedom of contract: Knightsbridge Estates v Byrne [1939].
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: EQUITY OF REDEMPTION · CLOGS AND FETTERS · NOAKES V RICE · COLLATERAL ADVANTAGE · KNIGHTSBRIDGE · COMMERCIAL MORTGAGE

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What is the appointment of a receiver, and when is it used as a mortgagee remedy?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
A mortgagee may appoint a receiver under s.101(1)(iii) and s.109 LPA 1925 when the same conditions as the power of sale apply (s.103). The receiver is appointed to manage the property and collect income (rents) on behalf of the mortgagee.

⚠️ The receiver is deemed to be the agent of the mortgagor — not the mortgagee. This means the mortgagee avoids direct liability for the receiver's acts. Appointment of a receiver is particularly useful for commercial or investment properties where the mortgagee wants the income stream without taking possession directly.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: RECEIVER · S.109 LPA 1925 · AGENT OF MORTGAGOR · COMMERCIAL PROPERTY · INCOME · MORTGAGEE NOT LIABLE

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What is foreclosure as a mortgagee remedy, and why is it rarely used?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
Foreclosure is a court order that extinguishes the mortgagor's equity of redemption — the mortgagee becomes the absolute owner of the property without the obligation to account for any surplus value. It requires a court order and the court can order an account, allow the mortgagor further time, or order a sale instead. It is rarely used because:

1. the court almost always orders a sale instead
2. a mortgagee who forecloses takes the property subject to its liabilities (e.g. if the property is worth less than expected)
3. if the mortgagor applies to have the foreclosure reopened, the court has jurisdiction to do so even after the order.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: FORECLOSURE · EXTINGUISHES EQUITY OF REDEMPTION · COURT ORDER · RARELY USED · SALE PREFERRED · REOPEN RISK

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: How is priority between competing mortgages determined in registered land?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
In registered land, priority between competing mortgages is determined by the order of registration at HMLR, not the order of creation (s.48 LRA 2002). A mortgage that is created first but registered second ranks behind one registered first.

⚠️

**Exception:** a later mortgagee who had notice of a prior unregistered mortgage does not take priority — actual or constructive notice of a prior unprotected interest may affect the later mortgagee's conscience (s.29 LRA 2002 does not protect a later mortgagee with notice against a prior unregistered charge in all circumstances). In practice, all legal mortgages should be registered promptly to protect priority.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: MORTGAGE PRIORITY · S.48 LRA 2002 · ORDER OF REGISTRATION · NOT CREATION · NOTICE EXCEPTION · PROMPT REGISTRATION

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What is the doctrine of undue influence in the context of mortgages, and how does it affect the mortgagee?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
Where a mortgagor signs a mortgage under the undue influence (or misrepresentation) of a third party (typically a co-habiting partner), the mortgagee may be unable to enforce the mortgage against that mortgagor if the mortgagee had notice of the risk. The leading case is Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2) [2001]: the House of Lords held a mortgagee is put on notice whenever a wife (or cohabitee) stands surety for her husband's (or partner's) debts. To protect itself, the mortgagee must take steps to ensure the surety has received independent legal advice. If the mortgagee fails to do so, it is fixed with constructive notice of the undue influence and cannot enforce the mortgage against the surety.

⚠️ Etridge does not require actual undue influence — the risk is presumed in certain relationships.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: UNDUE INFLUENCE · ETRIDGE · SURETY · CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE · INDEPENDENT LEGAL ADVICE · MORTGAGEE PROTECTION

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What protection does s.36 Administration of Justice Act 1970 give to a residential mortgagor?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
Section 36 AJA 1970 empowers the court to adjourn possession proceedings, or to suspend or postpone a possession order, where the mortgaged property is a dwelling house and the mortgagor is likely to be able within a reasonable period to pay any sums due under the mortgage or remedy any default. The court may impose conditions (e.g. payment of current instalments plus a contribution to arrears).

⚠️ Section 36 applies only to dwelling houses and only where the mortgagee seeks possession by court proceedings. It does not apply to commercial mortgages. Under s.8 AJA 1973, 'sums due' means the whole outstanding mortgage debt only where the entire loan has been called in — if only arrears are due, s.36 is assessed against those arrears.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: S.36 AJA 1970 · DWELLING HOUSE · ADJOURN POSSESSION · REASONABLE PERIOD · S.8 AJA 1973 · ARREARS ONLY

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What is tacking, and when may a mortgagee tack further advances in priority?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
Tacking allows a mortgagee to add further advances to the original mortgage and claim priority for those advances over intervening mortgages. In registered land (s.49 LRA 2002), a mortgagee may tack further advances in priority if:

1. the further advance is made pursuant to an obligation to do so
2. the mortgagee had no notice of the intervening mortgage at the time of the further advance; or
3. all the mortgagees agree.

⚠️ In practice, most modern mortgages include an 'all monies' clause securing all sums owed — this is treated as an obligation to make further advances, making tacking straightforward. A mortgagee who receives notice of a subsequent mortgage cannot tack further discretionary advances in priority to it.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: TACKING · S.49 LRA 2002 · FURTHER ADVANCES · ALL MONIES CLAUSE · NOTICE OF INTERVENING MORTGAGE · PRIORITY

Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.front: What is the effect of a mortgagor's bankruptcy on a mortgage?
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.answer:
On the mortgagor's bankruptcy, the mortgaged property vests in the trustee in bankruptcy. The mortgagee's security is not disturbed — the mortgage remains valid and enforceable against the property. The trustee in bankruptcy takes the property subject to the mortgage. The trustee can sell the property but must discharge the mortgage from the proceeds (or the mortgagee can exercise its own power of sale).

⚠️ If the mortgagor is bankrupt and the property is jointly owned, the trustee may apply to court under s.335A Insolvency Act 1986 (which applies TOLATA 1996 criteria) to force a sale — after one year, there is a presumption that the interests of the creditors outweigh those of the family, and sale will normally be ordered.
Mortgagee Remedies and Mortgagor Protect.summary_line: BANKRUPTCY · TRUSTEE IN BANKRUPTCY · MORTGAGE SURVIVES · S.335A INSOLVENCY ACT · ONE YEAR PRESUMPTION · SALE

Power of Sale and Duty on Sale.front: Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971]
Power of Sale and Duty on Sale.issue: Whether a mortgagee exercising the power of sale owed a duty to the mortgagor to obtain the true market value of the property.
Power of Sale and Duty on Sale.rule: A mortgagee exercising the power of sale owes a duty in tort to the mortgagor (and all others with an interest in the equity of redemption) to take reasonable care to obtain the true market value of the mortgaged property at the time of sale.
Power of Sale and Duty on Sale.application: The mortgagee sold land with planning permission for a large number of houses but failed to advertise this permission in the sale particulars. The property was sold at a substantial undervalue. The Court of Appeal held the mortgagee was in breach of its duty — the failure to advertise the planning permission was negligent.
Power of Sale and Duty on Sale.conclusion: The mortgagee was liable in damages for the shortfall. Cuckmere Brick established the mortgagee's duty of care on sale — not a fiduciary duty, but a tortious duty to take care to obtain the proper price. The mortgagee need not wait for the best market conditions but must not negligently undervalue the property.
Power of Sale and Duty on Sale.summary_line: MORTGAGEE · POWER OF SALE · DUTY OF CARE · TRUE MARKET VALUE · NEGLIGENT SALE · TORT · CUCKMERE BRICK
Power of Sale and Duty on Sale.subtopic: Power of Sale and Duty on Sale

Undue Influence and Mortgages.front: Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2) [2001]
Undue Influence and Mortgages.issue: When is a bank fixed with constructive notice of undue influence exercised over a surety, and what steps must it take to avoid being bound?
Undue Influence and Mortgages.rule: A bank is put on inquiry whenever a wife (or cohabitee) stands surety for her husband's (or partner's) debts. To avoid being fixed with constructive notice of undue influence, the bank must take reasonable steps to ensure the surety has received independent legal advice.
Undue Influence and Mortgages.application: A series of cases were heard together involving wives who had mortgaged the matrimonial home to secure their husbands' business debts, allegedly under undue influence. The House of Lords laid down detailed guidance on when banks are put on inquiry and the steps required — including ensuring the solicitor advising the wife explains the risks in a meaningful way.
Undue Influence and Mortgages.conclusion: Banks must take specified steps when a surety transaction raises the possibility of undue influence. Etridge is the definitive modern authority on undue influence in mortgage transactions — it sets out a comprehensive framework balancing the bank's commercial interests against protection of vulnerable sureties.
Undue Influence and Mortgages.summary_line: UNDUE INFLUENCE · MORTGAGE · CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE · INDEPENDENT LEGAL ADVICE · SURETY · ETRIDGE

Equity of Redemption.issue: Whether a clause postponing the mortgagor's right to redeem for 40 years was an unconscionable clog on the equity of redemption.
Equity of Redemption.rule: In a commercial mortgage between sophisticated parties, a postponement of the right to redeem for a long period is not necessarily a clog on the equity of redemption — courts are more willing to uphold such clauses as a genuine commercial bargain.
Equity of Redemption.application: A company mortgaged property and agreed not to redeem for 40 years in return for a lower interest rate. When interest rates fell, the company sought to redeem early. The Court of Appeal held the postponement was a genuine commercial transaction between parties of equal bargaining power and was not unconscionable.
Equity of Redemption.conclusion: The postponement clause was valid. Knightsbridge Estates confirms that freedom of contract applies in commercial mortgage transactions — equity's intervention is reserved for unconscionable terms, not merely unfavourable ones. It is distinguished from residential cases where courts are more protective.
Equity of Redemption.summary_line: MORTGAGE · EQUITY OF REDEMPTION · POSTPONEMENT · COMMERCIAL BARGAIN · CLOG · KNIGHTSBRIDGE

Equity of Redemption.issue: Whether a collateral advantage in a mortgage (requiring the mortgagor to take beer exclusively from the mortgagee after redemption) was void as a clog on the equity of redemption.
Equity of Redemption.rule: A collateral advantage in a mortgage that continues after redemption is void as a clog on the equity of redemption — once redeemed, the mortgagor must be free of all the mortgage terms.
Equity of Redemption.application: Rice mortgaged a public house to Noakes (a brewery) and agreed as a collateral term to take beer exclusively from Noakes for the entire lease term, including after any redemption. The House of Lords held this was a clog — the mortgagor must be able to redeem free of all obligations.
Equity of Redemption.conclusion: The tie extending beyond redemption was void. Noakes v Rice is the leading authority on clogs on the equity of redemption arising from collateral advantages that survive redemption. It protects the mortgagor's right to redeem and have the land free of all mortgage obligations.
Equity of Redemption.summary_line: EQUITY OF REDEMPTION · CLOG · COLLATERAL ADVANTAGE · POST-REDEMPTION TIE VOID · NOAKES V RICE

Possession.front: Four-Maids Ltd v Dudley Marshall Properties Ltd [1957]
Possession.issue: Whether a mortgagee's right to take possession arose only on default or from the moment the mortgage was created.
Possession.rule: A mortgagee has the right to take possession of the mortgaged property from the moment the mortgage is created — the right does not depend on any default by the mortgagor.
Possession.application: The mortgagee sought possession without any default having occurred. Harman J held that the mortgagee's right to possession arises as soon as the mortgage ink is dry — default is not a condition precedent. In practice the courts would exercise discretion to adjourn (now under s.36 AJA 1970 for dwelling houses).
Possession.conclusion: The right to possession exists from the moment of mortgage creation. Four-Maids is significant for establishing the theoretical breadth of the mortgagee's right — it is the starting point before statutory and equitable controls (principally s.36 AJA 1970) are applied to residential mortgages.
Possession.summary_line: MORTGAGEE · RIGHT TO POSSESSION · FROM CREATION · NO DEFAULT REQUIRED · S.36 AJA 1970 · FOUR-MAIDS

Mortgagee Remedies.front: Nick borrows £200,000 from Omega Bank, secured by a mortgage over his house. Nick falls into arrears of £8,000. Omega Bank writes to Nick demanding payment and, after three months without response, instructs estate agents to sell the house. Nick argues the bank has no right to sell. Advise Omega Bank.
Mortgagee Remedies.answer: Omega Bank must check whether the power of sale has both arisen and become exercisable. The power arises under s.101(1)(i) LPA 1925 when the mortgage is by deed and the mortgage money is due (the legal redemption date has passed — usually a short period after the mortgage starts). The power becomes exercisable under s.103 LPA 1925 when one of three conditions is met: (1) written notice requiring payment has been served and the mortgagor has been in default for three months — Omega Bank wrote demanding payment and three months have passed, satisfying this condition. Both the power of sale has arisen and is now exercisable. Omega Bank may sell. It must obtain the true market value (Cuckmere Brick). Proceeds are applied in the s.105 order — costs, then the mortgage debt, then any surplus to Nick.
Mortgagee Remedies.summary_line: POWER OF SALE · S.101 ARISES · S.103 EXERCISABLE · THREE MONTHS DEFAULT · CUCKMERE DUTY · S.105 ORDER

Mortgagee Remedies.front: Paula mortgaged her house to Quantum Bank. Paula defaults and Quantum Bank exercises the power of sale immediately after Paula misses one monthly payment, without serving any notice or waiting for any further conditions. The bank sells the house to a third party purchaser at what appears to be market value. Paula claims the sale was unlawful. Advise Paula and the purchaser.
Mortgagee Remedies.answer:
The power of sale had arisen (mortgage by deed, mortgage money due) but had not become exercisable under s.103 LPA 1925 — none of the three conditions were met (no three-month notice and default; interest was not two months in arrear; no other breach). Quantum Bank exercised the power before it was exercisable. Paula's position: the bank is liable to Paula in damages for breach of its statutory duty — exercising the power prematurely is a breach. Paula can claim compensation for any loss suffered. The purchaser's position: under s.104 LPA 1925, a purchaser from a mortgagee is not concerned to enquire whether the power has become exercisable. The purchaser takes good title regardless of the bank's breach.

⚠️ This is a critical distinction — the bank's liability to the mortgagor and the purchaser's good title are entirely separate questions.
Mortgagee Remedies.summary_line: POWER OF SALE BEFORE EXERCISABLE · S.103 · MORTGAGEE LIABLE IN DAMAGES · S.104 PURCHASER GOOD TITLE · KEY DISTINCTION

Mortgagee Remedies.front: Rachel mortgaged her house to Sterling Bank. The bank exercises the power of sale and instructs estate agents, who recommend a guide price of £350,000. The bank accepts an offer of £275,000 from the first viewer without further marketing. Rachel claims the bank sold at an undervalue. Advise Rachel.
Mortgagee Remedies.answer: A mortgagee exercising the power of sale owes a duty to the mortgagor to take reasonable care to obtain the true market value of the property at the time of sale: Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971]. The duty is tortious — it is not a fiduciary duty, so the bank need not obtain the best possible price, but must not negligently undervalue. On the facts: the estate agents recommended £350,000 and the bank accepted £275,000 — a shortfall of £75,000 — without further marketing. Accepting the first offer at a substantial discount below the recommended guide price, without any further steps to expose the property to the market, is likely to constitute a breach of the duty of care. Rachel can claim damages for the shortfall between the true market value and the actual sale price. The bank should have conducted proper marketing before accepting an offer.
Mortgagee Remedies.summary_line: CUCKMERE BRICK · TRUE MARKET VALUE · FIRST OFFER INSUFFICIENT · NO FURTHER MARKETING · BREACH · DAMAGES FOR SHORTFALL

Mortgagee Remedies.front: Sam and his wife Tina jointly own their home, mortgaged to Unity Bank. Unknown to Tina, Sam forged Tina's signature on additional loan documents extending the mortgage. The bank did not take any steps to ensure Tina received independent legal advice. Sam has defaulted and the bank seeks possession against both Sam and Tina. Advise Tina.
Mortgagee Remedies.answer:
Tina may be able to resist the bank's claim on the ground that the extended mortgage was procured by Sam's undue influence (or misrepresentation), and the bank is fixed with constructive notice of that risk. Under Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2) [2001], a bank is put on inquiry whenever a wife stands surety for her husband's debts. The bank must take reasonable steps — specifically, ensuring Tina received independent legal advice from a solicitor who explained the risks. Here, the bank took no such steps. The bank is fixed with constructive notice of Sam's wrongdoing. Tina can set aside the additional mortgage as against the bank to the extent of her share.

⚠️ The original mortgage (if properly executed) remains valid. Only the additional loan, procured by forgery without the bank taking protective steps, is voidable by Tina.
Mortgagee Remedies.summary_line: UNDUE INFLUENCE · ETRIDGE · FORGERY · CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE · NO INDEPENDENT ADVICE · VOIDABLE SURETY SHARE

Mortgagee Remedies.front: Victor mortgaged his commercial property to Westgate Bank in 2015. The mortgage deed provides that Victor cannot redeem the mortgage for 25 years. Victor now (2026) seeks to redeem early, arguing this is an unconscionable clog on the equity of redemption. Advise Victor.
Mortgagee Remedies.answer:
Victor faces a high bar. The equity of redemption is protected against clogs — provisions that prevent, restrict, or postpone redemption unconscionably. However, the courts distinguish between residential and commercial mortgages. In Knightsbridge Estates v Byrne [1939], a 40-year postponement of the right to redeem in a commercial mortgage between sophisticated parties was upheld as a genuine commercial bargain — not unconscionable. The court will consider:

1. the nature of the parties and whether they were of equal bargaining power
2. whether Victor received a commercial advantage in return (e.g. a lower interest rate)
3. whether the term is unreasonable in all the circumstances.

A 25-year postponement in a commercial context is within the range upheld in Knightsbridge. Victor is unlikely to succeed unless he can show the term was imposed unconscionably or the parties were not of equal bargaining power. Legal advice at the time of the mortgage is relevant.
Mortgagee Remedies.summary_line: EQUITY OF REDEMPTION · CLOG · POSTPONEMENT · KNIGHTSBRIDGE · COMMERCIAL MORTGAGE · FREEDOM OF CONTRACT

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: What is the numerus clausus principle, why does English land law maintain it, and is the academic case for relaxation persuasive?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
The numerus clausus principle holds that property rights in land are confined to a closed list — parties cannot create new proprietary rights by agreement. In English land law, the list is set by s.1 LPA 1925 (legal estates and interests) and by equity (equitable interests recognised by the courts). Novel rights that do not fit within a recognised category are treated as personal obligations only: Hill v Tupper [1863].

The leading academic defence of numerus clausus is Merrill and Smith ('Optimal Standardisation in the Law of Property: The Numerus Clausus Principle' (2000) 110 Yale LJ 1). They argue the principle serves an 'information cost' function: standardising property rights reduces the cost to third parties (purchasers, lenders, neighbours) of ascertaining what rights attach to land. If parties could create idiosyncratic proprietary rights, the burden of investigating title would be enormous.
