The case for relaxation has been made by Ben McFarlane and others, who argue that the principle is applied inconsistently in English law — proprietary estoppel, for example, generates flexible remedies that do not fit neatly into existing categories. McFarlane ('The Structure of Property Law' (2008)) argues that the distinction between proprietary and personal rights is not determined by the numerus clausus but by whether a right has been acquired through a recognised route.

Comparative note: civil law systems (France, Germany) apply a strict numerus clausus; common law systems vary. Scottish law has moved toward a more principled property law framework through the Property Law Act reforms.

The unresolved tension: numerus clausus provides certainty but may prevent the law from responding to new social and commercial needs. Whether the closed list should be expanded (and by whom — courts or Parliament) is an ongoing debate.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: NUMERUS CLAUSUS · MERRILL AND SMITH · INFORMATION COST · MCFARLANE · CLOSED LIST · HILL V TUPPER · SCOTTISH REFORM

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Quinn is the landlord of a commercial lease. Her tenant Rob breaches a repairing covenant — the premises have been in poor condition for two years. Quinn discovers the breach and accepts the next quarter's rent. Quinn's solicitor advises her she has permanently waived her right to forfeit for the disrepair and should pursue other remedies instead.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: Quinn accepted rent after discovering the breach — waiver appears to have occurred under Central Estates v Woolgar (No 2) [1972]. The solicitor's advice feels correct: waiver has destroyed the right to forfeit.
Trap Cards.rule: The Reality: The effect of waiver depends on whether the breach is once-and-for-all or continuing. Disrepair is a continuing breach — it exists afresh each day the premises remain unrepaired. Accepting rent waives the right to forfeit for the breach as it existed up to the date of acceptance. It does not waive future breaches — a fresh right to forfeit arises each day the disrepair continues. Quinn can serve a new s.146 notice and forfeit for the ongoing failure to repair.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the acceptance of rent, which triggers the waiver reflex. Students apply waiver without asking whether the breach is continuing or once-and-for-all. For continuing breaches, waiver only bars forfeiture for past instances — it does not permanently destroy the right. The solicitor's advice is wrong. Quinn should serve a fresh s.146 notice immediately, specifying the current state of disrepair as the breach.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Waiver permanently destroys the right to forfeit only for once-and-for-all breaches. For continuing breaches (disrepair, ongoing unlawful use), a fresh right to forfeit arises each day — acceptance of rent waives only the past breach, not the ongoing one.
Trap Cards.summary_line: WAIVER · CONTINUING BREACH · ONCE-AND-FOR-ALL · DISREPAIR CONTINUES · FRESH RIGHT TO FORFEIT DAILY

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: What is the distinction between a joint tenancy and a tenancy in common?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
A joint tenancy is characterised by the four unities (possession, interest, title, time) and the right of survivorship (ius accrescendi) — on death, the deceased's share automatically passes to the surviving joint tenants, not under their will or intestacy. A tenancy in common means each owner holds a distinct (undivided) share which can be separately disposed of — there is no right of survivorship.

⚠️ At law, co-owners must hold as joint tenants (s.1(6) LPA 1925). In equity, they may hold as joint tenants or tenants in common. Where equity is tenancy in common, the legal joint tenancy is held on trust for the tenants in common in their respective shares.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: JOINT TENANCY · TENANCY IN COMMON · SURVIVORSHIP · FOUR UNITIES · S.1(6) LPA 1925 · EQUITY DIFFERS
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: What are the four unities required for a joint tenancy?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
The four unities are:

1. Unity of possession — each joint tenant is entitled to possess the whole of the land
2. Unity of interest — each joint tenant holds the same interest (same extent, nature, and duration)
3. Unity of title — each joint tenant derives their title from the same act or document
4. Unity of time — each joint tenant's interest vested at the same time.

All four unities must be present for a joint tenancy. A tenancy in common requires only unity of possession — co-owners may have different shares, derive title from different documents, and vest at different times.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: FOUR UNITIES · POSSESSION · INTEREST · TITLE · TIME · JOINT TENANCY · TENANCY IN COMMON ONE UNITY ONLY
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: When will equity presume a tenancy in common rather than a joint tenancy?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
Equity presumes a tenancy in common (despite a legal joint tenancy) in three situations:

1. where co-owners contribute to the purchase price in unequal shares — equity presumes each holds in proportion to their contribution
2. where land is held for business partnership purposes — business people are unlikely to intend survivorship
3. where a mortgage is granted by two or more lenders — each lender contributes distinct money.

⚠️ Words of severance in the transfer document (e.g. 'in equal shares', 'share and share alike', 'equally') also create a tenancy in common in equity, overriding any presumption of joint tenancy.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: EQUITY PRESUMES TENANCY IN COMMON · UNEQUAL CONTRIBUTIONS · PARTNERSHIP · MORTGAGE LENDERS · WORDS OF SEVERANCE
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: How may a joint tenancy in equity be severed?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
Severance converts an equitable joint tenancy into a tenancy in common. Methods:

1. Written notice of severance served on all other joint tenants (s.196
4. LPA 1925 — written notice is sufficient; does not need to be signed: Kinch v Bullard [1998])
2. Mutual agreement — all joint tenants agree to hold as tenants in common
3. A course of dealing inconsistent with joint tenancy
4. Alienation — a joint tenant disposes of their equitable interest (e.g. by sale or mortgage) — severs their share only
5. Bankruptcy — the joint tenant's interest vests in the trustee in bankruptcy, effecting severance
6. Unlawful killing — a joint tenant cannot benefit from the right of survivorship if they unlawfully kill the other joint tenant.

⚠️ A joint tenancy at law cannot be severed — legal title remains joint.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: SEVERANCE · S.196(4) LPA 1925 · WRITTEN NOTICE · ALIENATION · MUTUAL AGREEMENT · KINCH V BULLARD · LAW NOT SEVERED
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: What is the effect of a notice of severance served on one of several joint tenants?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
A notice of severance must be served on all the other joint tenants — service on only one of two or more joint tenants is insufficient. Once served, severance is irrevocable. Under s.196(3) LPA 1925, notice is sufficiently served if left at the last known place of abode or business. In Kinch v Bullard [1998], a notice pushed through the letter box was held effective even before it was actually read — effective service occurs on delivery, not reading.

⚠️ Severance by notice affects only the share of the severing joint tenant — it does not sever the joint tenancy of the remaining joint tenants inter se.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: NOTICE OF SEVERANCE · ALL JOINT TENANTS · DELIVERY NOT READING · S.196(3) LPA · KINCH V BULLARD · IRREVOCABLE
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: What powers do trustees of land have under TOLATA 1996?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
Under TOLATA 1996, trustees of land have:

1. All powers of an absolute owner (s.6(1)) — including the power to sell, lease, mortgage, or otherwise deal with the land
2. Power to postpone sale (s.4) — there is no longer a duty to sell
3. Power to delegate functions to a beneficiary of full age in occupation (s.9)
4. Duty to consult beneficiaries of full age who are entitled in possession (s.11) and to give effect to their wishes so far as is consistent with the general interest of the trust.

⚠️ Section 11 consultation is a duty — failure to consult is a breach of trust, but does not make a sale void. Trustees may still act contrary to beneficiaries' wishes where the interests of the trust require it.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: TOLATA 1996 · ABSOLUTE OWNER POWERS · S.6 · POSTPONE SALE S.4 · CONSULT S.11 · BREACH NOT VOID
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: When may a court order a sale of co-owned land under s.14 TOLATA 1996, and what factors does it consider under s.15?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
Any trustee or beneficiary may apply to court under s.14 TOLATA 1996 for an order relating to trust land — including an order for sale. Under s.15, the court considers:

1. the intentions of the person who created the trust
2. the purposes for which the trust property is held
3. the welfare of any minor occupying or likely to occupy the land
4. the interests of any secured creditors.

⚠️ Purpose matters: if land was acquired as a family home and one party opposes sale, the court may refuse — but where the purpose has ended (e.g. the family has broken up), sale is more likely to be ordered. Creditors' interests are given significant weight once the collateral purpose has ended: Mortgage Corporation v Shaire [2001].
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: S.14 TOLATA · S.15 FACTORS · INTENTIONS · PURPOSE · MINOR WELFARE · CREDITORS · SHAIRE · FAMILY HOME
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: How does a resulting trust arise in co-ownership disputes?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
A resulting trust arises where one person contributes to the purchase price of property but legal title is taken in another's name (or jointly). The contributor acquires an equitable interest proportionate to their contribution: Dyer v Dyer [1788]. In domestic property disputes, the resulting trust has largely been supplemented by the constructive trust analysis (following Stack v Dowden [2007]), but remains relevant in commercial contexts.

⚠️ In domestic cases, the court now asks what the common intention of the parties was (which may differ from the strict arithmetic of contributions). In commercial cases, the resulting trust remains the default analysis: contribution to purchase price → proportionate beneficial interest.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: RESULTING TRUST · PURCHASE PRICE CONTRIBUTION · DYER V DYER · PROPORTIONATE SHARE · COMMERCIAL · STACK SUPPLEMENTS
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: How does the common intention constructive trust arise in domestic property disputes?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
A common intention constructive trust arises where:

1. there is a common intention (express or inferred) that the claimant should have a beneficial interest; and
2. the claimant has acted to their detriment in reliance on that common intention.

Following Stack v Dowden [2007] and Jones v Kernott [2011], in domestic cases where property is in joint names, there is a presumption of equal beneficial shares, rebuttable by evidence of a different common intention. Where property is in one name only, the starting point is sole beneficial ownership — the non-legal owner must establish a common intention and detrimental reliance. The court may impute (not merely infer) an intention where necessary: Jones v Kernott.

⚠️ Mortgage contributions, substantial indirect financial contributions, and conduct may all be relevant to the common intention.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: COMMON INTENTION CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · STACK V DOWDEN · JONES V KERNOTT · JOINT NAMES · IMPUTATION · DETRIMENTAL RELIANCE
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: What is a restriction on the register in co-ownership, and what is its purpose?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
Where land is held by co-owners on trust, a restriction is entered on the proprietorship register to prevent a sole surviving trustee from dealing with the property without a second trustee being appointed. The standard Form A restriction provides that no disposition is to be registered unless the capital money is paid to at least two trustees or a trust corporation. Purpose: to ensure overreaching can operate — a purchaser who pays two trustees overreaches the beneficial interests.

⚠️ Without a restriction, a sole surviving trustee could sell and give a receipt for capital money — but since they cannot overreach as a sole trustee, a beneficiary in actual occupation might have an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: RESTRICTION · FORM A · CO-OWNERSHIP · TWO TRUSTEES · OVERREACHING · SOLE TRUSTEE RISK · SCH 3 PARA 2
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: What is the right to occupy under s.12 TOLATA 1996, and how may it be restricted?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
Under s.12 TOLATA 1996, a beneficiary who is beneficially entitled in possession has a right to occupy trust land if:

1. the purposes of the trust include making the land available for occupation; or
2. the land is held by the trustees so as to be so available.

Trustees may restrict or exclude the right to occupy under s.13 — for example, to give one beneficiary exclusive occupation — provided they act reasonably and consider whether any other beneficiary's wishes should be taken into account. Where occupation is given to one and denied to another, the occupying beneficiary may be required to pay compensation to the excluded beneficiary.

⚠️ Trustees cannot use s.13 to exclude a beneficiary who is already in occupation without their consent or a court order.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: S.12 TOLATA · RIGHT TO OCCUPY · S.13 RESTRICT · REASONABLE · COMPENSATION EXCLUDED BENEFICIARY · CONSENT REQUIRED
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.front: How does the court approach a s.14 TOLATA application where one co-owner is bankrupt?
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.answer:
Where one co-owner is bankrupt, their trustee in bankruptcy may apply under s.14 TOLATA 1996 for an order for sale. Section 335A Insolvency Act 1986 (inserted by TOLATA 1996) governs the criteria. Within the first year of bankruptcy, the court balances the interests of the creditors against all other relevant circumstances (including the needs of a spouse or civil partner and any children). After one year, the court must order sale unless the circumstances are exceptional — the presumption strongly favours the creditors' interests.

⚠️ 'Exceptional circumstances' is a high bar — mere hardship to the family is not enough: Re Citro [1991]. Significant and unusual circumstances beyond the ordinary human misfortune of bankruptcy are required.
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.summary_line: S.335A INSOLVENCY ACT · ONE YEAR PRESUMPTION · EXCEPTIONAL CIRCUMSTANCES · RE CITRO · CREDITORS PREVAIL · TOLATA
Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOL.subtopic: Joint Tenancy, Tenancy in Common and TOLATA

Common Intention Constructive Trust.issue: Where a family home is purchased in joint names but the parties contribute unequally, what is the starting point for determining beneficial ownership, and how may it be rebutted?
Common Intention Constructive Trust.rule: Where property is conveyed into joint names, the starting point is that the beneficial interests are held equally — this presumption may be rebutted by evidence of a different common intention, inferred from the whole course of conduct.
Common Intention Constructive Trust.application: Stack and Dowden purchased a home in joint names but Ms Dowden contributed significantly more to the purchase price and kept her finances strictly separate. The House of Lords held the presumption of equal beneficial shares was rebutted on the facts — Ms Dowden held a 65% beneficial share.
Common Intention Constructive Trust.conclusion: Ms Dowden held 65%, Mr Stack 35%. Stack v Dowden is the leading authority on quantification of beneficial shares in joint names cases. It confirmed that the common intention constructive trust governs domestic property disputes, with equal shares as the starting point rebuttable by the whole course of conduct.
Common Intention Constructive Trust.summary_line: COMMON INTENTION CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · JOINT NAMES · EQUAL SHARES · REBUTTAL · WHOLE COURSE OF CONDUCT · STACK V DOWDEN

Common Intention Constructive Trust.issue: Whether the court could impute a common intention as to the division of beneficial shares where no actual common intention could be inferred from the parties' conduct.
Common Intention Constructive Trust.rule: Where a common intention constructive trust is established but the parties' actual common intention as to shares cannot be inferred, the court may impute the intention they would reasonably be taken to have had — producing a fair result in all the circumstances.
Common Intention Constructive Trust.application: Jones and Kernott purchased a home together. Kernott left and the parties' financial paths diverged significantly. The Supreme Court held that although equal shares was the starting point, the parties' common intention had changed over time — and where actual intention could not be inferred, an intention could be imputed to achieve a fair result.
Common Intention Constructive Trust.conclusion: Jones held 90%, Kernott 10%. Jones v Kernott confirmed that imputation is available at the quantification stage (not the existence stage) of a CICT. It extended Stack v Dowden by permitting the court to attribute a fair intention where no actual common intention can be found.
Common Intention Constructive Trust.summary_line: COMMON INTENTION CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · IMPUTATION · QUANTIFICATION · CHANGING INTENTION · JONES V KERNOTT

Severance.issue: Whether a notice of severance of a joint tenancy was effective where it had been delivered through the letter box but not yet read by the recipient.
Severance.rule: A notice of severance takes effect upon delivery to the recipient's address — not upon the recipient reading it. Under s.196(3) LPA 1925, a notice is sufficiently served if left at the last known place of abode or business.
Severance.application: Mrs Kinch served a notice of severance on her husband by posting it through the letter box of the matrimonial home. Before her husband read it, he suffered a heart attack and she intercepted and destroyed the notice, hoping survivorship would pass the property to her. The court held the notice was effective from the moment it was delivered.
Severance.conclusion: The joint tenancy had been severed. Kinch v Bullard confirms that service of a notice of severance is effective on delivery — the recipient's knowledge is irrelevant. Mrs Kinch could not unsever the tenancy by destroying a notice already served.
Severance.summary_line: SEVERANCE · JOINT TENANCY · NOTICE · EFFECTIVE ON DELIVERY · S.196 LPA 1925 · KINCH V BULLARD

Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.issue: Whether the court should order a sale of a jointly owned family home under the Insolvency Act where the bankrupt co-owner's spouse and children would be made homeless.
Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.rule: After one year from the vesting of a bankrupt's estate in the trustee in bankruptcy, the court will order a sale unless the circumstances are exceptional — the mere fact of hardship to a spouse and children is not exceptional and does not outweigh the interests of creditors.
Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.application: Two brothers were made bankrupt. Their wives and children occupied the family homes. The trustees in bankruptcy applied for orders for sale. The Court of Appeal held that the wives' and children's circumstances, though sympathetic, were not exceptional — they represented the ordinary human misfortune of bankruptcy.
Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.conclusion: Sale was ordered in both cases. Re Citro established that 'exceptional circumstances' under what is now s.335A Insolvency Act 1986 sets a very high bar — ordinary family hardship resulting from bankruptcy is not enough. The presumption after one year strongly favours the creditors.
Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.summary_line: BANKRUPTCY · S.335A INSOLVENCY ACT · SALE OF FAMILY HOME · EXCEPTIONAL CIRCUMSTANCES · CREDITORS PREVAIL · RE CITRO

Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.issue: How should the court balance the interests of a mortgagee creditor against those of a co-owner in occupation when considering a s.14 TOLATA 1996 application for sale.
Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.rule: Under s.15 TOLATA 1996, the court must consider the purpose of the trust, the welfare of occupying children, and the interests of secured creditors — but unlike the Insolvency Act regime, there is no presumption in favour of creditors after one year. The balancing exercise is genuinely open.
Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.application: Mrs Shaire had a beneficial interest in the family home. Her husband had forged her signature on a mortgage, so she was not personally liable. The mortgage company sought a sale. Neuberger J held that under TOLATA 1996 (unlike the Insolvency Act), the court has a genuine discretion — the creditor's interest is one factor among several, not automatically determinative.
Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.conclusion: Sale was postponed in the circumstances. Mortgage Corporation v Shaire confirmed that the TOLATA s.15 discretion is broader and more genuinely balanced than the s.335A Insolvency Act regime. Creditors' interests carry significant but not overwhelming weight.
Sale Under TOLATA and Insolvency.summary_line: TOLATA 1996 · S.14 APPLICATION · S.15 FACTORS · CREDITOR INTEREST · GENUINE DISCRETION · SHAIRE

Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.front: Alice and Bob purchase a house together. Alice contributes 70% of the purchase price and Bob contributes 30%. The transfer deed is silent as to beneficial shares. Alice and Bob later separate and dispute the beneficial ownership. Advise Alice.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.answer: The starting point depends on how the property is held. If registered in joint names: under Stack v Dowden [2007], there is a presumption of equal beneficial shares, rebuttable by evidence of a different common intention inferred from the whole course of conduct. Alice's unequal contribution is a relevant factor — but alone it may not be enough to rebut the presumption without other evidence of an intention to hold unequally. If registered in Alice's name only: Alice holds the full beneficial interest unless Bob can establish a common intention constructive trust. Bob would need to show a common intention that he should have a share and detrimental reliance. On these facts, if the property is in joint names, Alice should gather evidence of the parties' intentions — financial arrangements, discussions, how expenses were managed — to rebut the equal shares presumption and claim a 70% share.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.summary_line: COMMON INTENTION CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · STACK V DOWDEN · EQUAL SHARES PRESUMPTION · REBUTTAL · JOINT NAMES

Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.front: Carl and Diana own a house as joint tenants in law and equity. Carl sends a letter to Diana saying 'I want my share of the house — I am treating our joint ownership as ended' and posts it through Diana's letter box. Diana reads the letter but does nothing. Carl dies the next day. Carl's executor argues the joint tenancy was severed before Carl's death. Diana argues she never agreed to severance. Advise Carl's executor.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.answer: The joint tenancy was severed before Carl's death. A joint tenancy may be severed by written notice served on all other joint tenants under s.196(4) LPA 1925. Carl's letter constitutes a written notice of his intention to sever — it clearly indicates an intention to treat the joint tenancy as ended. Under Kinch v Bullard [1998], a notice of severance takes effect on delivery — not on the recipient reading it. The letter was delivered through the letter box. Severance was therefore effective from the moment of delivery — before Carl's death. Diana's agreement is not required for severance by notice. Carl's share of the beneficial interest (50%) passes under his estate, not to Diana by survivorship. Carl's executor is correct.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.summary_line: SEVERANCE · WRITTEN NOTICE · S.196(4) LPA · KINCH V BULLARD · DELIVERY NOT READING · IRREVOCABLE · ESTATE PASSES

Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.front: Ellie and Finn own a house as beneficial joint tenants. Finn is made bankrupt. His trustee in bankruptcy applies under s.14 TOLATA 1996 for an order for sale. Ellie opposes the sale, pointing out that her elderly mother lives with them and would be made homeless. The bankruptcy occurred eight months ago. Advise the trustee in bankruptcy.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.answer:
The trustee in bankruptcy's application is governed by s.335A Insolvency Act 1986, not simply s.15 TOLATA 1996. Under s.335A, where the application is by a trustee in bankruptcy: within the first year of the bankruptcy, the court balances the interests of the creditors against all relevant circumstances, including the needs of a spouse and any children. After one year, the court must order sale unless the circumstances are exceptional. Eight months have passed — the application is within the first year. The court will genuinely balance Ellie's interests and her mother's needs against the creditors' interests. However, the courts have set a high bar for 'exceptional circumstances' (Re Citro [1991]) — ordinary human hardship is not enough. The trustee should advise creditors that a sale is likely once 12 months have passed, but may face resistance within the first year.

⚠️

**Note:** Finn's bankruptcy severs the joint tenancy — Ellie and Finn now hold as tenants in common.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.summary_line: S.335A INSOLVENCY ACT · WITHIN ONE YEAR · BALANCE CREDITORS VS FAMILY · RE CITRO · BANKRUPTCY SEVERS · TOLATA

Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.front: Grace and Harry own a house as tenants in common, Grace with a 60% share and Harry with 40%. They are both trustees. Grace wants to sell the house; Harry refuses. The house was bought as a family home and Harry's two teenage children live there. Grace applies under s.14 TOLATA 1996 for an order for sale. Advise Harry.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.answer:
Under s.14 TOLATA 1996, the court may make any order it thinks fit relating to trust land. In exercising its discretion, s.15 requires the court to consider:

1. the intentions of the person who created the trust — likely to provide a family home
2. the purposes for which the trust property is held — as a family home for Harry's children
3. the welfare of any minor who occupies or might reasonably be expected to occupy the land — Harry's teenage children
4. the interests of secured creditors (not relevant here).

The purpose of the trust — providing a family home — remains active while the children are living there. The court is likely to postpone the sale until the children reach adulthood or otherwise cease to occupy, balancing Grace's interest in realising her share against the welfare of the children. Unlike the Insolvency Act regime, there is no one-year presumption — the discretion under TOLATA is genuinely open: Mortgage Corporation v Shaire [2001].
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.summary_line: S.14 TOLATA · S.15 FACTORS · FAMILY HOME PURPOSE · MINOR WELFARE · NO ONE-YEAR PRESUMPTION · SHAIRE

Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.front: Iris and Jack purchase a house in joint names. Iris contributes 80% of the purchase price and pays all mortgage instalments. Jack contributes nothing financially but manages all home maintenance and decorating. They have no express declaration of trust. They separate after 10 years. Iris claims 80% of the beneficial interest. Jack claims 50%. Advise.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.answer: The starting point under Stack v Dowden [2007] is that joint names registration gives rise to a presumption of equal beneficial shares. To rebut this, Iris must show a different common intention inferred from the whole course of conduct — not just the financial contributions. Relevant factors supporting Iris's 60% claim: her larger financial contribution to the purchase price; sole mortgage payments are significant; any evidence of separate finances. Factors supporting equal shares: Jack's non-financial contributions (maintenance, decorating); no evidence of discussions about unequal shares; the parties' overall relationship and lifestyle. The court in Stack indicated that departing from equal shares requires 'unusual' circumstances — in most domestic cases the presumption holds. Iris's 80% contribution alone is unlikely to be sufficient. The court may well award equal shares unless Iris can point to additional evidence of a different intention. Under Jones v Kernott [2011], if no actual common intention can be inferred, the court may impute a fair result — which on these facts may still be close to equal.
Beneficial Ownership and TOLATA.summary_line: STACK V DOWDEN · EQUAL SHARES PRESUMPTION · UNUSUAL CIRCUMSTANCES · JONES V KERNOTT · IMPUTATION · FINANCIAL AND NON-FINANCIAL

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: Is the adverse possession regime in the LRA 2002 compatible with Article 1 Protocol 1 ECHR, and what did JA Pye (Oxford) Ltd v UK decide?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
JA Pye (Oxford) Ltd v UK [2007] (Grand Chamber, European Court of Human Rights) is the leading authority on the compatibility of adverse possession with the right to peaceful enjoyment of possessions under Article 1 Protocol 1 ECHR (A1P1).

Background: Pye lost approximately 25 hectares of agricultural land to neighbouring farmers (the Grahams) under the Limitation Act 1980 after a relatively technical failure to renew a grazing licence. The land was worth approximately £10 million. The UK Chamber initially held the regime violated A1P1 — the automatic extinguishment of title without compensation was disproportionate. The Grand Chamber reversed this by a narrow majority (10-7), holding that the 1980 Act regime fell within the UK's margin of appreciation — it pursued a legitimate aim (certainty of title, preventing stale claims) and was not disproportionate.

The dissenting judges argued powerfully that automatic extinguishment without compensation was incompatible with A1P1 — the landowner suffered a total loss of a valuable asset through a procedural failure rather than any culpable conduct. This view has attracted academic support: Andrew Goymour ('Squatters and the European Convention' (2008)) argued the majority gave too wide a margin of appreciation.

The LRA 2002 regime may be more compatible with A1P1 than the 1980 Act: it includes a notification system (the proprietor is warned and given 65 business days to respond) before any title is lost. The Pye decision concerned the 1980 Act — whether the 2002 Act's notification system provides sufficient protection is an open question that has not been definitively tested at Strasbourg.

The unresolved tension: the law has moved toward greater protection of registered proprietors (LRA 2002) partly in response to human rights concerns, but the compatibility of the 1980 Act regime with A1P1 remains technically unresolved for unregistered land.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: ADVERSE POSSESSION · A1P1 ECHR · PYE V UK · GRAND CHAMBER · MARGIN OF APPRECIATION · GOYMOUR · LRA 2002 COMPATIBILITY

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Sophia has used a footpath across her neighbour Tom's field openly and continuously for 23 years. In year three of her use, Tom told her verbally 'that's fine, use it whenever you like.' Sophia now claims a prescriptive easement under the Prescription Act 1832, arguing 23 years of use exceeds the 20-year threshold.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: Sophia has used the path for 23 years — three years more than the 20-year Prescription Act 1832 threshold. The scenario makes the prescriptive claim feel straightforward and strong.
Trap Cards.rule: The Reality: Prescriptive acquisition requires use as of right — nec vi, nec clam, nec precario (without force, without secrecy, without permission). Tom's oral permission in year three granted Sophia a licence to use the path. Any permission, however informal, defeats the nec precario requirement — permissive use cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement. From year three onwards, Sophia's use was permissive, not as of right. The prescription period was fatally interrupted.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the 23-year duration, which comfortably exceeds the 20-year threshold. Students count the years and conclude the claim succeeds without checking the nature of the use throughout. Tom's casual verbal permission in year three is the specific fact that destroys the claim — it converts the use from as-of-right to permissive for the entire remaining period. The permission need not be formal or written to defeat prescription.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Any permission granted during the prescriptive period defeats the nec precario requirement and breaks the as-of-right use — the prescriptive period cannot be completed. Duration alone is never sufficient — the nature of the use throughout the period must be verified.
Trap Cards.summary_line: PRESCRIPTION · NEC PRECARIO · PERMISSION DEFEATS CLAIM · AS OF RIGHT · DURATION ALONE INSUFFICIENT

Registered and Unregistered Regimes.front: What is the adverse possession regime for registered land under the LRA 2002?
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.answer:
Under the LRA 2002 (applicable to registered land), an adverse possessor may apply to be registered as proprietor after 10 years of adverse possession (Sch 6, para 1). The registrar must then notify the registered proprietor, who has 65 business days to respond. If the registered proprietor (or mortgagee) objects, the application is rejected — unless one of three conditions applies (para 5):

1. it would be unconscionable to dispossess the applicant (proprietary estoppel)
2. the applicant is entitled to the land for another reason
3. the applicant is adjacent landowner who reasonably believed the land was theirs.

If the registered proprietor does not respond within 65 business days, the applicant is registered.

⚠️ After rejection, if the adverse possessor remains in possession for a further 2 years, they may re-apply and will be registered without the owner being able to object again.
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.summary_line: ADVERSE POSSESSION · SCH 6 LRA 2002 · 10 YEARS · 65 DAYS · NOTIFICATION · PARA 5 CONDITIONS · 2-YEAR RE-APPLY

Registered and Unregistered Regimes.front: What is the adverse possession regime for unregistered land under the Limitation Act 1980?
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.answer:
For unregistered land, the Limitation Act 1980 applies. Time runs against the paper owner from the moment adverse possession begins. After 12 years (s.15(1) LA 1980), the paper owner's title is automatically extinguished — the adverse possessor does not need to apply for anything; the limitation period simply bars the owner's action.

⚠️ Key distinction from registered land: in unregistered land, the owner's title is extinguished by effluxion of time alone — no notification system applies. The adverse possessor must then apply for first registration if they wish to have a registered title.
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.summary_line: LIMITATION ACT 1980 · UNREGISTERED LAND · 12 YEARS · AUTOMATIC EXTINGUISHMENT · NO NOTIFICATION · FIRST REGISTRATION

Registered and Unregistered Regimes.front: What constitutes factual possession for adverse possession purposes?
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.answer:
Factual possession requires the adverse possessor to be in single and exclusive physical control of the land, dealing with it as an occupying owner would: Powell v McFarlane [1977]. The degree of possession required depends on the nature of the land — a lower level of acts may suffice for wild or open land. Examples: fencing off land, cultivating it, building on it. Minimal acts such as occasional use or storage of items are unlikely to suffice without more.

⚠️ Factual possession alone is insufficient — the possessor must also have the requisite intention to possess (animus possidendi): Buckinghamshire CC v Moran [1990].
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.summary_line: FACTUAL POSSESSION · SINGLE EXCLUSIVE CONTROL · POWELL V MCFARLANE · ANIMUS POSSIDENDI · FENCING CULTIVATING

Registered and Unregistered Regimes.front: What is the animus possidendi requirement in adverse possession?
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.answer:
Animus possidendi is the intention to possess the land — the adverse possessor must intend to possess for themselves, to the exclusion of all others including the paper owner. The test is objective — what would a reasonable observer conclude from the adverse possessor's conduct?

⚠️ Crucially, the adverse possessor does not need to intend to own the land or to claim it as theirs permanently — they simply need to intend to possess it as owner for the time being: Buckinghamshire CC v Moran [1990]. A person who occupies land knowing they may have to give it up if required is still capable of adverse possession if they intend to hold it until that time comes.
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.summary_line: ANIMUS POSSIDENDI · INTENTION TO POSSESS · MORAN · OBJECTIVE TEST · NOT PERMANENT OWNERSHIP · FOR THE TIME BEING

Registered and Unregistered Regimes.front: What acts by the true owner stop time running for adverse possession purposes?
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.answer:
Time stops running against the true owner if:

1. the owner re-enters the land and re-asserts possession — actual re-entry interrupts the period
2. the owner brings an action to recover the land — issuing proceedings stops time running (Limitation Act 1980, s.29(2))
3. the adverse possessor acknowledges the owner's title in writing (s.29 LA 1980 — an acknowledgement restarts the period).

⚠️ Mere assertion of rights (e.g. a letter asserting ownership without re-entry) does not interrupt time — only an actual re-entry or court proceedings will do so. In registered land, the notification system under Sch 6 LRA 2002 gives the owner an opportunity to assert their title without needing to physically re-enter.
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.summary_line: INTERRUPTION · RE-ENTRY · PROCEEDINGS · ACKNOWLEDGEMENT S.29 · MERE LETTER INSUFFICIENT · SCH 6 NOTIFICATION

Registered and Unregistered Regimes.front: Can time run for adverse possession against a landlord during a lease?
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.answer:
Time can run against a tenant in possession by an adverse possessor — the adverse possessor will acquire the tenant's leasehold interest. However, time does not run against the landlord during the lease — the landlord's right of action only arises when the lease ends and they seek to recover possession. At that point, time runs against the landlord from the end of the lease (or from when the tenant's possession became adverse if the tenant voluntarily abandoned).

⚠️ This means an adverse possessor who displaces a tenant acquires only a leasehold interest (or better, whatever the tenant had) — not the freehold — until the lease ends and time separately runs against the freeholder.
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.summary_line: ADVERSE POSSESSION · LANDLORD · LEASE · TIME RUNS AGAINST TENANT · NOT LANDLORD DURING LEASE · LEASEHOLD ACQUIRED

Registered and Unregistered Regimes.front: What is the 'adjacent landowner' exception to adverse possession in registered land under Sch 6, para 5(c) LRA 2002?
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.answer:
Under Sch 6, para 5(c) LRA 2002, an adverse possessor may be registered despite the registered proprietor's objection if:

1. for at least 10 years immediately preceding the application, the applicant reasonably believed that the land they were possessing was theirs
2. the land is adjacent to the applicant's own land; and
3. the exact boundary has not been determined under the register.

This exception is designed for 'boundary disputes' — a neighbour who has cultivated a strip of adjacent land believing it to be theirs.

⚠️ The 'reasonable belief' requirement is objective and must subsist for the full 10-year period. If the applicant became aware at any point that the land was not theirs, the exception may fail.
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.summary_line: SCH 6 PARA 5(C) · ADJACENT LANDOWNER · REASONABLE BELIEF · 10 YEARS · BOUNDARY DISPUTE · REGISTERED LAND

Registered and Unregistered Regimes.front: How does adverse possession interact with registered land's guarantee of title?
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.answer:
The LRA 2002 significantly curtailed adverse possession in registered land precisely to protect the guarantee of title — the state guarantees the register's accuracy, and automatic extinguishment after 12 years (as under the LA 1980 for unregistered land) was incompatible with this guarantee. The 2002 Act replaced automatic extinguishment with the notification system (Sch 6) — the owner gets notice and an opportunity to reclaim. If a squatter is wrongly registered through a mistake in the notification process, the registered proprietor may apply to alter the register and claim indemnity under Sch 8.

⚠️ This reflects the deliberate policy choice to prioritise the registered proprietor's position over adverse possession claims in registered land.
Registered and Unregistered Regimes.summary_line: ADVERSE POSSESSION · GUARANTEE OF TITLE · LRA 2002 · NOTIFICATION SYSTEM · INDEMNITY SCH 8 · POLICY CHOICE

Animus Possidendi.issue: Whether a person who enclosed and used land, knowing they might have to give it up if required, had the necessary intention to possess (animus possidendi) for adverse possession.
Animus Possidendi.rule: Animus possidendi requires an intention to possess the land as owner for the time being — not an intention to own it permanently or to claim it as one's own. A person can have the necessary intention even if they know their possession may eventually be terminated.
Animus Possidendi.application: Moran enclosed a plot of land owned by Buckinghamshire CC, intending to use it until the council required it for a bypass. He knew the land might be needed and did not claim permanent ownership. The Court of Appeal held he had the necessary animus possidendi — his intention to possess for the time being sufficed.
Animus Possidendi.conclusion: Adverse possession was established. Buckinghamshire v Moran confirms that the intention to possess does not require a belief that the land belongs to the possessor or an intention to hold it permanently. This sets a relatively accessible standard for the mental element of adverse possession.
Animus Possidendi.summary_line: ADVERSE POSSESSION · ANIMUS POSSIDENDI · INTENTION TO POSSESS · NOT PERMANENT OWNERSHIP · MORAN

Factual Possession.issue: What acts are sufficient to constitute factual possession for the purposes of adverse possession, and what standard of proof applies?
Factual Possession.rule: Factual possession requires single and exclusive physical control of the land, dealing with it in the way that an occupying owner would — the degree of control required depends on the nature and situation of the land. The standard of proof is high and the acts relied upon must be unequivocal.
Factual Possession.application: McFarlane, then a teenager, had grazed a cow on Powell's land and done various acts of maintenance. Slade J held that while some acts of possession had been performed, the evidence of animus possidendi was insufficient — many of the acts were equivocal and consistent with use as a trespasser rather than as an owner.
Factual Possession.conclusion: Adverse possession was not established on the facts. Powell v McFarlane is the leading statement of the factual possession requirement and the high evidential bar for establishing adverse possession — unequivocal acts of possession and clear animus possidendi are required.
Factual Possession.summary_line: ADVERSE POSSESSION · FACTUAL POSSESSION · SINGLE AND EXCLUSIVE CONTROL · UNEQUIVOCAL ACTS · HIGH STANDARD · POWELL V MCFARLANE

Registration and Procedure.front: Kevin has been occupying an unregistered plot of land belonging to Lucy since 2008. He has fenced the land, cultivated a vegetable garden, and built a small shed. Lucy has taken no steps to recover the land. It is now 2026. Advise Kevin on his position.
Registration and Procedure.answer: Kevin's position is governed by the Limitation Act 1980 (unregistered land). Time starts running against the paper owner from the moment adverse possession begins — 2008. By 2026, Kevin has been in adverse possession for 18 years. The limitation period for unregistered land is 12 years (s.15(1) LA 1980). After 12 years, Lucy's title is automatically extinguished — no application or court order is needed. Lucy's title was extinguished in 2020. Kevin has been in adverse possession throughout — fencing, cultivation, and building a shed are unequivocal acts of factual possession: Powell v McFarlane [1977]. Kevin should apply for first registration at HMLR, providing evidence of his adverse possession. He will be registered with possessory title initially, which may be upgraded in due course.
Registration and Procedure.summary_line: LIMITATION ACT 1980 · UNREGISTERED LAND · 12 YEARS · AUTOMATIC EXTINGUISHMENT · POWELL V MCFARLANE · FIRST REGISTRATION

Registration and Procedure.front: Mark has been in adverse possession of registered land belonging to Nancy for 11 years. He applies to be registered as proprietor. Nancy receives the registrar's notification and objects within the 65-day period, without establishing any of the three conditions in Schedule 6, para 5 LRA 2002. The registrar rejects Mark's application. Mark continues in possession. Advise Mark on his next steps.
Registration and Procedure.answer:
Mark's application has been correctly rejected — Nancy objected within the 65-day period and none of the para 5 conditions apply. However, Mark is not without recourse. Under Sch 6, para 6 LRA 2002, if Mark remains in adverse possession for a further two years after the rejection of his application, he may make a second application to be registered. On the second application, Nancy cannot object on the same grounds — Mark will be registered as proprietor. Mark must therefore remain in adverse possession of the land for two more years (until approximately 2028 on these facts) and then re-apply.

⚠️ Nancy should use this two-year window to recover possession through court proceedings — if she successfully evicts Mark, his adverse possession period is broken and he cannot re-apply.
Registration and Procedure.summary_line: SCH 6 LRA 2002 · REJECTION · 2 YEARS FURTHER POSSESSION · RE-APPLY · PROPRIETOR CANNOT OBJECT · RECOVER OR LOSE

Registration and Procedure.front: Owen has maintained and used a strip of registered land between his garden and his neighbour's garden for 12 years, believing it to be part of his own property. The strip is shown on the register as belonging to his neighbour Paula. Owen has cultivated it, installed a fence, and paid for its upkeep. Paula has recently discovered the position and disputes Owen's claim. Advise Owen.
Registration and Procedure.answer:
Owen may qualify under the adjacent landowner exception in Sch 6, para 5(c) LRA 2002. This allows registration despite the proprietor's objection where:

1. for at least 10 years immediately before the application, Owen reasonably believed the land was his
2. the land is adjacent to Owen's own land; and
3. the exact boundary has not been determined under the register.

Owen has possessed the strip for 12 years, exceeding the 10-year threshold. The strip is adjacent to his property. The key question is whether Owen's belief that the strip was his was reasonable for the full 10-year period. Cultivating it, fencing it, and paying for its upkeep over 12 years strongly supports a reasonable belief. Owen should apply to be registered under Sch 6, para 1 and rely on para 5(c) if Paula objects.

⚠️ If at any point Owen knew or should have known the strip was not his, the reasonable belief requirement may fail.
Registration and Procedure.summary_line: SCH 6 PARA 5(C) · ADJACENT LANDOWNER · REASONABLE BELIEF · 10 YEARS · FULL PERIOD · BOUNDARY DISPUTE

Registration and Procedure.front: Quentin has been in adverse possession of registered land for 10 years. He applies to be registered. The registered proprietor, Ruth, objects but cannot establish any of the para 5 conditions. Quentin's application is nonetheless rejected because he cannot show factual possession for the full 10-year period — he was absent for eight months in year three. Ruth argues the absence broke the continuity of possession. Advise Quentin.
Registration and Procedure.answer: The question is whether Quentin's eight-month absence breaks the continuity of adverse possession. Adverse possession requires continuous factual possession — but continuity does not mean constant physical presence. What matters is whether Quentin retained exclusive control of the land during the absence and whether any acts are consistent with ongoing possession. If Quentin left the land fenced, maintained, and under his exclusive control during the eight-month absence, continuity is likely preserved — a landlocked or secured piece of land can remain in adverse possession even without the possessor's physical presence. If, however, the land was open and accessible, and Quentin took no steps to maintain possession during the absence, continuity may be broken. The 10-year period would then restart from when Quentin resumed factual possession. Quentin should gather evidence of acts of control during the absence — fencing, preventing access, maintaining the land. If continuity is established, the application should succeed on the second attempt after a further two years if Ruth does not take possession proceedings.
Registration and Procedure.summary_line: CONTINUITY OF POSSESSION · 8-MONTH ABSENCE · FENCED LAND · EXCLUSIVE CONTROL MAINTAINED · 10-YEAR PERIOD · RE-APPLY

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: Is the clogs on the equity of redemption doctrine coherent, and has academic criticism of it been persuasive?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
The doctrine of clogs on the equity of redemption holds that a mortgagor must be able to redeem the mortgage and recover the property free of all mortgage obligations — any provision that prevents, restricts, or postpones redemption unconscionably is void. Classic applications include: the prohibition on irredeemable mortgages; the rule against collateral advantages continuing after redemption (Noakes v Rice [1902]); and limits on postponement of the redemption date.

David Hayton ('Mortgages' in Megarry & Wade) and others have argued the doctrine is incoherent and commercially obstructive. The principal criticisms are:

1. the rules are inconsistently applied — Knightsbridge Estates v Byrne [1939] upheld a 40-year postponement as a genuine commercial bargain, while other cases strike down similar provisions
2. the doctrine treats all mortgages alike when the policy rationale (protecting vulnerable borrowers) applies most strongly to residential mortgages and hardly at all to sophisticated commercial transactions
3. the 'once a mortgage always a mortgage' maxim produces results that sophisticated parties could easily avoid through more creative structuring.

The Law Commission considered the clogs doctrine in its consultations but has not proposed wholesale reform. The courts have shown increasing willingness to uphold commercial mortgage terms as genuine bargains (Bradley v Carritt [1903] distinguished; Multiservice Bookbinding v Marden [1979] on inflation-linked repayment terms).

Comparative note: civil law systems do not have an equivalent doctrine — commercial mortgage flexibility is achieved through contract law rather than through a specialised equity jurisdiction.

The unresolved tension: the clogs doctrine persists as an equitable anomaly — largely benign in commercial practice but occasionally disruptive where courts cannot agree on what is 'unconscionable'.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: CLOGS ON EQUITY OF REDEMPTION · HAYTON · NOAKES V RICE · KNIGHTSBRIDGE · INCOHERENT · COMMERCIAL MORTGAGES · REFORM

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Ursula borrows £300,000 from Valley Bank, secured by a charge over her registered home. The charge is created by a properly executed deed signed by Ursula and a Valley Bank representative, and witnessed. Valley Bank does not register the charge at HMLR. Valley Bank argues it holds a legal mortgage over Ursula's property because the charge was created by deed.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: The charge was created by a properly executed deed — all the formality requirements are satisfied. Students conclude Valley Bank holds a legal mortgage.
Trap Cards.rule: The Reality: For registered land, a charge by way of legal mortgage does not take effect as a legal mortgage until it is registered at HMLR as a substantive disposition under s.27(2)(f) LRA 2002. Until registration, the charge operates as an equitable charge only (s.27(1) LRA 2002). Execution by deed is necessary but not sufficient — registration is the additional step that confers legal status in registered land.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the properly executed deed. For unregistered land, a deed is sufficient to create a legal mortgage. Students apply the unregistered land rule to registered land without checking the additional registration requirement. In registered land, the deed creates an equitable charge — the legal mortgage only comes into existence on registration. Valley Bank holds only an equitable charge, which should be protected by notice on the register. Its statutory remedies under s.101 LPA 1925 (which apply to legal mortgages) may not be automatically available.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: In registered land, a legal mortgage requires both a deed AND registration at HMLR (s.27 LRA 2002). A deed alone creates only an equitable charge. Registration is essential — not optional — for the charge to take effect as a legal mortgage.
Trap Cards.summary_line: LEGAL MORTGAGE · REGISTERED LAND · DEED INSUFFICIENT ALONE · REGISTRATION REQUIRED · S.27 LRA 2002 · EQUITABLE CHARGE

Licences and Estoppel.front: What are the main types of licence in land law?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
The main types of licence are:

1. Bare licence — a simple permission with no consideration, revocable at will on reasonable notice
2. Contractual licence — a permission supported by consideration, subject to ordinary contract principles
3. Licence coupled with a grant — a licence linked to a proprietary right (e.g. a right to enter land to take fish) — this is irrevocable so long as the proprietary right subsists
4. Licence by estoppel — where a party is estopped from revoking the licence by their own conduct creating a reasonable expectation.

⚠️ A licence (of any type) does not create a proprietary interest in land — it is personal and generally does not bind third parties, unlike an easement or a lease.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: LICENCE TYPES · BARE · CONTRACTUAL · COUPLED WITH GRANT · ESTOPPEL · PERSONAL NOT PROPRIETARY

Licences and Estoppel.front: What are the three elements required to establish a proprietary estoppel?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
To establish proprietary estoppel, the claimant must prove:

1. Assurance — a representation or assurance by the landowner that the claimant has or will have an interest in the land (Thorner v Major [2009] — the assurance need not be express but must be clear enough to be relied upon)
2. Reliance — the claimant must have acted in reliance on the assurance
3. Detriment — the claimant must have suffered detriment as a result of their reliance, such that it would be unconscionable for the landowner to resile from the assurance.

All three must be established — the court then considers what remedy satisfies the equity.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: PROPRIETARY ESTOPPEL · ASSURANCE · RELIANCE · DETRIMENT · THORNER V MAJOR · UNCONSCIONABLE · EQUITY

Licences and Estoppel.front: What remedies are available once a proprietary estoppel is established, and how does the court determine the appropriate remedy?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
Once estoppel is established, the court has a broad discretion to grant the minimum remedy necessary to satisfy the equity — it need not give the claimant everything they expected. Possible remedies include:

1. Transfer of the freehold
2. Grant of a long lease
3. Grant of a right of occupation for life
4. A monetary payment.

The proportionality principle from Jennings v Rice [2002] requires the court to balance: (a) the expectation created by the assurance; (b) the detriment suffered. Where the expectation and detriment are disproportionate, the remedy may be limited to the detriment.

⚠️ The remedy is discretionary — the court considers all the circumstances, including the conduct of both parties.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: ESTOPPEL REMEDY · MINIMUM TO SATISFY EQUITY · JENNINGS V RICE · PROPORTIONALITY · EXPECTATION VS DETRIMENT · DISCRETION

Licences and Estoppel.front: Can a proprietary estoppel equity bind a third party who purchases the land?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
An equity arising from proprietary estoppel is a proprietary right capable of binding third parties. In registered land: the equity should be protected by entry of a notice on the register. If the claimant is in actual occupation, the equity may override as an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002 (an interest of a person in actual occupation): Lloyd v Dugdale [2001]. In unregistered land: the equity binds all except a bona fide purchaser for value without notice.

⚠️ The equity is inchoate until the court grants a remedy — it may be registered as a notice even before the remedy is determined: a notice protects priority.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: ESTOPPEL EQUITY · PROPRIETARY RIGHT · NOTICE ON REGISTER · ACTUAL OCCUPATION SCH 3 · BONA FIDE PURCHASER

Licences and Estoppel.front: What is the difference between a contractual licence and a lease, and why does the distinction matter?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
A contractual licence is a personal right — it does not create a proprietary interest and generally does not bind third parties. A lease is a proprietary interest — it binds successors in title. The distinction matters because:

1. a purchaser of land takes free of a contractual licence (Ashburn Anstalt v Arnold [1989])
2. a tenant has statutory protection (e.g. security of tenure)
3. a licensee's rights cease if the licensor sells.

The determinative question is whether the agreement grants exclusive possession (Street v Mountford [1985]) — if yes, it is a lease regardless of the label used.

⚠️ A contractual licence may bind a successor in exceptional circumstances if equity imposes a constructive trust — but Ashburn Anstalt confirmed this requires something more than mere notice.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: CONTRACTUAL LICENCE · LEASE · PERSONAL VS PROPRIETARY · ASHBURN ANSTALT · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION · CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST

Licences and Estoppel.front: What is 'detriment' for the purposes of proprietary estoppel, and how broadly is it construed?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
Detriment in proprietary estoppel is broadly construed — it need not be financial loss. It includes any action or inaction that leaves the claimant in a worse position than if they had not relied on the assurance, assessed at the time the assurance is sought to be revoked. Examples: spending money on improvements to land (Inwards v Baker [1965]), giving up a job or other opportunity, providing personal care over many years.

⚠️ The detriment is assessed objectively and at the time the landowner attempts to resile from their assurance — not at the time the act was done. Acts that seemed reasonable at the time may become detrimental only when the assurance is withdrawn.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: DETRIMENT · BROADLY CONSTRUED · INWARDS V BAKER · FINANCIAL AND NON-FINANCIAL · ASSESSED ON REVOCATION

Licences and Estoppel.front: What is the relationship between proprietary estoppel and the common intention constructive trust in domestic property disputes?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
Both doctrines may arise on similar facts — a cohabitee who contributes to a property in reliance on a belief that they have (or will have) a share. The doctrines overlap but differ:

1. Common intention constructive trust (Stack v Dowden [2007]) — focuses on the parties' shared intention as to beneficial ownership, inferred or imputed from conduct
2. Proprietary estoppel — focuses on assurance, reliance, and detriment, with flexible remedial discretion.

The Supreme Court in Thorner v Major [2009] confirmed that estoppel requires an assurance clear enough to be acted upon — a vaguer understanding may ground a constructive trust instead.

⚠️ In practice, both claims are often pleaded in the alternative — the doctrines can overlap, but the remedy in estoppel is proportionate to the equity, whereas a constructive trust gives a fixed beneficial share.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: ESTOPPEL VS CICT · ASSURANCE REQUIRED · THORNER V MAJOR · STACK V DOWDEN · PROPORTIONATE REMEDY · PLEADED IN ALTERNATIVE

Licences and Estoppel.front: FRAMEWORK: What is the step-by-step procedure for an adverse possession application in registered land under Schedule 6 LRA 2002?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
**Step 1:** Adverse possessor has been in adverse possession for 10 years → may apply to be registered (Sch 6, para 1). Step 2: Registrar notifies the registered proprietor (and any mortgagee or other interested party) — they have 65 business days to respond. Step 3: If no response within 65 business days → applicant is registered as proprietor automatically. Step 4: If the proprietor (or mortgagee) objects → application is rejected UNLESS one of three conditions in para 5 applies: (a) unconscionable to dispossess (estoppel); (b) applicant otherwise entitled to the land; (c) adjacent landowner with reasonable belief the land was theirs for 10 years. Step 5: If application rejected and none of the para 5 conditions apply → adverse possessor must vacate or be evicted. Step 6: If the adverse possessor remains in possession for a further 2 years after rejection → they may re-apply and will be registered without the proprietor being able to object.

⚠️ The notification system is the key difference from unregistered land — the owner gets a chance to act.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: ADVERSE POSSESSION FRAMEWORK · SCH 6 · 10 YEARS · 65 DAYS · PARA 5 CONDITIONS · REJECT THEN 2 YEARS · RE-APPLY

Licences and Estoppel.front: FRAMEWORK: What is the decision sequence for a mortgagee considering which remedy to use on the mortgagor's default?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
**Step 1:** Has the power of sale arisen? — mortgage by deed + legal redemption date passed (s.101 LPA 1925). Step 2: Is the power of sale exercisable? — one of the s.103 conditions met (3 months' notice + default; 2 months' interest arrear; breach of other covenant). Step 3: Consider the appropriate remedy: (a) Possession — immediate right, but s.36 AJA 1970 gives court discretion to adjourn for residential property; use as precursor to sale or to manage the property. (b) Sale — most common; duty to obtain true market value (Cuckmere). Proceeds applied in s.105 order. (c) Receiver — useful for income-producing commercial property; receiver is agent of mortgagor. (d) Foreclosure — rare; requires court order; court usually orders sale instead. Step 4: Check for prior mortgages — selling mortgagee must discharge prior mortgages from proceeds first.

⚠️ A mortgagee who exercises the power of sale before it is exercisable is liable in damages — but the purchaser still gets good title (s.104 LPA 1925).
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: MORTGAGEE REMEDIES FRAMEWORK · S.101 S.103 · POSSESSION · SALE · RECEIVER · FORECLOSURE · S.104 PURCHASER

Licences and Estoppel.front: Overreaching gap-fill: A property is held by two trustees on trust for three adult beneficiaries. The trustees sell the property and pay the proceeds to only one of them. Which condition for overreaching has failed, and what is the consequence?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
The condition that has failed is the requirement to pay capital money to at least two trustees or a trust corporation (s.27 LPA 1925). Overreaching requires payment to a minimum of two trustees — payment to a sole trustee is insufficient. Consequence: overreaching has not operated. The beneficial interests remain attached to the land rather than shifting to the proceeds. A beneficiary who was in actual occupation at the time of the disposition may have an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002, binding the purchaser.

⚠️ The purchaser is not necessarily protected by registration alone — a beneficiary in actual occupation who is not overreached can still enforce their interest against the purchaser.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: OVERREACHING FAILS · SOLE TRUSTEE PAYMENT · S.27 LPA 1925 · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · SCH 3 PARA 2 · INTERESTS REMAIN

Licences and Estoppel.front: Severance gap-fill: Which of the following methods of severance operates at law as well as in equity, and which operates in equity only? (a) Written notice under s.196 LPA 1925; (b) Alienation of a joint tenant's share; (c) Mutual agreement; (d) Course of dealing.
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
None of these methods sever the legal joint tenancy — the legal estate cannot be severed. All four methods operate in equity only, converting the equitable joint tenancy into a tenancy in common. The legal title remains held by the joint tenants (up to a maximum of four: s.34 LPA 1925) as trustees for the equitable tenants in common.

⚠️ This is a fundamental principle: s.1(6) LPA 1925 requires the legal estate in co-owned land to be held as a joint tenancy. Severance affects only the equitable interest. A surviving legal joint tenant holds the legal title on trust for the remaining equitable owners — they cannot take the legal estate beneficially by survivorship once the equitable joint tenancy has been severed.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: SEVERANCE EQUITY ONLY · LEGAL ESTATE CANNOT BE SEVERED · S.1(6) LPA 1925 · S.34 MAX FOUR · TRUSTEE FOR EQUITABLE OWNERS

Licences and Estoppel.front: Four unities gap-fill: A and B acquire a property on different dates, under different conveyances, but in equal shares with the right to possess the whole. Which unity is missing, and what is the consequence?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
Two unities are missing: unity of title (they derive title from different conveyances) and unity of time (their interests vested at different times). Without all four unities (possession, interest, title, time), there cannot be a joint tenancy — the co-owners hold as tenants in common in equity. They each have an undivided equal share with no right of survivorship.

⚠️

**Note:** unity of possession is present (both entitled to possess the whole) — but this alone only supports a tenancy in common, not a joint tenancy. At law, co-owners always hold as joint tenants (s.1(6) LPA 1925) — so the legal title will be held jointly as trustees, but the equitable interest is a tenancy in common.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: FOUR UNITIES · TITLE MISSING · TIME MISSING · TENANCY IN COMMON · NO SURVIVORSHIP · S.1(6) LEGAL JOINT TENANCY

Licences and Estoppel.front: Contrast card: A sells part of their land to B. Before the sale, A used a path across the retained land (now B's land) to access the rear garden of the part sold. Apply both Wheeldon v Burrows and s.62 LPA 1925 to determine whether B acquires an easement over A's retained land.
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
Wheeldon v Burrows:

1. Was there a quasi-easement? Yes — A used the path over the retained land before the grant.
2. Was it continuous and apparent? Likely yes if there is a visible path.
3. Is it necessary for reasonable enjoyment? Access to the rear garden is likely to satisfy this.

Result: an easement is implied in B's favour under Wheeldon v Burrows. Section 62 LPA 1925: s.62 requires a prior diversity of occupation — it upgrades permissions or advantages enjoyed at the time of the conveyance. If A and B are now in a conveyance situation and there was no prior diversity of occupation (A owned both plots), s.62 may not apply to imply the easement — it is more useful where a tenant's permission is upgraded on purchase.

⚠️

**Key distinction:** Wheeldon v Burrows is the primary route for implied easements on sale of part where grantor previously used a quasi-easement. Section 62 is more useful for upgrading existing permissions enjoyed by a prior occupier.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: WHEELDON V BURROWS · S.62 CONTRAST · QUASI-EASEMENT · DIVERSITY OF OCCUPATION · GRANT OF PART · IMPLIED EASEMENT

Licences and Estoppel.front: Forfeiture contrast: A tenant breaches a covenant not to assign without consent (once-and-for-all breach) and separately breaches a covenant to keep the property in repair (continuing breach). The landlord accepts rent after becoming aware of both breaches. What is the effect of waiver on each?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
Once-and-for-all breach (unauthorised assignment): accepting rent after knowledge of this breach permanently waives the right to forfeit for that specific breach. The landlord cannot subsequently forfeit on the basis of the unauthorised assignment — the right is extinguished. Continuing breach (disrepair): accepting rent waives the right to forfeit for the breach as it existed up to the date of acceptance — but does not waive future breaches. A fresh right to forfeit arises each day the breach continues. The landlord may serve a fresh s.146 notice and forfeit for the ongoing failure to repair.

⚠️ The distinction between once-and-for-all and continuing breaches is the key to waiver analysis — always identify which category the breach falls into before advising on the effect of any act of waiver.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: WAIVER CONTRAST · ONCE-AND-FOR-ALL PERMANENT · CONTINUING BREACH FRESH RIGHT · DISREPAIR · UNAUTHORISED ASSIGNMENT

Licences and Estoppel.front: What is the effect of failing to register a legal charge over registered land, and what interest does the chargee hold in the interim?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
A charge by way of legal mortgage over registered land must be completed by registration as a substantive disposition under s.27(2)(f) LRA 2002. Until registration, the charge does not take effect as a legal mortgage — it operates as an equitable charge only (s.27

1. LRA 2002).

**Consequences:**
1. The chargee has an equitable interest, not a legal one — they should protect it by entering a notice on the register
2. A subsequent registered legal mortgagee without notice of the equitable charge may take priority under s.29 LRA 2002
3. The mortgagee's statutory remedies (power of sale, appointment of receiver under s.101 LPA 1925) apply to legal mortgages — an equitable chargee may need to go to court to enforce.

⚠️ Registration is essential for the charge to operate as intended — delay creates a priority risk.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: UNREGISTERED CHARGE · EQUITABLE ONLY · S.27 LRA 2002 · PRIORITY RISK · NOTICE ON REGISTER · STATUTORY REMEDIES LOST

Licences and Estoppel.front: Once a common intention constructive trust is established, how does the court quantify each party's beneficial share under Stack v Dowden and Jones v Kernott?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
Once a CICT is established, quantification follows a two-stage process:

1. Infer the parties' actual common intention as to shares from the whole course of conduct — financial contributions (mortgage payments, deposit, improvements), how outgoings were managed, any discussions about ownership.
2. If no actual common intention can be inferred, the court may impute an intention — attribute to the parties the intention they would reasonably be taken to have had, producing a fair result in all the circumstances: Jones v Kernott [2011].

Starting points: joint names → equal shares (Stack v Dowden); sole name → no beneficial share for the non-owner (unless CICT established).

⚠️ Imputation is available only at the quantification stage — the existence of the trust must be established by inference from actual common intention, not imputation. The distinction between inference and imputation is subtle but was confirmed as significant in Jones v Kernott.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: CICT QUANTIFICATION · INFER THEN IMPUTE · JONES V KERNOTT · STACK V DOWDEN · WHOLE COURSE OF CONDUCT · INFERENCE VS IMPUTATION

Licences and Estoppel.front: Contrast card: What is the difference between an easement implied by necessity and one implied by common intention, and when does each apply?
Licences and Estoppel.answer:
Easement of necessity: implied where the land granted would be completely inaccessible without the easement — strictly construed. Any alternative access, however inconvenient, defeats the claim. The easement is limited to what is strictly necessary. Common intention easement: implied where both parties intended at the time of the grant that the land would be used in a particular way, and that use requires the easement — the easement is implied to give effect to the parties' known common purpose: Pwllbach Colliery Co v Woodman [1915]. The common intention easement is broader — it does not require strict necessity, only that the easement was necessary to give effect to the intended use.

⚠️

**Key distinction:** necessity asks 'is the land completely inaccessible without it?'; common intention asks 'did both parties intend a use that requires this right?' A right may fail the necessity test but succeed on common intention grounds.
Licences and Estoppel.summary_line: NECESSITY VS COMMON INTENTION · STRICT NECESSITY · PWLLBACH · INTENDED USE · ABSOLUTE VS PURPOSIVE · IMPLIED EASEMENT

Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.issue: Whether an assurance sufficient to found a proprietary estoppel could be made by indirect, oblique conduct rather than by express words.
Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.rule: An assurance for proprietary estoppel need not be express — it may be implied from conduct, provided it is clear enough in its context to be reasonably understood as an assurance that the claimant would acquire an interest in the land.
Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.application: David Thorner worked on his cousin Peter's farm for nearly 30 years without pay, in the expectation (encouraged by oblique comments and conduct) that he would inherit it. Peter died intestate, leaving the farm to others. The House of Lords held the assurance was sufficiently clear in the context of their relationship — both men were taciturn farmers.
Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.conclusion: The estoppel was established. Thorner v Major confirmed that the assurance requirement is assessed in context — it need not be explicit, provided it is clear enough to be reasonably relied upon. It is a significant authority for recognising estoppels arising from conduct in long-term relationships.
Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.summary_line: PROPRIETARY ESTOPPEL · ASSURANCE · OBLIQUE CONDUCT · CLEAR ENOUGH · CONTEXT · THORNER V MAJOR

Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.issue: Whether a son who built a bungalow on his father's land in reliance on an assurance that he could live there for life had a licence enforceable against the father's estate.
Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.rule: Where a person expends money on another's land in reliance on an assurance that they may remain, a court of equity will protect that interest — the licensor (and their estate) cannot revoke the licence and evict the licensee.
Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.application: Baker's father encouraged him to build a bungalow on the father's land, suggesting Baker could live there. Baker built the bungalow at his own expense. After the father's death, his estate sought possession. The Court of Appeal held Baker's equity arising from detrimental reliance gave him a right to remain for life.
Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.conclusion: Baker was entitled to remain. Inwards v Baker is an early and important authority on proprietary estoppel — it established that detrimental expenditure on another's land in reliance on an assurance creates an equity enforceable in specie (a right to occupy), not merely a monetary claim.
Proprietary Estoppel — Elements.summary_line: PROPRIETARY ESTOPPEL · DETRIMENTAL RELIANCE · LICENCE · EXPENDITURE ON LAND · RIGHT TO REMAIN · INWARDS V BAKER

Proprietary Estoppel — Remedy.issue: How should a court determine the appropriate remedy once a proprietary estoppel has been established, where the expectation created and the detriment suffered are disproportionate.
Proprietary Estoppel — Remedy.rule: The remedy in proprietary estoppel is the minimum necessary to satisfy the equity — where the expectation and the detriment are disproportionate, the court should not automatically award the full expectation but should fashion a proportionate remedy reflecting the detriment and the unconscionability of the conduct.
Proprietary Estoppel — Remedy.application: Jennings had worked for Mrs Royle for many years without full pay, in expectation of inheriting her house and furniture (worth around £435,000).
