The Court of Appeal held the full expectation was disproportionate to the detriment — a monetary award of £200,000 was more appropriate.
Proprietary Estoppel — Remedy.conclusion: Jennings received £200,000, not the full estate. Jennings v Rice is the foundational authority on proportionality in proprietary estoppel remedies — the remedy need not match the expectation. Courts balance the expectation, the detriment, and all the circumstances to achieve a result that satisfies the equity without overcompensating.
Proprietary Estoppel — Remedy.summary_line: PROPRIETARY ESTOPPEL · REMEDY · PROPORTIONALITY · EXPECTATION VS DETRIMENT · MINIMUM TO SATISFY EQUITY · JENNINGS V RICE

Licences.issue: Whether a contractual licence coupled with an equity could bind a successor in title to the licensor.
Licences.rule: A contractual licence may, in exceptional circumstances, bind a successor in title where equity imposes a constructive trust or the licence is coupled with a proprietary interest — but the general rule is that contractual licences do not bind third parties.
Licences.application: A father bought a house and allowed his son and daughter-in-law to live there, promising to convey it to them if they paid the mortgage. After the father's death, the widow (who had inherited) sought possession. The Court of Appeal held the contractual licence coupled with an equity bound the widow.
Licences.conclusion: The widow took subject to the licence. Errington v Errington was influential but later qualified — Ashburn Anstalt v Arnold [1989] confirmed that contractual licences do not generally bind successors, and that a constructive trust requires something more than mere notice. Errington is now treated as an exceptional case on its facts.
Licences.summary_line: CONTRACTUAL LICENCE · THIRD PARTIES · CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · SUCCESSOR · EXCEPTIONAL CIRCUMSTANCES · ERRINGTON

Proprietary Estoppel.front: Simon tells his niece Tara that she can live in his cottage 'for as long as she wants.' Tara moves in and lives there for five years, making no improvements. Simon then asks Tara to leave and she refuses. Has Tara any proprietary right to remain?
Proprietary Estoppel.answer:
Tara has no proprietary right to remain on these facts. Simon's statement — 'for as long as she wants' — is a bare licence: a permission with no consideration and no proprietary right attached. A bare licence is revocable at will on reasonable notice. It does not create an easement, a lease, or any other proprietary interest. Tara has not spent money or acted to her detriment in reliance on any assurance — she merely moved in and lived there. Proprietary estoppel requires an assurance, reliance, and detriment. There is no detrimental reliance here — living in a property rent-free is a benefit, not a detriment. Simon may revoke the licence on giving reasonable notice and then recover possession. Tara has no defence.

⚠️ If Tara had made substantial improvements or given up other opportunities in reliance on a more specific assurance of long-term security, the answer might differ.
Proprietary Estoppel.summary_line: BARE LICENCE · NO CONSIDERATION · REVOCABLE AT WILL · NO DETRIMENT · NO ESTOPPEL · REASONABLE NOTICE

Proprietary Estoppel.front: Uma worked on her uncle Vijay's farm without pay for 15 years. Vijay repeatedly told her 'this will all be yours one day' and she gave up a well-paid job to work on the farm full-time in reliance on this. Vijay dies leaving the farm to his son Wen. Uma claims the farm on the basis of proprietary estoppel. Advise Uma.
Proprietary Estoppel.answer:
Uma has a strong proprietary estoppel claim. The three elements are:

1. Assurance — Vijay's repeated statements that the farm 'will all be yours one day' constitute a clear assurance of a future interest in the land. Under Thorner v Major [2009], assurances need not be express and formal — repeated conduct and statements in context can suffice.
2. Reliance — Uma gave up a well-paid job and worked without pay in reliance on the assurance.
3. Detriment — giving up a career and working without pay for 15 years is substantial detriment.

All three elements are established — the equity arises. Remedy: the court has a discretion to grant the minimum necessary to satisfy the equity (Jennings v Rice [2002]). Given the strength of the assurance and the substantial detriment, the court may order transfer of the farm or a significant monetary award. Wen, as beneficiary under the will, takes the farm subject to Uma's equity.
Proprietary Estoppel.summary_line: PROPRIETARY ESTOPPEL · ASSURANCE · DETRIMENTAL RELIANCE · THORNER V MAJOR · JENNINGS V RICE · TRANSFER OR COMPENSATION

Proprietary Estoppel.front: Xavier spent £40,000 renovating a cottage owned by his partner Yasmin, in reliance on her repeated assurances that she would leave it to him in her will. Yasmin later sells the cottage to Zara and moves abroad. Xavier seeks to enforce his equity against Zara. Advise Xavier.
Proprietary Estoppel.answer: Xavier has an equity arising from proprietary estoppel — assurance (Yasmin's repeated statements), reliance (renovation works), and detriment (£40,000 expenditure) are all established. The question is whether this equity binds Zara as a purchaser. In registered land: Xavier's equity should have been protected by entry of a notice on the register. If a notice was entered, it binds Zara. If no notice was entered: Xavier may still have an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002 if he was in actual occupation of the cottage at the time of the sale. If Xavier was living in the cottage, his occupation was likely obvious on inspection, and his interest overrides. If Xavier was not in occupation and no notice was entered, Zara as a registered disponee for valuable consideration takes free of Xavier's unprotected interest under s.29 LRA 2002. Xavier's remedy would then be against Yasmin personally for the value of his equity.
Proprietary Estoppel.summary_line: ESTOPPEL EQUITY · NOTICE ON REGISTER · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · SCH 3 PARA 2 · S.29 PURCHASER FREE · REMEDY AGAINST YASMIN

Proprietary Estoppel.front: Adam worked as a carer for his elderly neighbour Beth for eight years without payment. Beth told him on several occasions 'I want you to have this house — you deserve it.' Beth had capacity throughout. Beth dies leaving the house to her daughter Clara. Adam claims the house under proprietary estoppel. Clara argues the assurance was too vague to found an estoppel and Adam's remedy, if any, should be monetary. Advise.
Proprietary Estoppel.answer: Assurance: Beth's repeated statements that she 'wants' Adam to have the house are likely sufficient — Thorner v Major [2009] confirmed that assurances need not be formal. In the context of an ongoing caring relationship and repeated statements, the assurance is clear enough to be reasonably relied upon. Reliance and detriment: Adam worked as a carer for eight years without payment — this is substantial detriment in reliance on the assurance. The equity arises. Remedy: the court applies the proportionality principle from Jennings v Rice [2002] — the minimum necessary to satisfy the equity. Clara argues for a monetary remedy. The court will balance: (a) the expectation (the house — significant); (b) the detriment (eight years' unpaid care — very substantial). Where expectation and detriment are roughly proportionate, the court may award the full expectation. Eight years of full-time unpaid care is substantial enough that transfer of the house, or at minimum a very significant monetary award, is likely. Clara's argument for a merely nominal payment is unlikely to succeed given the scale of Adam's detriment.
Proprietary Estoppel.summary_line: PROPRIETARY ESTOPPEL · REPEATED ASSURANCE · LONG-TERM CARE · PROPORTIONALITY · JENNINGS V RICE · TRANSFER LIKELY

Proprietary Estoppel.front: Two friends, Dan and Eve, sign separate but identical agreements with a landlord Frank to occupy a two-bedroom flat, each described as a 'licence'. Each agreement states Frank may introduce other occupiers at any time. Dan and Eve are in a relationship and chose the flat together. Frank never exercised the right to introduce others. Dan and Eve claim they have a joint tenancy. Advise.
Proprietary Estoppel.answer: This is directly analogous to Antoniades v Villiers [1990]. The court will look at the substance of the arrangement, not the label. Key factors: Dan and Eve chose the flat together as a couple; the 'licences' were interdependent — neither would have signed without the other; Frank never exercised or intended to exercise the right to introduce other occupiers (it was a sham device to avoid a tenancy). Together the agreements grant Dan and Eve exclusive possession of the whole flat for a term at a rent. The hallmarks of a lease (Street v Mountford [1985]) are all present. The agreements are a sham — the court disregards the artificial structure and finds a joint tenancy. Dan and Eve have a joint tenancy lease with the statutory protections that follow.
Proprietary Estoppel.summary_line: ANTONIADES V VILLIERS · SHAM LICENCE · JOINT TENANCY · EXCLUSIVE POSSESSION · STREET V MOUNTFORD · SUBSTANCE OVER FORM

Proprietary Estoppel.front: Gina grants Harry a 15-year lease of a commercial unit. The lease was granted in 2010. Harry assigns the lease to Ivan in 2015 without Gina's consent, in breach of the alienation covenant. Gina discovers the assignment in 2016 and continues to accept quarterly rent from Ivan without comment. In 2024, Gina serves a s.146 notice and seeks to forfeit for the 2015 breach. Advise Gina.
Proprietary Estoppel.answer:
Gina cannot forfeit for the 2015 unauthorised assignment. Waiver occurred when Gina accepted rent from Ivan in 2016 with knowledge of the breach. Accepting rent is an unequivocal act recognising the continued existence of the lease — waiver is automatic regardless of intention: Central Estates v Woolgar (No 2) [1972]. The unauthorised assignment is a once-and-for-all breach — waiver permanently destroys the right to forfeit for that specific breach. Gina has waived it. Eight years of accepting rent without objection reinforces this. The s.146 notice and forfeiture attempt in 2024 are ineffective for the 2015 breach.

⚠️ Gina may still have other grounds for forfeiture if Ivan has committed further breaches — but she cannot revive the 2015 breach. Gina should have acted immediately on discovering the assignment in 2016.
Proprietary Estoppel.summary_line: WAIVER · ONCE-AND-FOR-ALL BREACH · 8 YEARS RENT ACCEPTED · PERMANENT WAIVER · CANNOT FORFEIT · ACT IMMEDIATELY

Proprietary Estoppel.front: Jane and Karl own a house in joint names as joint tenants in law and equity. Karl sends a text message to Jane saying 'I want to sever our joint tenancy — I want my 50% share.' Jane acknowledges receipt but takes no action. Has the joint tenancy been severed?
Proprietary Estoppel.answer:
The joint tenancy has not been effectively severed. Under s.196(4) LPA 1925, severance by notice requires a written notice — but a text message is unlikely to satisfy the writing requirement for a legally effective notice of severance, depending on jurisdiction and case law development. More fundamentally, the method of service matters — s.196(3) requires the notice to be left at the last known place of abode or business, or delivered personally. A text message does not satisfy this. However, the parties' subsequent conduct (Jane's acknowledgement, Karl's clear statement) may evidence a mutual agreement to sever — severance by mutual agreement is a recognised method not requiring s.196 formalities. On balance, the safest position is that a formal written notice should be served at Jane's address. Karl should serve a written notice of severance by post or personal delivery to Jane's address to ensure effective severance.

⚠️ If Karl dies before proper severance, survivorship would pass the property to Jane.
Proprietary Estoppel.summary_line: SEVERANCE · TEXT MESSAGE INSUFFICIENT · S.196 LPA 1925 · FORMAL WRITTEN NOTICE · MUTUAL AGREEMENT POSSIBLE · SURVIVORSHIP RISK

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: Should proprietary estoppel remedies be expectation-based or detriment-based, and has English law chosen the right approach?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
The central debate in proprietary estoppel remedies is whether the court should aim to fulfil the claimant's expectation (giving them what they were promised) or merely to compensate for the detriment suffered (restoring them to their pre-reliance position). English law, following Jennings v Rice [2002], takes a middle position: the remedy is the minimum necessary to satisfy the equity, with the court balancing expectation and detriment and applying a principle of proportionality.

Ben McFarlane and Andrew Robertson ('Apocalypse Averted: Proprietary Estoppel in the House of Lords' (2009) 125 LQR 535) have argued that the flexible approach in Jennings v Rice, while producing just outcomes on the facts, creates unpredictability — parties cannot know in advance what remedy a court will award. They prefer a more rule-based approach, with expectation as the default subject to clear limiting principles.

John Mee ('Proprietary Estoppel, Promises and Mistaken Belief' in Bright (ed), Modern Studies in Property Law (2011)) has argued for a detriment-based approach — the purpose of estoppel is to prevent unconscionable conduct, and the unconscionability is measured by the detriment, not the expectation. Awarding the full expectation where it is disproportionate to the detriment is itself difficult to justify on principle.

The Court of Appeal in Suggitt v Suggitt [2012] awarded the full expectation (a farm) despite significant disproportion with the detriment, suggesting the balance can tilt strongly toward expectation where the assurance was clear and unconditional.

The unresolved tension: English law's flexible, discretionary approach to estoppel remedies achieves just results in individual cases but sacrifices predictability. Whether this is preferable to a more rule-based system remains an open question in property law scholarship.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: PROPRIETARY ESTOPPEL REMEDY · EXPECTATION VS DETRIMENT · JENNINGS V RICE · MCFARLANE AND ROBERTSON · SUGGITT · PREDICTABILITY

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Will and Xena have lived together for eight years in a house registered solely in Will's name. Xena contributed nothing to the purchase price or mortgage payments but managed the household and cared for their children throughout. They separate and Xena claims a 50% beneficial share, relying on Stack v Dowden [2007] and arguing that the presumption of equal shares applies to their relationship.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: Stack v Dowden is the leading case on beneficial shares between cohabitees — students apply the equal shares presumption and conclude Xena has a 50% share.
Trap Cards.rule:
The Reality: The Stack v Dowden presumption of equal beneficial shares applies only where the property is registered in joint names. Where the property is in one name only, the starting point is that the sole registered proprietor holds the entire beneficial interest. The non-legal owner (Xena) must establish a common intention constructive trust by showing:

1. a common intention that she should have a beneficial interest; and
2. detrimental reliance on that intention.

There is no presumption of equal shares in sole name cases.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the Stack v Dowden reference and the long domestic relationship. Students apply the joint names presumption to a sole name case without checking the fundamental threshold question — whose name is the property in? Stack v Dowden itself makes clear the starting point differs depending on whether the property is in joint or sole names. Xena's household contributions and childcare may support a CICT claim, but she must affirmatively prove it — there is no presumption in her favour.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Stack v Dowden's equal shares presumption applies only to joint names cases. In sole name cases, the non-owner must establish a common intention constructive trust from scratch — there is no presumption of any share, let alone an equal one. Always check whose name the property is in before applying Stack.
Trap Cards.summary_line: STACK V DOWDEN · JOINT NAMES ONLY · SOLE NAME DIFFERENT · NO PRESUMPTION · CICT MUST BE ESTABLISHED · STARTING POINT

Actual Occupation and Overriding Interes.issue: Whether a wife's beneficial interest under a trust, coupled with her actual occupation of the matrimonial home, constituted an overriding interest binding a mortgagee bank.
Actual Occupation and Overriding Interes.rule: A person in actual occupation of registered land with a proprietary interest has an overriding interest under what is now Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002 — actual occupation requires physical presence, not merely a legal entitlement to occupy.
Actual Occupation and Overriding Interes.application: Mr Boland mortgaged the matrimonial home to the bank without his wife's knowledge. Mrs Boland had contributed to the purchase price and was in actual occupation. The bank sought possession. The House of Lords held Mrs Boland's beneficial interest, coupled with her actual occupation, overrode the bank's mortgage.
Actual Occupation and Overriding Interes.conclusion: The bank took subject to Mrs Boland's overriding interest. The case confirmed that actual occupation by a beneficiary under a trust can override a registered disposition — including a mortgage. It led directly to the practice of requiring all occupiers to sign mortgage deeds or consent forms.
Actual Occupation and Overriding Interes.summary_line: ACTUAL OCCUPATION · OVERRIDING INTEREST · BENEFICIAL INTEREST · REGISTERED LAND · MORTGAGEE BOUND · BOLAND

Overreaching.front: City of London Building Society v Flegg [1988]
Overreaching.issue: Whether beneficiaries in actual occupation retained an overriding interest binding a mortgagee where the mortgage had been granted by two trustees and the capital money paid to them.
Overreaching.rule: Where overreaching operates — capital money paid to at least two trustees or a trust corporation — the beneficial interests are detached from the land and shifted to the proceeds, defeating even an overriding interest of a person in actual occupation.
Overreaching.application: The Fleggs contributed to the purchase of a property registered in the names of their daughter and son-in-law. The couple then mortgaged the property to the building society, receiving the capital money themselves. The Fleggs were in actual occupation. The House of Lords held overreaching had operated — capital money was paid to two trustees — and the Fleggs' interests were overreached.
Overreaching.conclusion: The building society took free of the Fleggs' interests despite their actual occupation. Overreaching, when properly effected, defeats even Sch 3 overriding interests. The case establishes the primacy of overreaching over actual occupation where the two-trustee requirement is met.
Overreaching.summary_line: OVERREACHING · TWO TRUSTEES · ACTUAL OCCUPATION DEFEATED · CAPITAL MONEY · REGISTERED LAND · FLEGG

Land Charges — Unregistered Land.front: Midland Bank Trust Co v Green [1981]
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.issue: Whether an unregistered estate contract (option to purchase) was void against a purchaser for money or money's worth of a legal estate, even where the purchaser had actual notice of it.
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.rule: An unregistered land charge is void against a purchaser for money or money's worth of a legal estate (s.4(6) Land Charges Act 1972) — actual notice is irrelevant. The registration system replaces the doctrine of notice for registrable interests.
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.application: A father granted his son an option to purchase a farm. The option was an estate contract (Class C(iv) land charge) but was not registered. The father later sold the farm to his wife for a nominal sum (£500 against a true value of £40,000) specifically to defeat the option. The House of Lords held the option was void against the wife as purchaser — the motive for the sale was irrelevant.
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.conclusion: The son's option was void. The decision confirms the strict operation of the land charges registration system — actual notice cannot substitute for registration, and the purchaser's motive is irrelevant. Widely criticised as harsh but upheld as a matter of statutory construction.
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.summary_line: LAND CHARGES · ESTATE CONTRACT · ACTUAL NOTICE IRRELEVANT · VOID AGAINST PURCHASER · UNREGISTERED LAND · GREEN

Land Charges — Unregistered Land.front: Oak Co-operative Building Society v Blackburn [1968]
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.issue: Whether registration of a land charge against an incorrect version of the estate owner's name constituted valid registration against a purchaser who searched under the correct name.
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.rule: Registration of a land charge must be against the correct full name of the estate owner. Registration against an incorrect or variant name is not effective against a purchaser who searches under the correct name and receives a clear certificate.
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.application: A land charge was registered against 'Frank David Blackburn' — a common variant of the estate owner's full name. The building society searched against the correct full name and received a clear certificate. The court held the registration was ineffective against the building society.
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.conclusion: The building society took free of the charge. The case illustrates the rigidity of the land charges name-based system — a charge 'registered' under a variant name provides no protection against a purchaser who searches correctly. The result can be harsh but reflects the system's need for certainty.
Land Charges — Unregistered Land.summary_line: LAND CHARGES · INCORRECT NAME · VOID REGISTRATION · CLEAR CERTIFICATE · UNREGISTERED LAND · BLACKBURN

Doctrine of Notice — Unregistered Land.issue: Whether a purchaser of unregistered land was fixed with constructive notice of a tenant's rights where the tenant was in actual occupation.
Doctrine of Notice — Unregistered Land.rule: In unregistered land, a purchaser has constructive notice of the rights of persons in actual occupation — a purchaser who fails to inspect the property and make reasonable enquiries of occupiers is fixed with notice of whatever those enquiries would have revealed.
Doctrine of Notice — Unregistered Land.application: A purchaser bought unregistered land without inspecting it. Tenants were in actual occupation paying rent to an agent. The Court of Appeal held the purchaser had constructive notice of the tenants' rights — inspection would have revealed the occupation and enquiry would have revealed the tenancies.
Doctrine of Notice — Unregistered Land.conclusion: The purchaser took subject to the tenancies. Hunt v Luck is the leading authority on constructive notice through actual occupation in unregistered land — a purchaser who fails to inspect is treated as knowing what inspection and reasonable enquiry would have revealed.
Doctrine of Notice — Unregistered Land.summary_line: CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · UNREGISTERED LAND · INSPECTION · REASONABLE ENQUIRIES · HUNT V LUCK

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: Ahmed owns a freehold property in fee simple absolute in possession. He grants his daughter Fatima the right to live in the property for her lifetime, with the property passing to his son Tariq on Fatima's death. Fatima asks whether she holds a legal estate in the property. Advise Fatima.
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer: Fatima does not hold a legal estate. Under s.1(1) LPA 1925, only two legal estates exist: the fee simple absolute in possession and the term of years absolute. A life interest is neither — it is not a fee simple (it is not of indefinite duration) and not a term of years (it has no fixed maximum). Since 1925, life interests can only exist as equitable interests behind a trust. Fatima holds an equitable life interest under a trust of land, with Ahmed (or trustees) holding the legal estate on trust for her. Tariq holds an equitable remainder. Neither Fatima nor Tariq holds a legal estate.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: LIFE INTEREST · NOT LEGAL ESTATE · EQUITABLE ONLY · TRUST OF LAND · S.1(1) LPA 1925 · REMAINDER

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: Priya purports to transfer her freehold property to her nephew Ravi by signing a document headed 'Transfer of Property' and handing it to him. The document is not witnessed. Ravi pays no money. Has legal title passed to Ravi?
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer: Legal title has not passed. Under s.52(1) LPA 1925, a legal estate must be created or transferred by deed. A deed requires: writing, a clear indication it is a deed, signature by the grantor, and attestation by a witness present at signing (s.1 LP(MP)A 1989). The document here was not witnessed — it is therefore not a deed and cannot transfer a legal estate. Additionally, for registered land, legal title does not pass until registration at HMLR (s.27 LRA 2002) — even a valid deed only operates in equity until registration. The transfer is ineffective at law. Ravi may have an equitable interest if a court would order specific performance, but as he paid nothing (a volunteer), specific performance is unlikely.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: FORMALITIES · DEED · WITNESS REQUIRED · S.52 LPA 1925 · REGISTRATION · VOLUNTEER · SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: Chloe grants her neighbour Dan a right to use a path across her garden to reach his workshop. The right is granted by a properly executed deed but neither party registers it at HMLR. Dan later sells his workshop to Ellie. Chloe then sells her garden to Frank, who searches the register and finds no mention of the right of way. Is Frank bound by the easement?
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer:
The easement was expressly granted by deed but not registered as a registrable disposition under s.27(2) LRA 2002. Until registration, it operates as an equitable easement only. Frank, as a registered disponee for valuable consideration, takes free of interests not protected by a notice on the register and not qualifying as overriding interests (s.29 LRA 2002). The key question is whether the easement qualifies as an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 3 LRA 2002 — a legal easement may override if it was known to Frank, would have been obvious on a reasonably careful inspection, or had been exercised in the year before the disposition. As an unregistered express grant, it is equitable, not legal — equitable easements do not qualify under Sch 3, para 3. Frank takes free of the easement.

⚠️ Dan and Ellie should have ensured registration immediately on grant.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: UNREGISTERED EASEMENT · EQUITABLE ONLY · S.27 LRA 2002 · S.29 DISPONEE FREE · SCH 3 PARA 3 · REGISTER IMMEDIATELY

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: A property is registered in the names of Marco and Nina as trustees. They hold it on trust for themselves and their adult son Paolo, who contributed to the purchase price. Marco and Nina sell the property to Quentin and receive the purchase price themselves, paying it into a joint account. Paolo was living in the property at the time of sale. Quentin now seeks vacant possession. Advise Paolo.
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer: The key question is whether overreaching has operated. Overreaching requires capital money to be paid to at least two trustees or a trust corporation (s.2 and s.27 LPA 1925). Here, capital money was paid to Marco and Nina — two trustees. Overreaching has therefore operated successfully. Paolo's beneficial interest has shifted from the land to the proceeds of sale in the joint account, regardless of his actual occupation. Following City of London Building Society v Flegg [1988], even a beneficiary in actual occupation is overreached where capital money is paid to two trustees. Quentin takes the property free of Paolo's interest. Paolo's remedy is against Marco and Nina for the proceeds — not against Quentin.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: OVERREACHING · TWO TRUSTEES · ACTUAL OCCUPATION DEFEATED · FLEGG · PROCEEDS OF SALE · REMEDY AGAINST TRUSTEES

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: Rosa holds a beneficial interest in a property registered solely in her partner Stefan's name. Stefan mortgages the property to a bank without Rosa's knowledge. Rosa is in actual occupation at the time the mortgage is granted. The bank did not inspect the property or make enquiries of Rosa. Stefan defaults and the bank seeks possession. Can Rosa's interest override the bank's mortgage?
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer:
Rosa's beneficial interest may override the bank's mortgage as an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002, if:

1. Rosa had an interest in the property at the time of the disposition (the mortgage)
2. she was in actual occupation at that time; and
3. her occupation would have been obvious on a reasonably careful inspection, or the bank had actual knowledge of it.

Rosa was in actual occupation — Williams & Glyn's Bank v Boland [1981] confirms that actual physical occupation by a beneficiary satisfies this. The bank failed to inspect, so it cannot argue Rosa's occupation was not obvious. Rosa's interest is not excluded by enquiry (she was not asked and so did not fail to disclose). Rosa's overriding interest binds the bank. The bank cannot obtain possession without satisfying Rosa's interest.

⚠️ The bank should have inspected and required Rosa to consent to or postpone her interest before the mortgage was granted.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: ACTUAL OCCUPATION · OVERRIDING INTEREST · BOLAND · INSPECTABILITY · BANK DID NOT INSPECT · MORTGAGEE BOUND

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: Tom purchases unregistered land from Uma. Before completion, Tom's solicitor searches the land charges register against Uma's name and receives a clear certificate. After completion, Tom discovers that Uma's former business partner Victor had registered an estate contract against Uma, but under the name 'U. Blackwell' rather than Uma's full name 'Uma Blackwell'. Victor claims his interest binds Tom. Advise Tom.
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer: Tom is not bound by Victor's estate contract. Under the Land Charges Act 1972, a land charge must be registered against the correct full name of the estate owner. Registration against an incorrect or variant name ('U. Blackwell' rather than 'Uma Blackwell') is not effective registration against a purchaser who searches under the correct name: Oak Co-operative Building Society v Blackburn [1968]. Tom's solicitor searched against the correct name — Uma Blackwell — and received a clear certificate. The certificate is conclusive in Tom's favour. The estate contract is void against Tom as a purchaser for money or money's worth of a legal estate (s.4(6) LCA 1972), even though the charge was nominally 'registered'. Victor's remedy (if any) is against the person who registered incorrectly.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: LAND CHARGES · WRONG NAME · CLEAR CERTIFICATE · OAK CO-OPERATIVE V BLACKBURN · VOID AGAINST PURCHASER

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: Wendy purchases registered land from Xavier. At the time of completion, Xavier's sister Yolanda is staying at the property temporarily while her own flat is being renovated. Yolanda has a beneficial interest under a trust arising from her contribution to the original purchase price. Wendy did not inspect the property before completion. Is Wendy bound by Yolanda's interest?
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer:
Yolanda's beneficial interest may be an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002 if she was in actual occupation at the time of the disposition. Actual occupation requires physical presence — Yolanda was staying at the property, which is likely to constitute actual occupation. However, Sch 3, para 2 requires that the occupation would have been obvious on a reasonably careful inspection. Wendy did not inspect — so she cannot argue the occupation was not obvious (had she inspected, Yolanda's presence would have been apparent). Yolanda was not asked about her interest and so did not fail to disclose. On these facts, Yolanda's interest is likely to override and bind Wendy.

⚠️ The temporary nature of the occupation may be relevant — courts require a degree of permanence: Abbey National v Cann [1991] — but a stay of several weeks during renovation is likely sufficient.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: ACTUAL OCCUPATION · OVERRIDING INTEREST · TEMPORARY OCCUPATION · INSPECTABILITY · SCH 3 PARA 2 · ABBEY NATIONAL

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: Zara holds registered land on trust for herself and her adult daughter Bella. Zara is the sole registered proprietor. Zara sells the property to Carl, who pays the purchase price to Zara alone. Bella was not in occupation at the time of sale — she had moved out six months earlier. Carl now claims the property free of Bella's interest. Advise Bella.
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer: Bella's position depends on two issues: overreaching and overriding interests. Overreaching: capital money was paid to Zara alone — a sole trustee. Overreaching requires payment to at least two trustees or a trust corporation (s.27 LPA 1925). Overreaching has therefore failed. Bella's beneficial interest remains attached to the land. Overriding interest: since overreaching has failed, Bella's interest could still bind Carl if it qualifies as an overriding interest under Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002. This requires Bella to have been in actual occupation at the time of the disposition. Bella had moved out six months before the sale — she was not in actual occupation at the relevant time. Her interest is not an overriding interest. Protection by notice: Bella had not protected her interest by notice on the register. Under s.29 LRA 2002, Carl as a registered disponee for valuable consideration takes free of Bella's unprotected, non-overriding interest. Carl takes the property free of Bella's interest. Bella's remedy is against Zara personally.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: OVERREACHING FAILS · SOLE TRUSTEE · NOT IN OCCUPATION · S.29 FREE · UNPROTECTED INTEREST · REMEDY AGAINST TRUSTEE

Registration and Protection of Interests.front: Derek purchased unregistered land in 1990. He had actual knowledge at the time of purchase that his vendor's neighbour Edward held an option to purchase the land, granted in 1985 and registered as a Class C(iv) land charge. Derek now seeks to build on the land, and Edward seeks to exercise the option. Derek argues the option does not bind him as he paid full market value. Advise Edward.
Registration and Protection of Interests.answer:
Edward's option is a Class C(iv) land charge (estate contract) under the Land Charges Act 1972. It was registered in 1985 — and registration is the only means of protecting such an interest in unregistered land. The question is whether the registered option binds Derek. The charge was properly registered against the correct name of the estate owner. Derek purchased with actual knowledge of the option. However, in unregistered land, actual notice is irrelevant for land charges — what matters is whether the charge was registered: Midland Bank Trust Co v Green [1981]. Here the charge was registered — so it binds Derek regardless of any other consideration. Derek is bound by Edward's option to purchase. Edward may exercise it.

⚠️

**Note:** the harsh result in Green (where actual notice did not save an unregistered charge) is the converse of this scenario — here, registration protects the interest even against a purchaser who claims ignorance would have been preferable.
Registration and Protection of Interests.summary_line: LAND CHARGES REGISTERED · BINDS ALL · GREEN CONTRASTED · ACTUAL NOTICE IRRELEVANT · ESTATE CONTRACT · PURCHASER BOUND

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: Why has the Law Commission's recommendation to reform the law on positive covenants never been implemented, and what was commonhold intended to achieve?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
The rule that the burden of a positive covenant does not run with freehold land (Austerberry v Oldham (1885); Rhone v Stephens [1994]) has been criticised for over a century. The Law Commission has recommended reform on multiple occasions — most recently in its 2011 report 'Making Land Work: Easements, Covenants and Profits à Prendre' (Law Com No 327), which proposed the introduction of a new statutory 'land obligation' that would allow both positive and negative obligations to bind successors. The proposal has not been implemented.

Commonhold, introduced by the Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Act 2002, was intended as a partial solution for developments where positive obligations between unit holders are essential (e.g. maintaining shared structures). In commonhold, unit owners hold freehold titles within a commonhold association — positive obligations are enforceable between unit holders as a matter of company law rather than land law. In practice, commonhold has been almost entirely unused — developers and lenders have shown little appetite for it, preferring the familiar leasehold structure despite its acknowledged defects.

The academic literature is largely critical of the current position. Susan Bright ('Avoiding Tenancy Legislation: Sham and Contrived Transactions' (2002)) and others have noted that the indirect methods available to enforce positive covenants (indemnity chains, benefit and burden, leasehold structures) are cumbersome and unreliable. The fundamental problem — that freehold land cannot bear positive running obligations — remains unresolved.

The unresolved tension: English freehold land law is uniquely hostile to positive obligations compared with civil law systems, where running positive obligations are routine. The Law Commission's inability to secure parliamentary time for reform reflects the political difficulty of disturbing established property interests, not the weakness of the case for change.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: POSITIVE COVENANTS · LAW COMMISSION · COMMONHOLD · RHONE V STEPHENS · LAND OBLIGATION · REFORM UNIMPLEMENTED · 2011 REPORT

Deeper Understanding.front: Going deeper: Is the benefit and burden principle from Halsall v Brizell a coherent part of English property law or a problematic anomaly?
Deeper Understanding.answer:
The benefit and burden principle — that a person who takes the benefit of a deed must accept the corresponding burdens — was applied in Halsall v Brizell [1957] to make a positive covenant indirectly enforceable against a successor. It represents the most significant exception to the rule that positive covenant burdens do not run at law.

Susan Bright ('Of Estates and Interests: A Tale of Ownership and Property Rights' in Bright & Dewar (eds), Land Law: Themes and Perspectives (1998)) has argued that the principle is incoherent. The fundamental objection is that it allows parties to circumvent the rule in Rhone v Stephens by packaging a positive obligation with a benefit — effectively permitting the indirect running of positive burdens through creative drafting. The principle rewards sophisticated conveyancing and penalises those who did not structure their transaction carefully.

Thamesmead Town Ltd v Allotey [1998] attempted to cabin the principle by requiring a direct and genuine correlation between the benefit taken and the burden imposed — and a genuine choice to take or reject the benefit. Peter Clarke ('The Benefit and Burden Principle' (1999) Conv 245) argued that Thamesmead's refinements make the principle more defensible but also more unpredictable — courts must assess the directness of the benefit-burden link on a case-by-case basis.

A further objection is that the principle produces an all-or-nothing result: either the successor takes the benefit and is bound by the burden, or they must relinquish the benefit entirely. Where the benefit is practically essential (e.g. a right of way), the 'choice' to reject it is illusory.

The unresolved tension: Halsall v Brizell and its progeny represent an ad hoc solution to a systematic problem. Until Parliament implements the Law Commission's recommended land obligations scheme, the benefit and burden principle will remain an imperfect workaround.
Deeper Understanding.summary_line: BENEFIT AND BURDEN · HALSALL V BRIZELL · THAMESMEAD · BRIGHT · PETER CLARKE · ILLUSORY CHOICE · LAW COMMISSION · AD HOC

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Anja and her brother Ben hold a property on trust for themselves and their adult sister Clara. The property is registered in Anja and Ben's names as trustees. Clara has lived in the property for three years and is in actual occupation at the time of sale. Anja and Ben sell the property to David, paying the proceeds into their joint account. David has searched the register and found no notice protecting Clara's interest. Clara argues her actual occupation gives her an overriding interest binding David.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: Clara is in actual occupation and has a beneficial interest — the scenario is engineered to make Sch 3, para 2 LRA 2002 feel like the answer. Most students conclude Clara's overriding interest defeats David.
Trap Cards.rule: The Reality: Overreaching defeats even an overriding interest of a person in actual occupation, provided capital money is paid to at least two trustees or a trust corporation (s.2 and s.27 LPA 1925; City of London Building Society v Flegg [1988]). Clara's interest has been overreached — it shifted from the land to the proceeds in Anja and Ben's joint account. David takes free of Clara's interest.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is Clara's actual occupation. Actual occupation matters under Sch 3, para 2 only where overreaching has not operated. Here, capital money was paid to two trustees — the precise condition for overreaching. Flegg directly decided this point: the House of Lords held that a beneficiary in actual occupation is overreached when the two-trustee requirement is met. The student who jumps to Sch 3, para 2 without first asking whether overreaching has operated will always get this wrong.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Always ask whether overreaching has operated before analysing actual occupation overriding interests. If capital money was paid to two or more trustees, overreaching defeats the beneficial interest regardless of actual occupation.
Trap Cards.summary_line: OVERREACHING · TWO TRUSTEES · DEFEATS ACTUAL OCCUPATION · FLEGG · CHECK OVERREACHING FIRST

Trap Cards.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Ella mortgages her house to First Bank by deed. She falls behind on payments and misses one monthly instalment. First Bank immediately instructs estate agents to sell the house, which they do within three weeks. Ella claims the sale was unlawful because the power of sale had not become exercisable. First Bank argues the power of sale arose when the mortgage was executed and they were entitled to sell. The purchaser, Fred, is concerned about the validity of his title.
Trap Cards.issue: The Trap: The mortgage was created by deed and the mortgage money is due — the power of sale has arisen under s.101(1)(i) LPA 1925. The scenario makes it tempting to conclude the bank was entitled to sell and Fred has good title with nothing further to consider.
Trap Cards.rule:
The Reality: The power of sale arising and the power of sale becoming exercisable are two distinct things. The power arises under s.101 but only becomes exercisable under s.103 LPA 1925 when one of three conditions is met:

1. written notice requiring payment served and three months' default
2. interest two months in arrear
3. breach of another mortgage covenant.

One missed monthly instalment satisfies none of these conditions. First Bank exercised the power before it was exercisable — it is liable to Ella in damages.
Trap Cards.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the word 'arose' — students conflate arising with exercisability. The s.101/s.103 distinction is the entire point of the trap. However — and this is the second part students miss — Fred as purchaser takes good title regardless of the bank's breach. Section 104 LPA 1925 protects a purchaser from a mortgagee: they are not concerned to enquire whether the power has become exercisable. Fred's title is valid. The bank's liability is to Ella, not Fred.
Trap Cards.conclusion: The Key Rule: Power of sale arising (s.101) ≠ power of sale exercisable (s.103). A mortgagee who sells before the power is exercisable is liable to the mortgagor in damages — but the purchaser still gets good title under s.104.
Trap Cards.summary_line: POWER OF SALE · ARISING VS EXERCISABLE · S.101 VS S.103 · PURCHASER PROTECTED S.104 · MORTGAGEE LIABLE
